event_handlers: onClickInternalLink: handle clicks on any internal links within the article body that navigate to other pages using URL parameters (e.g., other articles, tarot spreads).

components.functionality: Provides primary actions for the current article: Save to Reading List, Add to Favorites, and Save to Collection (navigation-only from this page). Shows inline feedback after each action and exposes navigation to the Reading List and Favorites pages.
event_handlers: onClickSaveToReadingList: calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId = Article.id, source based on context, such as 'other' or a value indicating the originating page). On success, displays the response.message and reveals a 'Go to Reading List' link that navigates to reading_list.html.

onClickFavoriteArticle: calls favoriteArticle(articleId = Article.id, source = 'article_detail'). On success, displays the response.message and reveals a 'View Favorites' link that navigates to favorites.html.

onClickSaveToCollection: triggers navigation to collections.html, optionally with a URL parameter (e.g., addArticleId = Article.id) that allows the Collections page to know which article the user intends to save. The Article Detail page does not call any collection-specific interfaces.
onFeedbackDismiss: hides success or error messages related to save/favorite actions.

components.functionality: Conditionally renders a Plan This Ritual / Add to Planner button only when Article.article_type indicates a ritual_guide. Opens the ritual planner form to create a scheduled ritual entry.
event_handlers: onRender: check Article.article_type; only render the CTA when it equals 'ritual_guide'.
onClickOpenPlannerForm: opens the ritual-planner-form-modal component, seeding it with Article.id and Article.title.

components.functionality: Provides a form for scheduling a ritual based on the current ritual guide article. Collects scheduledStart, scheduledEnd (optional), note (optional), and status, and calls createRitualPlannerEntry to create the planner entry.
event_handlers: onOpen: initialize form fields (for example, default status to a planned state and pre-fill the title if shown).
onChangeField: update local form state for scheduledStart, scheduledEnd, note, and status.

onSubmitPlannerForm: validate required fields (at minimum scheduledStart), then call createRitualPlannerEntry(articleId = Article.id, moonEventId from the form if provided or omitted, scheduledStart, scheduledEnd, note, status). On success, capture the returned RitualPlannerEntry for subsequent navigation.
onPlannerEntryCreated: display a confirmation message and show an 'Open in Planner' button using the created RitualPlannerEntry.scheduled_start to determine the month and year.

onClickOpenInPlanner: navigate to planner.html with URL parameters view = 'month', month = month extracted from RitualPlannerEntry.scheduled_start, and year = year extracted from RitualPlannerEntry.scheduled_start.
onClose: close the modal without submitting and reset form state as needed.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related articles for the current article, with sorting and optional minimum rating filtering. Each related article item shows metadata and provides an inline Save to Reading List button.
data_binding: Article.id (as the base articleId for fetching related articles)
event_handlers: onInit: read relatedSort and relatedMinRating from the URL (if present) and call getRelatedArticlesForArticle(articleId = Article.id, sortBy = relatedSort, minRating = relatedMinRating).

onChangeSort: when the user selects a new sort option (for example, a value representing 'Rating: High to Low'), update the relatedSort parameter in the URL and re-call getRelatedArticlesForArticle with the new sortBy.
onChangeMinRatingFilter: when the user adjusts a rating filter, update relatedMinRating in the URL and re-fetch related articles with that minRating.
onClickRelatedArticleTitle: navigate to article.html?id=<relatedArticle.id>. This triggers the article-data-loader to fetch the new article context.

onClickSaveRelatedToReadingList: for a related article item, call saveArticleToReadingList(articleId = relatedArticle.id, source = 'related_articles'); on success, display an inline confirmation using the response.message and optionally a 'Go to Reading List' link to reading_list.html.
onReloadOnArticleChange: when the main article changes (due to navigation), re-read URL state and re-call getRelatedArticlesForArticle with the new Article.id.

components.functionality: Loads and displays comments for the current article, shows the comment count, allows sorting, and provides a form for posting new comments.
event_handlers: onInit: read commentsSort from the URL (if present) and call getArticleComments(articleId = Article.id, sortBy = commentsSort).
onChangeCommentsSort: when the user selects a different sort option, update commentsSort in the URL and re-call getArticleComments with the new sortBy value.

onSubmitCommentForm: validate that both authorName and content are present, then call postComment(articleId = Article.id, authorName, content). On success, insert the returned comment into the in-memory list according to the active sort, update the displayed comment count using new_comment_count, clear the form, and show a success message.
onReloadOnArticleChange: when the main article changes (due to navigation to another id), re-read commentsSort from the URL and re-call getArticleComments for the new Article.id.
onErrorPostComment: display an error message if postComment fails.
onErrorLoadComments: display an error message if getArticleComments fails and offer a retry that re-calls getArticleComments with the current parameters.

components.functionality: Dedicated subcomponent of the comments section responsible for collecting the user's name and comment text and delegating submission to postComment.
event_handlers: onChangeAuthorName: update local state for the authorName input.
onChangeCommentContent: update local state for the comment content textarea.
onSubmit: call the parent comments-section's onSubmitCommentForm handler with the current input values.

components.functionality: Centralized area for rendering transient success and error messages originating from actions such as saving to the reading list, favoriting, posting a comment, or creating a ritual planner entry.
event_handlers: onShowMessage: receives message content and type (success or error) from other components (article-actions-bar, comments-section, ritual-planner-form-modal, related-articles-section) and renders it.
onDismissMessage: clears the current message when the user closes the notification or after a timeout.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: All components live inside a single centered content column that sits on a full-width lavender background. Above these components (handled by the overall template, not this list) are the watercolor illustration header, the primary navigation pill, and the breadcrumb pill, stacked vertically. Directly below the breadcrumb, the article content is presented as one large rounded article card that internally stacks the article-header at the top, followed by the article-actions-bar, then the article-body, with the ritual-planner-cta sitting near the end of the article content if applicable. Beneath this main article card, in the same centered column, come separate rounded cards for related-articles-section, then comments-section with the comment-form integrated at its top. The notification-area appears as a pill-like banner near the top of the content column when needed. The ritual-planner-form-modal floats above all of this as a full-screen overlay when triggered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components live inside a single centered content column that sits on a full-width lavender background. Above these components (handled by the overall template, not this list) are the watercolor illustration header, the primary navigation pill, and the breadcrumb pill, stacked vertically. Directly below the breadcrumb, the article content is presented as one large rounded article card that internally stacks the article-header at the top, followed by the article-actions-bar, then the article-body, with the ritual-planner-cta sitting near the end of the article content if applicable. Beneath this main article card, in the same centered column, come separate rounded cards for related-articles-section, then comments-section with the comment-form integrated at its top. The notification-area appears as a pill-like banner near the top of the content column when needed. The ritual-planner-form-modal floats above all of this as a full-screen overlay when triggered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The watercolor illustration header at the top of the page occupies roughly the upper 25–30% of the initial viewport height. The navigation pill and breadcrumb pill underneath each occupy around 8–10% of the viewport height with generous lavender space between them. The main article card (containing article-header, actions, body, and CTA) fills most of the remaining vertical space in the initial viewport and continues below the fold as needed. Within the article card, 16–24px padding is applied on all sides, with slightly more vertical padding around the title and CTA. The related-articles-section and comments-section each form their own cards below the main article, separated from each other and from the article card by roughly 24px of vertical margin. The notification-area uses a compact vertical height but spans nearly the full width of the content column, and the modal takes over the full viewport when open.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate. Large elements (navigation, breadcrumb, article card, related and comments cards) are clearly separated by 16–24px of lavender background so each reads as its own band. Inside cards, paragraphs and controls are comfortably spaced but not airy: about one baseline of paragraph spacing, 16–20px gaps between rows of controls, and 24–32px around major section dividers (e.g., between header and body or before the CTA). Text never touches edges; a consistent 16–24px inner padding is maintained. The actions bar uses roomy spacing between icons/buttons so nothing feels cramped. The comments list is slightly denser to accommodate more content while still respecting the base spacing scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a strong top-to-bottom rhythm: the eye is first drawn to the watercolor illustration header and logo, then to the navigation pill, then to the breadcrumb. From there, attention naturally drops into the main article card, where the article-header title in a bright accent purple is the primary focal point. The gaze moves down through the metadata and article-actions-bar, then into the article-body paragraphs in a classic reading flow. Near the end of the article, the ritual-planner-cta stands out as a mint-toned band, inviting action. Below the article, the related-articles-section catches the eye as a secondary exploration area, followed by the comments-section where the comment-form at the top encourages participation. The notification-area briefly interrupts near the top of the content column when present, and the ritual-planner-form-modal temporarily overrides the normal flow, dimming the background and centering focus on the ritual planning form.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a soft lavender canvas with a single centered column that feels like a stack of rounded paper cards laid down the page. At the very top, a watercolor illustration header with astrology motifs spans the full width, occupying roughly the top quarter of the screen and setting a whimsical tone. Below this, a pill-shaped navigation bar stretches across the centered content width, followed by a slightly smaller breadcrumb pill, each separated by comfortable lavender gaps. Beneath the breadcrumb, the main article appears as a large white or very pale-toned rounded card centered on the page, extending downward as the user scrolls. The article’s title and metadata occupy the upper part of this card, followed by a friendly, pill-like actions bar and then the body text flowing in easy-to-read lines. Near the lower part of this card, a softly colored mint CTA band for ritual planning may appear, subtly framed by extra vertical space. Below the article card, the page continues with a related articles card and then a comments card, each full-width within the content column and clearly separated by vertical lavender bands. When shown, notification banners slide into view near the top of the content column as rounded pills. When the ritual planning form is opened, the rest of the page fades slightly and a rounded modal card pops into the center of the screen, maintaining the same playful, handcrafted aesthetic while focusing the user’s attention on the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-data-loader is positioned within the main content column, occupying the same footprint as the main article card area beneath the breadcrumb pill. When active, it displays a rounded placeholder card centered horizontally that matches the width of the article card (spanning roughly 80–90% of the viewport width up to the fixed max content width, e.g., around 960px). Vertically, this placeholder extends for at least one viewport height or until it covers the expected article space. A soft overlay or skeleton elements appear inside with generous 16–24px padding on all sides, indicating where the title, metadata, actions bar, and paragraphs will load. The lavender page background remains visible above and below this loader card, preserving the stacked-card rhythm. If implemented as an overlay over the loaded page state, it subtly dims the article card area while keeping the watercolor header, navigation, and breadcrumb untouched above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally flows in the document directly below the breadcrumb pill, replacing the article-header, article-body, and article-actions-bar while loading. Optionally can include a semi-transparent overlay over the article card region but does not fix to the viewport; it scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header forms the top band of the main article card, sitting immediately below the breadcrumb pill with a vertical gap of about 24px of lavender background between. The card itself is centered in the viewport, with rounded corners and a soft shadow, spanning the full content column width (approximately 80–90% of the viewport up to the max-width). Inside this card, the article-header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the visible article area. The title is left-aligned but visually centered within the card width, set in a bright accent purple or green, and given generous breathing room: 24–32px of top padding before the first line and 16–24px below. Directly under the title, in the same left alignment, a single row of metadata (date, author, category, reading time) spans horizontally, using a subtle muted color. This metadata row sits about 8–16px below the title and has 16–24px of bottom padding before the next section (the actions bar). All text is contained within a 16–24px padding inset from the card edges, ensuring no text touches the rounded corners.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main article card, occupying the top portion and pushing subsequent sections (actions bar and body) downward in a standard flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-body fills the central and lower portion of the same main article card, directly beneath the article-actions-bar. It occupies the majority of the card’s vertical height, extending beyond the initial viewport as the reader scrolls. The text is left-aligned with a comfortable line length constrained within the card’s inner padding (16–24px from left and right edges). Paragraphs stack in a classic vertical reading order, with approximately one line of space (8–12px) between paragraphs and 24px before and after any section subheadings. Images or inline illustrations, when present, are centered within the body width and framed by slightly larger vertical spacing (24–32px above and below) to avoid crowding the text. The bottom of the article-body leaves enough space (around 24–32px) before any inline elements like the ritual-planner-cta if included, or before the card’s bottom padding. The density feels like a typical blog article: not sparse, but with enough breathing room that each paragraph is easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally inside the main article card as a scrollable content block. It expands vertically as needed, pushing the bottom of the card down and thus moving the related-articles-section and comments-section further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-actions-bar appears inside the main article card as a distinct horizontal pill-shaped strip between the article-header and the article-body. It is aligned with the card’s inner content width, inset by 16–24px from the card edges on left and right, and sits about 16px below the metadata row. This bar is approximately 48–56px tall, with rounded ends and a slightly tinted background (e.g., a pale mint or lavender) to distinguish it from the card surface. Inside the bar, action icons and labels (such as Favorite, Share, Print, or Save Ritual) are laid out in a single row from left to right, evenly spaced with about 16–24px between each interactive element. On narrower screens, these elements can wrap to two lines while preserving at least 12–16px of padding above and below the icons/text. The bar has 16–24px of vertical space below it before the first line of article-body text starts, emphasizing its function as a transitional control area between header and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the article card, anchored between the header and body. It scrolls away with the article content and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual-planner-cta is visually treated as a special in-article band inside the main article card, appearing near the lower portion of the article-body content. It spans the full inner width of the card (respecting the 16–24px side padding) and is shaped as a softly rounded rectangle or pill with a mint or pale green background that stands out from the white article surface. Vertically, it is about 96–160px tall depending on content, with a clear hierarchy: a short heading at the top, a brief description or benefit line below, and a prominent primary button aligned left or centered horizontally. There is generous vertical spacing inside: about 24px of padding at top and bottom, and 16px between the heading, description, and button. It is separated from the surrounding article text by larger-than-normal gaps—approximately 24–32px of white space above and below—so it feels like a clear call-to-action block without breaking the overall card continuity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded within the flow of the article-body. It appears after a relevant paragraph section and before the final paragraphs or conclusion. It scrolls naturally with the article content and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual-planner-form-modal appears as a centered floating card overlaying the entire page when the CTA is activated. The background of the entire viewport is dimmed with a semi-transparent lavender or charcoal-tinted overlay, while the watercolor header and underlying content remain faintly visible but clearly de-emphasized. The modal card itself is horizontally centered and vertically placed around the middle of the viewport, taking up roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (up to around 560–720px) and 60–80% of the viewport height, with ample 24–32px rounded corners. Inside the modal, content is arranged top-to-bottom: a short title at the top, a close icon in the top-right corner within the modal boundary, followed by form fields stacked in a single column with 16–24px vertical gaps, and a primary action button row at the bottom. There is 24–32px padding on all sides inside the modal. If the content exceeds the viewport height, the modal interior scrolls while the header and close control remain visible, or the entire modal scrolls but keeps its centered placement visually anchored.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport as a full-screen overlay, centered both horizontally and vertically. It sits above all other components in the stacking order and disables background interaction while open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-section is presented as its own rounded card directly beneath the main article card. A lavender band of about 24–32px separates the bottom of the article card from the top of this section. The card is centered within the content column, matching the article card’s width and corner radius, and includes 24px of padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a section heading (e.g., “More magic to explore” or “Related readings”) is left-aligned with 8–16px of spacing below it. Below the heading, related article items are arranged in a simple grid or stacked list: on wider screens, two columns of cards per row using the underlying 12-column feel (each item roughly 5 columns wide with 1-column gutter), and on smaller screens a single column. Each related item is a small rounded sub-card with its own 12–16px inner padding, thumbnail (optional), title, and small metadata, separated from others by about 16–24px horizontally and vertically. The bottom of this section has 24–32px of padding before the lavender background reappears, clearly marking the end of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and stacked directly after the article card within the main content column. It expands vertically based on the number of related items and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comments-section appears as another full-width rounded card within the centered column, positioned directly below the related-articles-section with a 24–32px lavender gap between them. It shares the same width and styling as the article and related cards, with 24px of inner padding and soft shadow. Inside this card, the comment-form is visually integrated at the top as its own block (see comment-form description), followed by the list of existing comments stacked vertically. A section title like “Comments” or “Moon circle chat” is placed near the upper left, either just above the form or as part of its header, with about 8–16px of space beneath it. Each individual comment occupies its own rounded sub-block or lightly separated area with 12–16px inner padding and 16px gaps between comments, maintaining slightly higher density than the article but still honoring the 8px spacing grid. The card extends vertically according to the number of comments, with a comfortable 24px of bottom padding before it meets the lavender background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and ordered after the related-articles-section, scrolling normally as part of the page. It can become quite tall on comment-heavy articles, pushing page footer content further down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comment-form sits at the top of the comments-section card, directly under the comments section heading. It is treated as a subtle sub-card area within the card: a lightly tinted background or a slim divider line can help distinguish it from the comment list below. The form fields are arranged in a single vertical column, left-aligned with the card’s content, and constrained within the same 16–24px side padding. A name field, email field (if shown), and main comment textarea stack with about 12–16px vertical spacing between them. The submit button (e.g., “Post comment”) is aligned left beneath the last field with 16px of top margin and at least 16px of bottom padding before the first displayed comment. Overall, the form’s height is compact—roughly 160–240px depending on field count—so it feels inviting but not overwhelming, and it clearly anchors the top of the comments card as the place to participate.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded at the top of the comments-section card, it flows with the comments content. It does not float independently; submitting or focusing it may temporarily reveal messages in the notification-area above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-area appears as a pill-shaped feedback banner within the main content column near the top of the article detail layout. When present, it is horizontally centered and spans most of the column width (roughly 70–80% of the article card width) but remains slightly narrower than the main cards so it visually reads as a floating pill rather than another full card. It sits in the vertical stack between the breadcrumb pill and the main article card, separated from each by about 16–24px of lavender background. The banner has fully rounded ends, a brighter accent background (for success, warning, or error), and 12–16px of inner padding. Text is centered or slightly left-aligned and kept to one or two lines, with a close icon on the right. The overall height is compact—around 40–56px—so it briefly catches the eye without dominating the layout. When multiple notifications occur, they either stack vertically with 8–12px gaps or are shown one at a time in this same area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows with the page layout within the content column, pushing the article card down while visible. It is not fixed; as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen like other content. It appears and disappears dynamically in response to user actions (e.g., saving an article, posting a comment).

name: Tarot Spreads
title: Tarot Spreads
description: Search, filter, sort, and bookmark tarot spread guides, then open individual spread details.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for tarot spreads using getTarotSpreadFilterOptions.
Allow users to enter a search query and submit it to searchTarotSpreads.
Allow users to apply filters such as difficulty and theme and pass them as part of the filters argument to searchTarotSpreads.
Allow users to change sort order using sort options returned by getTarotSpreadFilterOptions and pass the selected value to searchTarotSpreads.
Display a list of tarot spreads returned by searchTarotSpreads, showing key metadata from the TarotSpread model.
Allow users to open a spread detail page by clicking a spread title, navigating to tarot_spread_detail.html with the spread ID as a URL parameter.
Allow users to bookmark a spread directly from the results list using favoriteTarotSpread and show inline feedback for that action.
Provide a way to navigate to favorites.html after bookmarking spreads.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on the page with prefilled search and filters
1. User navigates to tarot_spreads.html, optionally with URL parameters for query, difficulty, theme, and sortBy.
2. The page controller reads the URL parameters and stores them in the page state.
3. The page calls getTarotSpreadFilterOptions to retrieve available difficulties, themes, and sort options.
4. The filter panel and search form initialize their controls based on the URL parameters and available options.
5. The page calls searchTarotSpreads with the current query, a filters object containing difficulty and theme from the URL (if present), and sortBy (if present).
6. The results list displays the tarot spreads returned by searchTarotSpreads.
7. The user can click any spread title to open tarot_spread_detail.html with the selected spread ID in the URL.

Workflow: Searching and refining results from within the page
1. User types text into the search input field.
2. User optionally adjusts difficulty and theme filters in the filter panel.
3. User optionally chooses a sort option from the sort dropdown.
4. User submits the search via the search form submit action or by changing filters (depending on implementation behavior).
5. The page controller updates the URL parameters to reflect the current query, difficulty, theme, and sortBy values.
6. The page calls searchTarotSpreads with the updated query, filters, and sortBy.
7. The results list is refreshed with the new set of TarotSpread records returned by searchTarotSpreads.
8. The user may repeat adjusting filters and sort options, causing the URL and results to update each time.

Workflow: Bookmarking a spread and visiting favorites
1. User locates a desired spread in the results list.
2. User clicks the bookmark/favorite action associated with that spread item.
3. The page calls favoriteTarotSpread with the spread’s ID and a source value indicating that the action originated from the tarot spreads listing page.
4. On successful response, the page updates the visual state of that spread’s bookmark action (for the current session) and may show a short confirmation message using only the returned message field.
5. User clicks a 'View Favorites' navigation element.
6. The browser navigates to favorites.html (without additional parameters from this page).

Workflow: Returning to a saved filter state via URL
1. User previously interacts with the page and shares or saves the URL that includes query, difficulty, theme, and sortBy parameters.
2. Another visit using that URL loads tarot_spreads.html with those parameters present.
3. The page controller reads the parameters and initializes search and filter controls accordingly.
4. The page calls searchTarotSpreads with the parameters from the URL so that the results reflect the saved state.
5. The user sees the same filtered and sorted results without needing to re-enter settings.
interactions: On initial page load, read query, difficulty, theme, and sortBy from the URL, then fetch filter options with getTarotSpreadFilterOptions and results with searchTarotSpreads.
When the user types in the search field and submits the form, update the query parameter in the URL and call searchTarotSpreads with the new query.
When the user changes the difficulty filter, update the difficulty parameter in the URL and call searchTarotSpreads with filters containing the selected difficulty.
When the user changes the theme filter, update the theme parameter in the URL and call searchTarotSpreads with filters containing the selected theme.
When the user changes the sort option, update the sortBy parameter in the URL and call searchTarotSpreads with the new sortBy value.
When the user clicks a spread title or main clickable region, navigate to tarot_spread_detail.html with the TarotSpread.id passed as the id URL parameter.

When the user clicks the bookmark action on a spread item, call favoriteTarotSpread with the corresponding tarotSpreadId and a source indicating the tarot spreads page; then update only that item’s inline bookmark state based on the result.
When the user clicks the 'View Favorites' navigation element, navigate to favorites.html.
When the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, detect URL parameter changes and re-run searchTarotSpreads so the displayed results match the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s primary persistent state is encoded in URL parameters. The query parameter represents the current search text. The difficulty parameter represents the selected difficulty filter value. The theme parameter represents the selected theme filter value. The sortBy parameter represents the chosen sort option. On page load, the controller reads these parameters and initializes internal state accordingly, then performs a searchTarotSpreads call with: query set from the query parameter (or an empty string if absent), filters as an object containing difficulty and theme values when those parameters are present (and omitting them when not), and sortBy set from the sortBy parameter (or omitted if absent). Whenever the user changes search text, filters, or sort selection, the controller constructs a new URL with the updated parameters and uses history navigation to replace or push the state, then calls searchTarotSpreads with the new values. Clearing a filter removes the corresponding parameter from the URL and from the filters object in the searchTarotSpreads call. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all shareable state is represented via these URL parameters. Transient UI states, such as the visual indication of a just-bookmarked spread, are kept only in memory for the current session and are not encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, API calls to getTarotSpreadFilterOptions and searchTarotSpreads, distribution of data to child components, handling of history navigation, and orchestration of favoriteTarotSpread calls triggered by child components.

Displays and manages the filter controls for difficulty and theme, using options provided by getTarotSpreadFilterOptions. Emits filter change events when the user selects or clears filters so the controller can update URL parameters and re-run searchTarotSpreads.

Provides a text input for the query and a select input for sort options. Initializes its values from the current URL state and available sort options from getTarotSpreadFilterOptions. On submission or sort selection change, it notifies the controller to update URL parameters and call searchTarotSpreads.

Renders the collection of TarotSpread items returned by searchTarotSpreads, including each spread’s key attributes. Delegates per-item interactions (open detail, bookmark) to child result item components and surfaces those events to the page controller as needed.

Represents a single TarotSpread in the results. Shows title and other metadata fields from the TarotSpread model. Clicking the title triggers navigation to tarot_spread_detail.html with the spread’s id parameter. Clicking the bookmark control triggers a favoriteTarotSpread call via the controller and updates this item’s bookmark state based on the returned success status.

Displays a message and contextual actions when searchTarotSpreads returns an empty list (for example, suggesting that the user adjust filters or clear the search query). The visibility of this component depends on the size of the TarotSpread results array.

Displays a loading state while getTarotSpreadFilterOptions or searchTarotSpreads are in progress. It appears when the controller begins an API call and hides when the call completes, regardless of success or failure.

Shows non-technical error messages when getTarotSpreadFilterOptions, searchTarotSpreads, or favoriteTarotSpread encounter errors. Receives error content from the controller and may offer a retry action that re-invokes the failed call.
Provides navigation to favorites.html. It can be displayed persistently or emphasized after a successful favoriteTarotSpread call, but it does not itself call any data interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column sitting on a full-width lavender background. Above, the existing site watercolor illustration and global navigation remain unchanged. Within the Tarot Spreads page, components are arranged in a top‑to‑bottom stack of clearly separated bands: (1) a page controller card (title + high-level actions), (2) a search pill bar, (3) a dismissible error banner when present, and (4) a main results band that, on larger screens, splits into a soft 2‑column layout: a left filter sidebar card and a right results card containing the list items. On smaller screens, the filters and results collapse back into a simple vertical stack, preserving the classic blog-like reading flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column sitting on a full-width lavender background. Above, the existing site watercolor illustration and global navigation remain unchanged. Within the Tarot Spreads page, components are arranged in a top‑to‑bottom stack of clearly separated bands: (1) a page controller card (title + high-level actions), (2) a search pill bar, (3) a dismissible error banner when present, and (4) a main results band that, on larger screens, splits into a soft 2‑column layout: a left filter sidebar card and a right results card containing the list items. On smaller screens, the filters and results collapse back into a simple vertical stack, preserving the classic blog-like reading flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content column spans about 80–90% of the viewport width up to a fixed max-width (roughly 960–1040px), centered horizontally with generous lavender margins left and right. The watercolor header occupies the upper 25–30% of the viewport height. Below it, the Tarot Spreads page controller card takes around 8–10% of the initial viewport height, the search pill another 8–10%, and the main results band (filters + list) fills the remaining height. Vertically, each major band (page controller, search, error banner, main results card) is separated by about 16–24px. Inside each card or pill, there is 16–24px of padding on all sides so text never touches edges; headings get slightly more top/bottom space than body text.
layout_strategies.content_density: Macro-level density is airy: each card is clearly separated with visible lavender background between elements, and containers have comfortable internal padding. Micro-level density within the content is moderate: standard line heights, readable paragraph spacing, and compact but not cramped arrangement of controls. Filters are grouped with small gaps between chips/toggles; list items stack vertically with 12–16px of space or soft dividers between them. Buttons and links (like Favorites) have enough surrounding space to feel tappable and relaxed rather than tightly packed.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the watercolor header and site logo, then moves down into the Tarot Spreads page controller card where the page title and Favorites link establish context. From there, the gaze naturally drops to the search pill, which feels like the main input to start interacting. Below that, any error banner is quickly noticed due to its accent color yet remains visually lightweight. The main results band then draws attention, starting with the left filter sidebar (pastel card with playful chips and toggles), then sweeping rightward to the results list card where individual tarot spread items—with bold titles and bookmark icons—become the primary reading units. Within each result, the flow goes title → key metadata → short description → tags/bookmark. Empty, loading, and error states occupy the same results area so the eye always returns to that central region for feedback.

layout.overall_description: The Tarot Spreads page sits within the familiar watercolor-and-lavender shell of the site. Centered on the screen, a single content column holds a sequence of soft, rounded cards that feel like pieces of stationery stacked on a pastel desk. Just below the site’s illustrated header, a wide rounded card announces the Tarot Spreads section with a playful title on the left and a colorful Favorites link on the right. A pill-shaped search bar follows, floating with ample space above and below so it feels inviting rather than utilitarian. Beneath, a slim error banner appears only when needed, gently glowing with warm red accents without overpowering the page. The lower portion of the view is dominated by a large, pastel main results region. On larger screens this region splits into a left-hand filter card and a right-hand results card, like a margin full of charms and a main page of entries; on smaller screens the filter card gracefully stacks above the results, keeping the reading flow linear. Each tarot spread in the list is its own mini card inside the results panel, with rounded corners, a bit of breathing room, bold titles, and bookmark icons that echo the handcrafted, retro-fantasy vibe. Loading spinners, friendly empty-state illustrations, and error messages all appear inside this results area, maintaining a consistent focal point. The whole page feels like a whimsical index of spreads—playful but orderly, easy to scan from top to bottom in a single, coherent flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is the first Tarot Spreads–specific card beneath the existing site watercolor header and global navigation. It sits centered in the page within the main content column, with approximately 24px of lavender background margin above it and 16–24px below before the search form. The card spans about 80–90% of the content width (up to the column max-width) and has generously rounded corners and a soft pastel background (e.g., pale mint) to distinguish it from the lavender page background. Inside, there is around 24px padding on all sides. The layout is a horizontal split: on the left, the page title “Tarot Spreads” in a large, playful, slightly retro type that takes up roughly two-thirds of the card width; below or just under the title, a short one-line description can appear, aligned left. On the right, aligned to the top-right corner but with at least 16px inset from the edges, sit any page-level controls (such as a sort dropdown or view toggle) and the Favorites link pill, aligned along a common baseline with the title where possible. On narrower screens, these right-side controls wrap below the title, still inside the same card, creating a stacked layout: title at top, description, then a row of controls centered or right-aligned within the card. The card maintains the overall vertical reading order and is clearly perceived as the contextual ‘header’ for the Tarot Spreads section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll; it scrolls away with the rest of the content. The card remains fully within the central column, always horizontally centered on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel occupies a left-hand column within the main results region on medium and large screens, but appears as its own full-width card stacked above the results list on smaller screens. In the vertical flow, this panel sits below the search form (with about 16–24px of vertical gap) and alongside the results list as part of a shared container. On desktop, the main results region uses an internal 12-column grid: the filter panel card spans roughly 4 columns (about 30–35% of the content width) on the left, and the results list spans the remaining 8 columns (about 65–70%) on the right. Both cards share a common top edge to form a visual ‘band’. The filter panel itself is a rounded, pastel card (e.g., very light mint or cream) with 16–24px padding. A small but clear heading like “Filter spreads” appears at the top-left inside the panel, with a bit of extra top margin. Beneath, filter controls (checkboxes, toggles, chips for categories like ‘Love’, ‘Career’, ‘Daily Draw’, etc.) are arranged in vertical groups with 8–12px spacing between each control and 16px between groups. Filters may be arranged in two narrow columns on desktop within the card to avoid excessive vertical length, while on mobile they collapse into a single column. The panel’s height adjusts to its content; if it becomes taller than the visible viewport, it simply scrolls away with the page. The pastel background, thin borders, and rounded shape echo the handcrafted blog-card aesthetic from the mockup while still clearly functioning as an interactive control area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the filter panel is part of a 2-column layout within the central content column, aligned flush to the left of the results list. It may use a subtle sticky behavior so that, when the user scrolls, the top of the panel pins to the top of the viewport within the main content area while the results list continues to scroll; on smaller screens, this sticky behavior is disabled and the filter panel scrolls normally in a stacked layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form appears directly below the Tarot Spreads page controller card, separated from it by about 16–24px of vertical space filled with the lavender page background. It is designed as a long, pill-shaped band centered in the content column, spanning about 80–90% of the content width but visually slimmer in height than the page controller. Its background is a slightly lighter or contrasting pastel (for example, a pale lavender against a slightly darker lavender page) with a thin outline to emphasize the pill silhouette. Inside, there is roughly 16px of padding vertically and 16–24px horizontally. The main search input field occupies most of the pill width, horizontally centered, with rounded ends that roughly echo the outer pill. A small tarot-inspired icon (e.g., a magnifying glass with a star) can sit at the left inside the field, and a compact but clearly labeled search button can be aligned to the right edge of the field or just outside it. On larger screens, the field and button form a single horizontal row; on smaller screens, the button may drop underneath while still centered. There is ample space above and below the text baseline so it feels airy, consistent with the mockup’s navigation pill style. The search form is clearly the primary interactive entry point right after reading the page title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll and always centered within the content column. It expands horizontally with the column up to the max width and shrinks gracefully on smaller screens while maintaining the pill shape.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list sits to the right of the filter panel on medium and large screens, and directly below it on smaller screens. It is contained within a large, rounded article-style card that visually mirrors the main article card in the mockup. This card begins roughly 16–24px below the search form (or below any error banner if visible) and extends downward to fill the remainder of the viewport and beyond as needed. On desktop, the results list card spans about 65–70% of the content width, aligned to the right edge of the central column, sharing its top baseline with the filter panel card to form a single horizontal band. The card’s background is a soft pastel (e.g., very light cream or faintly tinted lavender) with 24px of internal padding. At the top of the card, a subtle header row displays result context: a small title like “All spreads” or “X spreads found” aligned to the left, and optional sort or layout controls aligned to the right; this header has a bit of extra bottom spacing (12–16px) before the list items begin. Below, tarot-spreads-result-item components are stacked vertically, each taking full width of the card, separated by 12–16px of vertical space, a faint divider line, or a subtle watercolor stroke. The list scrolls naturally with the page; no internal scrolling is used, maintaining the continuous vertical reading feel of a blog. When there are no results, a dedicated empty-state message occupies the central area within this same card; when loading, a spinner appears centered within this area; when errors specific to results occur, a message appears at the top inside this card. Thus, the card is always the primary focal region for search outcomes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the 2-column layout with the filter panel on larger screens; full-width in the central column on smaller screens. It scrolls with the page; internal content is not separately scrollable. Empty, loading, and error states replace the internal list region without changing the card’s position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each result item is a self-contained mini card inside the results list card, visually distinct but not as heavy as a standalone page card. It spans the full width of the results list’s inner content area, with about 16–20px of internal padding on all sides. The item’s background is either subtly elevated (a slightly lighter pastel) or simply outlined with a fine line and rounded corners (4–8px radius) to maintain the handcrafted aesthetic. At the top-left of the item, the tarot spread title appears in a bold, colorful heading style (e.g., purple or deep green) that immediately catches the eye. To the top-right, aligned with the title’s baseline, sits a bookmark or favorite icon button, set within a small circular or pill-shaped background that uses one of the accent colors (green/purple/red) to echo the playful look; this button is inset 8–12px from the item’s right edge. Beneath the title, a single line of metadata (such as ‘3-card spread • Beginner • 10–15 min’) is displayed in smaller, muted text and may include tiny icon accents (stars, moons) placed to the left of each meta chunk. Below that, there is a short 1–2 line description in normal body text, with about 8px of vertical space separating it from the metadata. At the bottom of the card, an optional row of tags or category chips (‘Love’, ‘Shadow work’, etc.) aligns left, with small pastel pill shapes and 4–8px spacing between chips. The vertical spacing within the item is tight enough to feel information-rich yet still gives each line breathing room. The entire item is clickable/tappable, with a subtle hover or focus effect like a soft shadow or a very light background tint shift.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the results list card as part of a vertical stack. On smaller screens, text reflows naturally to multiple lines and the bookmark icon may move to a second row if necessary, but the item remains a single unified tap target.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message appears inside the tarot-spreads-results-list card, replacing the standard list of result items when no spreads match the current search and filters. It is vertically and horizontally centered within the card’s content area, with ample whitespace around it so it feels light and not like an error. The layout consists of a small, whimsical illustration or icon (perhaps an empty tarot table or a single card with a question mark) centered near the top of the visible area, followed by a short, friendly headline such as “No spreads found in this constellation” in a slightly larger, colored type (purple/green), centered, with about 8–12px of margin below. Underneath, 1–2 lines of explanatory body text suggest next steps (e.g., ‘Try removing a filter or searching a broader term.’), again centered and constrained to a comfortable reading width (about 60–70% of the card width). Optional action buttons or links (e.g., ‘Clear filters’) align center below the text with 16px of top margin. The empty state keeps the card’s pastel background, rounded corners, and padding, so it feels like a natural alternate “page” of the same results area rather than a separate overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the central interior of the results list card when there are zero results; it displaces list items but does not change the size or position of the outer card. It scrolls with the rest of the page if content above pushes it below the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator is displayed within the tarot-spreads-results-list card, centered in the list region to maintain focus where results typically appear. When a search or filter action triggers loading, existing content can either show a faint dim overlay or subtle opacity reduction, with the loading indicator placed above it. The indicator itself is a small, circular spinner or a looping constellation-like animation in one of the bright accent colors (e.g., a purple ring with small star nodes), approximately 32–48px in diameter. It is centered horizontally and positioned slightly above the vertical center of the card so it feels visually balanced. A small line of text beneath the spinner (e.g., ‘Shuffling your spreads…’) in muted text color can appear to reinforce the friendly tone and make the wait feel intentional. There is sufficient empty space around the indicator (at least 24px padding from any nearby text) so it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlay or temporary replacement within the results list card during loading states, without moving the card itself. It disappears once results or an empty state are ready, returning the card to its normal content. It scrolls with the page rather than being fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner is a full-width pill-shaped bar within the central content column that appears only when there is a relevant error (such as a network issue or failed search). It is positioned in the vertical flow directly below the search form and above the main results region, separated from each by about 12–16px of lavender background. The banner spans roughly the same width as the search pill (80–90% of the column width), centered, with soft rounded corners and a warm, slightly saturated background (e.g., a pastel red or peach) that contrasts with but does not clash against the lavender page background. Inside there is around 12–16px of padding. On the left side of the banner, a small warning icon (e.g., an exclamation mark inside a triangle or a distressed star) appears, followed by concise error text like ‘We couldn’t load spreads. Please check your connection and try again.’ The text uses a readable, slightly darker red or berry tone. On the far right side, aligned with the text baseline, a subtle ‘Retry’ link or button can be placed, using an accent color that still feels harmonious. The overall height of the banner is modest—enough to be clearly visible but not so tall that it overwhelms the search or results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and dismissible; it scrolls with the content and disappears when the error condition is resolved or dismissed by the user. It does not overlay other content or remain fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites link appears as a small but visually bright navigation element within the tarot-spreads-page-controller card. It is positioned toward the top-right corner of the card, sharing a horizontal baseline with the page title where space allows. The link is styled as a pill-shaped button or bold text link with an accent color fill (for example, bright green or purple) and white or very light text reading something like ‘View Favorites’. It has 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–20px of horizontal padding, with fully rounded ends that match the overall pill motif seen in the navigation and search elements. On larger screens, it sits to the right of any other page-level controls in a small horizontal cluster; on smaller screens, it may shift to a separate row below the title, centered or right-aligned within the card, maintaining at least 8–12px of vertical spacing from other elements. The favorites link is clearly distinguishable from plain text links through its background color and slight shadow or border, but it is smaller than primary call-to-action buttons elsewhere to reflect its supporting role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the page controller card; it moves with the card as the page scrolls. On responsive layouts it wraps to a new line as needed but always remains confined to the interior of the page controller card and aligned to the right side of the content column.

name: Tarot Spread Detail
title: Tarot Spread Detail
description: Displays full details and instructions for a single tarot spread, with card count, estimated reading time, bookmarking, and related content navigation.

core_features: Load and display a tarot spread’s full details (title, description, difficulty, theme, tags, content, layout image) using getTarotSpreadDetail based on the id URL parameter.
Show key stats (number of cards, estimated reading time, rating, rating count) in a prominent stats section near the top of the page to support quick comparison between spreads.
Provide a Bookmark/Add to Favorites action that calls favoriteTarotSpread for the currently viewed spread and offers navigation to the Favorites page.
Display related tarot spreads and related articles for the current spread using getRelatedContentForTarotSpread and allow navigation to those items.
Offer contextual navigation back to the spreads listing (tarot_spreads.html) filtered by a theme or keyword when possible.
user_workflows: View a tarot spread from the spreads list: 1) User clicks a spread in tarot_spreads.html which navigates to tarot_spread_detail.html?id={TarotSpread.id}. 2) On page load, the Tarot Spread Detail page reads the id parameter from the URL. 3) The page calls getTarotSpreadDetail with tarotSpreadId = id. 4) After a successful response, the page populates the header, stats, layout, and full instructions with the TarotSpread data. 5) In parallel or after the main spread loads, the page calls getRelatedContentForTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId = id and displays related spreads and articles when available.

Bookmark a tarot spread for later practice: 1) While viewing a spread, the user clicks the Bookmark/Add to Favorites button. 2) The page triggers favoriteTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId = current TarotSpread.id and source = 'tarot_spread_detail'. 3) While the request is in progress, the button is disabled or shows a loading state to prevent duplicate submissions. 4) On success, the interface updates to indicate that the spread is saved (e.g., toggled state or success feedback) using the returned message and favorite_item_id. 5) The user can click a 'View Favorites' navigation link which opens favorites.html (no additional parameters required).

Explore related spreads for comparison of card count and reading time: 1) After the current spread is loaded, the page calls getRelatedContentForTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId = current TarotSpread.id. 2) The related spreads are listed with their titles, difficulty, number_of_cards, and estimated_reading_time_minutes, allowing the user to assess which spread might be shorter or more complex. 3) The user clicks a related spread item, which navigates to tarot_spread_detail.html?id={related TarotSpread.id}. 4) The page re-runs the load logic for the new id and refreshes stats and related content accordingly.

Open related astrology or tarot articles for deeper learning: 1) In the related content section, the user sees related_articles loaded from getRelatedContentForTarotSpread. 2) The user clicks a related article item. 3) The browser navigates to article.html?id={Article.id}, where the Article Detail page is responsible for loading and displaying that article.

Return to the spreads listing with contextual filtering: 1) When the main TarotSpread is loaded, the page derives a themeOrKeyword value for back navigation, preferring the spread’s theme field when available. 2) The 'Back to all spreads' link points to tarot_spreads.html with query={themeOrKeyword} if a themeOrKeyword can be determined, otherwise it points to tarot_spreads.html without parameters. 3) The user clicks this link and is taken back to the spreads listing page, which may use the query parameter to show related spreads (handled on that page).
interactions: On initial page load or when the URL id parameter changes, the page calls getTarotSpreadDetail with tarotSpreadId set to the id URL parameter and, on success, populates all bound components with the returned TarotSpread data.
If getTarotSpreadDetail fails (e.g., invalid id or network error), an error message component is shown and spread-specific components remain hidden or show a fallback state.

When the main spread detail has loaded successfully, the page calls getRelatedContentForTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId set to the current spread’s id and populates the related spreads and related articles list on success.

Clicking the Bookmark/Add to Favorites button triggers favoriteTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId set to the current spread’s id and source set to 'tarot_spread_detail'; the button is temporarily disabled while waiting for the response.

On successful favoriteTarotSpread response, the favorite button’s state is updated (for example, to indicate it is now saved), and a success message is shown to the user based on the returned message field.
If favoriteTarotSpread returns a non-success status, an error or retry message is displayed; the button is re-enabled so the user can try again.
Clicking the 'View Favorites' link navigates directly to favorites.html without additional parameters.

Clicking a related spread item navigates to tarot_spread_detail.html?id={related TarotSpread.id}, which updates the URL id parameter and triggers a full or partial reload of spread data via getTarotSpreadDetail and getRelatedContentForTarotSpread.
Clicking a related article item navigates to article.html?id={Article.id}.
Clicking the 'Back to all spreads' link navigates to tarot_spreads.html, optionally with a query URL parameter derived from the current spread’s theme or other contextual keyword.

If the layout image is interactive (e.g., click-to-enlarge), clicking it toggles a local UI state (such as opening a modal or zoomed view) without calling additional interfaces and without changing the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the id URL parameter (tarot_spread_detail.html?id={TarotSpread.id}). When the page loads, it reads id from the query string; if id is present and non-empty, it calls getTarotSpreadDetail with tarotSpreadId = id. Until the response returns, the page is in a loading state in which detail components display loading indicators and interactive buttons like the favorite button may be disabled. If getTarotSpreadDetail succeeds, the TarotSpread object returned becomes the single source of truth for all spread-bound components (title, description, difficulty, theme, number_of_cards, estimated_reading_time_minutes, rating, rating_count, tags, content, layout_image_url, timestamps). After a successful spread load, the page requests related content by calling getRelatedContentForTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId = current TarotSpread.id and uses the returned related_spreads and related_articles arrays to populate related content components. If either interface call fails, the page tracks an error state associated with that call and displays an appropriate error message while leaving other components operational where possible (for example, the main detail can show even if related content fails). The favorite state is determined per session by user actions and responses from favoriteTarotSpread; since there is no interface to pre-load whether the current spread is already favorited, the page initializes the favorite button in a neutral 'not yet saved' state and only updates it on successful calls to favoriteTarotSpread. During a favoriteTarotSpread call, the page sets a transient 'favoriting' state, disabling the button until the response returns. Navigation does not rely on localStorage; instead, navigation targets are constructed from current in-memory state and URL parameters. The 'Back to all spreads' link constructs its target URL dynamically: if the current TarotSpread.theme is available and non-empty, the link points to tarot_spreads.html?query={encoded theme}; if not, it points to tarot_spreads.html without a query parameter. When a user clicks a related spread, the browser navigates to tarot_spread_detail.html?id={related TarotSpread.id}, which resets the page state to loading and re-runs the entire load sequence for the new id. Browser back/forward changes to the id parameter are handled the same way, ensuring URL-driven state without localStorage.

components.functionality: Reads the id URL parameter, calls getTarotSpreadDetail with tarotSpreadId = id, manages loading and error states for the main TarotSpread data, and triggers the related content load after a successful response.
Displays the spread’s title, difficulty, theme, and key tags once the TarotSpread data is available.

Shows the spread’s number_of_cards, estimated_reading_time_minutes, rating, and rating_count in a compact area near the top of the page to help users quickly understand spread complexity and time commitment.
Renders the spread’s layout image if layout_image_url is provided and optionally supports click-to-enlarge behavior without calling additional interfaces.
Displays the full instructions and interpretation guidance for the spread using the content field of the TarotSpread entity.
Allows the user to bookmark/add the current spread to favorites by calling favoriteTarotSpread; manages neutral, loading, success, and error UI states based on the API response.
Provides a link to open the Favorites page after or independent of bookmarking actions.
Constructs and displays a 'Back to all spreads' link pointing to tarot_spreads.html, optionally including a query parameter derived from the current spread’s theme or another contextual keyword.

After the main spread is successfully loaded, calls getRelatedContentForTarotSpread with tarotSpreadId set to the current spread’s id and stores the returned related_spreads and related_articles for display in related content components.

Shows a list of related TarotSpread items returned by getRelatedContentForTarotSpread, exposing key details like title, difficulty, number_of_cards, and estimated_reading_time_minutes to support quick comparison and navigation.
Displays related Article items returned by getRelatedContentForTarotSpread and provides navigation to each article’s detail page.
Shows a loading state while getTarotSpreadDetail or getRelatedContentForTarotSpread requests are in progress, and hides itself when those calls complete.

Displays error messages if getTarotSpreadDetail or getRelatedContentForTarotSpread fail, differentiating between critical errors (which prevent displaying the main spread) and non-critical errors (such as missing related content).

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column on top of a full-width lavender background. Below the site’s illustrated watercolor header (global, not part of this spec), stack components vertically in this order: a back navigation pill bar, then a favorites/navigation pill bar, then a large main spread-detail card that contains header, bookmark, stats, diagram, and full instructions. Beneath the main card, stack related content cards (related spreads, then related articles). Loaders and status indicators temporarily replace or overlay these areas while data is loading or on error.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column on top of a full-width lavender background. Below the site’s illustrated watercolor header (global, not part of this spec), stack components vertically in this order: a back navigation pill bar, then a favorites/navigation pill bar, then a large main spread-detail card that contains header, bookmark, stats, diagram, and full instructions. Beneath the main card, stack related content cards (related spreads, then related articles). Loaders and status indicators temporarily replace or overlay these areas while data is loading or on error.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly the top 25–30% of the viewport for the global watercolor header. The back and favorites navigation pills each consume about 8–10% of the viewport height when visible, with a 16–24px vertical gap between them. The main spread-detail card occupies the majority of the remaining viewport height, often extending beyond the fold. Within that card, the header and bookmark cluster occupies the top ~20%, the stats bar ~10%, the layout diagram ~20–25%, and the text content fills the rest. The related content cards each take about 20% of viewport height when populated. All major elements sit inside a fixed-width content column (approx. 960–1040px on desktop) with 24px side padding on smaller screens and 16–24px vertical gaps between stacked blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: distinct, generously spaced macro elements (pills and cards) with 16–24px margins between them, and card interiors padded 16–24px on all sides. Within the spread content section, paragraph spacing is comfortable but not loose (around one line-height between paragraphs). Navigation pills and stats rows have roomy line height, with icons and text breathing inside rounded containers; nothing feels cramped against edges.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the illustrated header (global). Immediately below, the strong horizontal back-navigation pill at the top of the content column catches attention, then the favorites pill below it. From there, the gaze drops into the main spread-detail card, hitting the bold spread title in the header first, then sliding to the bookmark button on the right. The eye then moves down to the stats bar, then to the central layout diagram, and finally flows through the instructions text. At the end of the reading, the eye naturally continues down to the related spreads card and then the related articles card, maintaining a clear top-to-bottom reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Tarot Spread Detail page sits on a soft lavender backdrop with a playful watercolor header stretching full width at the very top (site-level). Centered beneath this, a single, rounded content column anchors the page. At the top of this column, a wide, pill-shaped back-navigation bar runs horizontally with ample padding, echoing the handcrafted, retro-blog mood. A second, slightly smaller pill bar just below leads to favorites, mimicking a breadcrumb-like band. Below these, a large, rounded main card with a mint or pale-cream interior contains all spread details: a bold, colorful spread title and subtitle at the top, the bookmark control tucked into the top-right corner, then a soft pastel stats strip, a centered tarot layout illustration, and finally a tall text section describing how to use the spread. The card feels like a self-contained article sitting comfortably in space, with lavender background visible around it. Once users scroll past this main card, they encounter two additional rounded cards stacked vertically: one listing related spreads, the other related articles, each styled like mini blog cards with their own headings and item lists. When loading or on error, simple, rounded panels and whimsical indicators appear in the same positions as the cards they replace, preserving the clear vertical flow and calm, spacious rhythm of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that governs the main content stack directly beneath the global illustrated header. It conceptually wraps the entire centered content column—from the back navigation pill down through the related content cards. When in a loading state, it hides the underlying components and instead shows the loading-indicator centered within the column at roughly mid-viewport height. When in an error state, it replaces the main spread-detail card area (just below the navigation pills) with the error-message-panel. Visually, it ensures that either a single, focused loading/error surface is shown in the central column or the full set of stacked cards is visible, never both at once.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a container controlling visibility of the stacked components in the natural document flow; does not render its own box but switches between showing the loading-indicator/error-message-panel and the standard vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spread header sits at the very top of the main spread-detail card, aligned to the left inside the card’s generous 16–24px padding. It spans nearly the full width of the card, leaving space on the far right for the bookmark-button. The header includes the spread title in large, colorful, slightly whimsical type, possibly followed by a short subtitle or tagline in a smaller, softer color. Vertically, it occupies the top 20–25% of the card’s height, with extra top and bottom padding so the title does not feel cramped. The text is left-aligned, with the top edge sitting about 16–24px below the card’s top border, creating a strong, immediate focal area within the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main card, anchored to the top of the card; shares the top row horizontally with the bookmark-button, which is aligned to the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spread-stats-bar appears directly below the spread-header within the same main card, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 8–12px. It is a full-width, horizontally oriented strip with a pastel background (e.g., very light mint or blush) and slightly rounded corners that echo the card’s rounding but inset slightly from its edges. Within this bar, small icons and labels—for card count, estimated reading time, and difficulty—are distributed horizontally with ample spacing, following a loose 3- or 4-column grid. It occupies roughly 10% of the card’s height and about 80–90% of the card’s internal width, centered visually so lavender background is visible at card edges. The bar reads like a compact, friendly info panel that visually anchors the area between the title above and the diagram below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width child of the main card, spanning edge-to-edge within the card padding; it does not scroll independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The layout-diagram-viewer is positioned inside the main card directly beneath the spread-stats-bar, with a vertical gap of about 16–24px. It is centered horizontally, occupying roughly 70–80% of the card’s width, with equal left and right margins showing the card background. The diagram appears as a framed illustration—perhaps a soft-edged image—with mild rounding on its corners and enough padding or margin so no content touches its edges. Vertically, it takes up around 20–25% of the card’s height on a typical desktop view, shrinking proportionally on smaller screens. On tall screens, it sits in the middle third of the card, visually bridging the numbers-focused stats above and the instructional text below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and centered within the main card; scales responsively with the card’s width while maintaining aspect ratio, but always remains within the bounds of the card padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The spread-content-section occupies the entire lower portion of the main spread-detail card beneath the layout-diagram-viewer. It begins about 16–24px below the diagram, spanning the full internal width of the card (minus standard padding) and extending downward as needed. Within this area, headings and paragraphs of instruction stack vertically, aligned left, following the classic blog-article pattern. Paragraphs have normal line length (approximately 60–75 characters) and moderate spacing between them, with no text touching the edges. Any subheadings or bullet lists appear with a bit of extra top margin (around 16px) to create clear rhythm. On scroll, this section forms the bulk of the reading experience, occupying the majority of the card height and often extending beyond the initial viewport, inviting vertical scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content flowing within the main card; scrolls with the page as part of the single-column layout, without any fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-button is visually paired with the spread-header at the top of the main card. It sits in the same horizontal row as the title, aligned to the top-right corner of the card, inset by the same 16–24px padding that the header uses. The button appears as a small pill or rounded icon button (e.g., a star or ribbon) with a bright accent color (vivid green, purple, or red) that stands out against the softer card background. It occupies a compact area—no more than 10–15% of the card’s width—and is vertically centered relative to the title’s first line to create a balanced header row. The button does not dominate but is immediately discoverable in the corner of the header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned at the top-right within the main card; it does not float over scrolling content or stick on scroll, remaining fixed in place relative to the card header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-navigation-link is presented as a full-width, pill-shaped bar situated directly below the back-to-spreads-link and above the main spread-detail card. It mirrors the breadcrumb bar pattern: a low-profile yet clearly separated band. The pill spans nearly the full width of the centered content column (about 90–100% of the column width) with rounded ends and 16–20px of internal horizontal padding. The link text is left-aligned, reading something like “View all saved spreads,” with a modest icon on the left if desired. This bar has a slightly softer color than the back-to-spreads pill—perhaps a light lavender or mint tone—so it feels secondary yet still noticeable. Vertically, it accounts for about 8–10% of the viewport height, with 16–24px of lavender background visible both above (towards the back pill) and below (towards the main card).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the main vertical flow; occupies its own row in the column and scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-spreads-link appears at the top of the content column, just beneath the global watercolor header. It is styled as a prominent navigation pill: a wide, horizontally oriented bar with rounded ends, standing out against the lavender page background. The pill spans roughly 90–100% of the content column’s width, centered within the page. Inside, an arrow icon and label text such as “Back to all spreads” are left-aligned, with plenty of left padding so the text feels comfortably inset. The bar’s height is generous (approximately 48px or more) to echo the playful, blog-like navigation in the mockup. A vertical gap of 16–24px separates it from both the illustrated header above and the favorites-navigation-link below, creating a clear, stepped hierarchy of navigation and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-level element in the main stack beneath the site header; scrolls normally with the page and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-content-loader conceptually wraps the lower section of the page, starting immediately below the main spread-detail card and extending through the area where the related-spreads-list and related-articles-list appear. In its loading state, instead of these two cards, a single loading-indicator or a simplified placeholder panel appears centered in this lower region, occupying approximately the same vertical space as one medium-height card. When loaded, the loader reveals the two related content cards stacked vertically.
