A 16–24px margin separates this whole related section from the main card above, keeping the stacks distinct while maintaining the same single-column flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Controls visibility of related content elements within the normal document flow; does not render its own visible box but determines whether loading-indicator or the related lists are shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-spreads-list appears below the main spread-detail card, separated by a vertical gap of about 24px. It is contained within its own rounded card, aligned with the same centered content column and width as the main card, but slightly shorter. The card has 16–24px of internal padding and a soft background color (light mint or off-white) distinct from the page’s lavender. At the top of the card, a small heading like “Related spreads” is left-aligned, followed by a vertical list of related spread items, each with enough line spacing and possibly small thumbnail or icon to preserve the moderate density. The list typically occupies around 15–20% of the viewport height, extending further if many items are present, but remains visually secondary to the main spread detail above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked card within the single-column layout; scrolls with the page and maintains the same maximum width and side alignment as the main card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list is positioned directly beneath the related-spreads-list card, with a consistent vertical gap of about 16–24px. It mirrors the visual treatment of the related-spreads card: a rounded, pastel-backed card aligned to the same centered column, with 16–24px internal padding. A heading such as “Related articles” anchors the top-left of the card, followed by a vertical list of article titles (and optional short descriptions) stacked with comfortable spacing. This card typically appears as the last major element in the page’s content column, giving users a clear sense of completion at the bottom of the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, final card in the vertical stack; scrolls off-screen like any other content, adhering to the column’s max width and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears in place of content while data is being fetched. When used with spread-detail-loader, it is centered horizontally within the main content column and vertically within the area where the main spread-detail card would normally sit—roughly in the middle of the viewport beneath the navigation pills. It may present as a whimsical spinner or animated tarot card icon within a small, rounded container or simply floating on the lavender background. When used with related-content-loader, a similar indicator appears centered in the space reserved for the related content cards, occupying a compact, self-contained area with ample whitespace around it so the page still feels airy and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the space of the card it represents (main detail or related content), but remains centered in the single-column flow; does not fix to the viewport edges or follow the user on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-message-panel is displayed when loading the spread detail fails. It appears in the exact vertical position where the main spread-detail card would normally be, directly beneath the favorites-navigation-link and above the related content section. The panel itself is a rounded card with a slightly more saturated background (e.g., soft pink or warm cream) to signal an issue while still fitting the playful, handcrafted aesthetic. It spans the same width as the main card and includes 16–24px of padding, with a clear error headline at the top and explanatory text below. Optional retry or navigation actions sit at the bottom, aligned left or centered. Lavender background remains visible around the card, maintaining the same section spacing so the overall page rhythm is unchanged even in error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card occupying the main spread-detail region in the single-column stack; it replaces the main card in the flow rather than overlaying it, and scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

name: Tarot Card Meanings Index
title: Tarot Card Meanings Index
description: Browse and search tarot cards by arcana, suit, and name, then open each card’s detailed meaning.

core_features: Display a tarot card index using getTarotCardsIndex with optional filters for arcana type and suit and an optional sortBy parameter.
Provide a search form that submits a query via URL parameters and uses searchTarotCards to return matching tarot cards by name.
Allow users to filter the index by arcana type and suit, updating the URL parameters and reloading the index via getTarotCardsIndex.
Allow users to change the sort order of the tarot card index (for example, by name or by arcana and number) using getTarotCardsIndex with the sortBy parameter.
Render search results or index results as a list of tarot cards, each linking to its tarot_card_detail.html page with the appropriate id parameter.
Show an empty-state message when no tarot cards are returned from getTarotCardsIndex or searchTarotCards for the current URL state.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open the Tarot Card Meanings Index from site navigation
1) User clicks the 'Tarot Card Meanings' link in the header or footer.
2) Browser loads tarot_card_meanings.html without query, arcana, suit, or sortBy URL parameters.
3) Page state manager reads URL parameters (all empty) and sets mode to index.
4) Page calls getTarotCardsIndex with an empty filters object and a default sortBy value.
5) The tarot card list component renders all returned TarotCard items with links to tarot_card_detail.html?id=<TarotCard.id>.

Workflow: Use the search field on this page to look up a tarot card by name
1) User types text into the search input and submits the search form.
2) The search form submits a GET request to tarot_card_meanings.html, setting the query URL parameter to the entered value and clearing arcana, suit, and sortBy parameters as needed.
3) On page load, the state manager detects a non-empty query parameter and sets mode to search.
4) Page calls searchTarotCards with query taken from the URL and a maxResults value derived from a configurable setting.
5) The tarot card list component renders the array of TarotCard results returned by searchTarotCards.
6) If the user clears the search input and resubmits, the search form removes the query parameter from the URL, the page reloads in index mode, and getTarotCardsIndex is called instead.

Workflow: Filter tarot cards by arcana and suit
1) User opens the arcana/suit filter controls and selects a value for arcana (or none) and optionally a suit (or none).
2) User submits the filter form.
3) The filter form submits a GET request to tarot_card_meanings.html with arcana and suit URL parameters set according to the selected values and query removed (if present) so the page returns to index mode.
4) On page load, the state manager reads arcana and suit parameters, sets mode to index, and constructs a filters object for getTarotCardsIndex (mapping arcana to arcana_type and including suit if present).
5) Page calls getTarotCardsIndex with the filters object and the current sortBy value (if any).
6) The tarot card list component renders the filtered TarotCard collection.

Workflow: Change sort order for the tarot card index
1) User selects a sort option (e.g., alphabetical or arcana/number order) in the sort controls while no query is active.
2) The sort controls update the sortBy URL parameter and preserve current arcana and suit parameters.
3) Browser reloads tarot_card_meanings.html with the new sortBy parameter.
4) The state manager reads arcana, suit, and sortBy parameters, sets mode to index, and calls getTarotCardsIndex with the corresponding filters and sortBy value.
5) The tarot card list component re-renders the TarotCard collection in the new order.

Workflow: Arrive from another page with a pre-populated search query
1) User interacts with a search element on index.html that navigates them to tarot_card_meanings.html with a query URL parameter.
2) Browser loads tarot_card_meanings.html with query set and arcana, suit, and sortBy either absent or preserved depending on the navigation.
3) The state manager detects the non-empty query parameter, sets mode to search, and clears any conflicting filter state in the UI.
4) Page calls searchTarotCards using the query parameter.
5) The tarot card list component displays matching TarotCard items.
6) If the user modifies filters, the filter form submission removes the query parameter, returning the page to index mode and switching to getTarotCardsIndex.

Workflow: Open a tarot card detail page from the index or search results
1) User clicks on a tarot card name in the list.
2) The click handler reads the TarotCard.id bound to that list item.
3) Browser navigates to tarot_card_detail.html?id=<TarotCard.id> using standard link navigation.
4) The tarot card meanings index state is encoded in the URL parameters, so using the browser back button returns the user to the same index/search state.
interactions:
Interaction: Search input change
- User types text into the search input.
- The component may optionally call searchTarotCards with the current input and a small maxResults to show inline suggestions without changing the main URL state.
- Suggestions are displayed in a suggestions panel; selecting a suggestion populates the search input and can optionally trigger a full search form submission.

Interaction: Search form submit
- User presses Enter or clicks a search button.
- The search form constructs a URL for tarot_card_meanings.html with query set to the current input value and arcana, suit, and sortBy either cleared or preserved based on design rules (for example, clearing filters when search is applied).
- Browser reloads the page using a GET request; the state manager then calls searchTarotCards.
- The main card list updates to show only the search results.

Interaction: Clear search
- User clicks a 'Clear search' control within the search form.
- The control resets the search input to empty and triggers a navigation to tarot_card_meanings.html without the query parameter (preserving or resetting other parameters as designed).
- On reload, the state manager switches to index mode and calls getTarotCardsIndex.

Interaction: Change arcana filter
- User selects an arcana value from a filter control (or selects an option indicating no arcana filter).
- The filter component updates its internal state and, on form submission, updates the arcana URL parameter accordingly and removes the query parameter to avoid conflicting modes.
- Browser reloads, and getTarotCardsIndex is called with filters.arcana_type derived from URL arcana.

Interaction: Change suit filter
- User selects a suit value from a filter control (or an option indicating no suit filter).
- On submission, suit is written to the URL parameter and query is removed.
- Page reloads; state manager reads suit and arcana parameters and calls getTarotCardsIndex with matching filters.

Interaction: Reset filters
- User clicks a 'Reset filters' control.
- The control navigates to tarot_card_meanings.html without arcana or suit parameters but preserves sortBy if desired.
- Page reloads; getTarotCardsIndex is called with an empty filters object.

Interaction: Change sort order
- User selects a sort option from a sort control when in index mode (no query parameter).
- The sort control updates sortBy in the URL while preserving arcana and suit.
- Page reloads; getTarotCardsIndex is called with the existing filters and new sortBy, and the list re-renders.

Interaction: Click tarot card list item
- User clicks a card name or row in the results list.
- The click handler reads the associated TarotCard.id from the bound data.
- Browser navigates to tarot_card_detail.html?id=<TarotCard.id>.

Interaction: Empty state handling
- After a call to getTarotCardsIndex or searchTarotCards returns an empty array, the list component hides the card list and shows an empty-state message.
- The empty-state component offers actions such as clearing filters or returning to the full index by updating URL parameters and triggering a page reload.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled entirely via URL parameters and the two assigned interfaces. On initial load and on each navigation, the state manager parses URLSearchParams for query, arcana, suit, and sortBy. If query is present and non-empty, the page enters search mode: the search input is populated from query, filter controls visually indicate that arcana and suit filters are inactive or cleared, and searchTarotCards is called with the query value and a maxResults value obtained from configuration. The tarot card list binds directly to the array returned from searchTarotCards. When query is absent or empty, the page enters index mode: arcana and suit parameters (if present) are mapped into a filters object for getTarotCardsIndex, where arcana maps to arcana_type and suit maps directly to suit. If arcana or suit are missing, the corresponding filter fields are omitted from the filters object. The sortBy parameter, when present, is passed directly to getTarotCardsIndex; when absent, a default sort order is used. Any user interaction that changes search, filters, or sortBy results in navigation to tarot_card_meanings.html with an updated query string: submitting the search form sets query (and usually clears arcana and suit), submitting filter changes sets arcana and/or suit and clears query, and changing sort updates sortBy while preserving the current mode. After each navigation, the page re-runs the same initialization logic, ensuring that the visible UI (search input value, selected filters, active sort option) always reflects the current URL and that the card list always corresponds to either getTarotCardsIndex or searchTarotCards results based on the presence of query.

components.functionality: Central coordinator that reads query, arcana, suit, and sortBy from the URL, determines whether the page is in search mode or index mode, triggers the appropriate interface call (searchTarotCards or getTarotCardsIndex), and exposes the resulting TarotCard collection and derived state (active filters, active sort, search term) to other components.

Provides an input field for users to type a tarot card name and submit a search. On submit, constructs a URL to tarot_card_meanings.html with the query parameter set and with any conflicting filter parameters adjusted, causing the page to reload in search mode. Also supports clearing the search, which removes query from the URL.

Optional, inline suggestions panel that watches the search input value and calls searchTarotCards for short, non-empty queries to display a limited list of matching tarot cards as suggestions. Selecting a suggestion copies its name into the search input and can optionally submit the search form to navigate using the selected name as the query parameter.

Renders arcana and suit filter controls that reflect the current URL parameters. When the user changes values and submits the filters, the component constructs a URL to tarot_card_meanings.html with arcana and suit parameters set according to the selected values, removes query to return to index mode, and allows sortBy to be preserved. This causes a reload where getTarotCardsIndex is called with matching filters.

Displays available sort options for the card index and synchronizes the selected option with the sortBy URL parameter. When the user chooses a sort option, the component updates sortBy in the URL (preserving arcana and suit) and triggers a reload so that getTarotCardsIndex is called with the selected sort order. In search mode, the control can be disabled or ignored if sorting is not applied to searchTarotCards results.

Shows a concise summary of the current state, such as whether the user is viewing search results or a filtered index, which arcana and suit filters are active, which sort option is active, and how many TarotCard items are currently displayed. Provides quick actions to clear the search or reset filters by navigating to tarot_card_meanings.html with adjusted URL parameters.

Displays the collection of TarotCard items returned either from getTarotCardsIndex or searchTarotCards, depending on the current mode determined by the state manager. Each item shows key card metadata (such as its name and high-level attributes) and includes a link that navigates to tarot_card_detail.html?id=<TarotCard.id>. If the bound TarotCard array is empty, the component delegates to the empty-state component instead of rendering the list.

Renders a contextual message when the TarotCard array provided by the state manager is empty, indicating that no tarot cards match the current search or filter settings. Provides actions to clear search or reset filters, implemented as navigations that modify URL parameters and cause the page to reload with a broader getTarotCardsIndex call.

Utility component used within each tarot card list item to construct the correct href for the tarot card detail page by binding TarotCard.id into the URL tarot_card_detail.html?id=<TarotCard.id>. Ensures consistent navigation from any index or search result item to the corresponding detail page.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s stacked, single-column blog layout. At the very top of the page, a full-width watercolor illustration header spans the screen, with the site logo and any global controls embedded in it. Directly beneath is a rounded navigation pill bar, then a rounded breadcrumb pill. Below the breadcrumb sits a large, rounded “article-style” content card that holds all tarot index-specific components in a clear vertical reading order: page title and short intro at the top, then the tarot search form, then the search suggestions panel (when active) hanging directly below the search field, followed by arcana/suit filter controls, then a compact summary/sort row, and finally the tarot card index list or the empty state message in the main body area. Card-detail navigation links live inside each result row within the results list. The URL state manager has no visual presence and simply governs state behind the scenes.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s stacked, single-column blog layout. At the very top of the page, a full-width watercolor illustration header spans the screen, with the site logo and any global controls embedded in it. Directly beneath is a rounded navigation pill bar, then a rounded breadcrumb pill. Below the breadcrumb sits a large, rounded “article-style” content card that holds all tarot index-specific components in a clear vertical reading order: page title and short intro at the top, then the tarot search form, then the search suggestions panel (when active) hanging directly below the search field, followed by arcana/suit filter controls, then a compact summary/sort row, and finally the tarot card index list or the empty state message in the main body area. Card-detail navigation links live inside each result row within the results list. The URL state manager has no visual presence and simply governs state behind the scenes.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The illustrated header occupies roughly the top quarter to one third of the initial viewport, similar to the mockup. The global navigation pill and breadcrumb pill each use about 8–10% of the viewport height, with 16–24px of vertical lavender background space separating each band. The tarot index content card occupies the remaining space and continues vertically as needed. Inside this main card, the top title/intro and search form collectively use around 15–20% of the card’s vertical space; the arcana/suit filter controls take about another 10–15% (depending on wrapping), the summary/sort row is a slim horizontal band, and the tarot card index list occupies the majority of the card’s height. The search suggestions panel temporarily overlays a small area directly beneath the search field, pushing the filters visually downward. All elements sit within a centered content column at a fixed max width (around 960px), with 16–24px padding inside any pill or card component and 16–24px vertical gaps between stacked sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Macro-level spacing is generous: the illustrated header, nav pill, breadcrumb pill, and main card are each clearly separated by visible lavender background space. Within the main card, content density is moderate and reading-focused: search, filters, and list items are comfortably spaced but not sparse. Text uses normal paragraph spacing, and controls (chips, buttons, links) have breathing room so labels don’t crowd pill edges. The search box is large and inviting with ample padding; filters and sort controls use pill-style buttons spaced by 8–16px. The results list items are compact cards or rows with 12–16px vertical padding, keeping the list scannable yet clearly grouped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first captured by the colorful watercolor header at the top, then moves to any global nav, then into the breadcrumb that anchors the user’s location. From there, attention drops into the main tarot index card where the bold page title sits at the top, followed immediately by the prominent search form as the key interactive element. When active, the search suggestions panel appears directly under the search field, becoming the next focal point. Below that, arcana and suit filter controls draw the eye as a set of colorful pills, followed by a more subdued summary/sort row. Finally, focus moves into the vertically flowing tarot card index list where each row leads the eye left-to-right from card name and metadata to the card-detail navigation link on the far right. The page thus supports a strong, top-to-bottom flow with occasional left-to-right micro-scans in each row.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a soft lavender background, with a single centered content column capped by a watercolor-style header illustration that gives a dreamy, astrology-themed mood. Beneath the header, a rounded navigation pill and then a breadcrumb pill form horizontal bands that guide the user into the main content. The Tarot Card Meanings Index lives inside a large, rounded white or very pale mint card, centered in the column with generous padding. At the top of this card is a friendly, colorful title and a one-line description, followed by a wide, pill-shaped search bar that visually anchors the interactive experience. Directly under the search bar, a dropdown-like suggestions panel appears when the user types, floating over the background of the card and nudging attention downward. Beneath that, a band of pill-style arcana and suit filters stretches across the card, wrapping gracefully on smaller screens. A slim row under the filters shows a results summary on the left and sorting options on the right. The lower portion of the card is dominated by the tarot card index list: a vertical stack of rounded rows, each with card name and metadata on the left and a subtle ‘View meaning’ link on the right, maintaining the playful, handcrafted style. When no results match, the list area is replaced by a centered, friendly empty-state message. Throughout, spacing follows an 8px-based rhythm and the pastel color palette with bright accents supports the whimsical, fantasy blog feel while keeping the layout clear and readable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager operates invisibly at the page level and does not occupy any visual space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind all other components, synchronizing search inputs, selected filters, sort options, and list state with the URL. It has no rendered container, no padding, and no impact on spacing; all visible gaps and alignments are entirely dictated by the surrounding UI components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical component; it does not render any element or affect the spatial flow directly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tarot search form is positioned inside the main tarot index content card near its top, immediately below the page title and intro text. It is centered horizontally within the fixed-width content column and spans roughly 80–90% of that width on desktop, leaving comfortable side margins of lavender or card background. The form is a single, large pill-shaped search field with an embedded search icon on the left and placeholder text in the center. It has generous internal padding (about 16–20px vertically and 24px horizontally), making it visually prominent and easy to interact with. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the intro text and the top of the search pill, and another 8–12px gap below the search pill, where the search suggestions panel will appear. On smaller screens, the search form still remains full-width within the card, with consistent top and bottom spacing, staying as the primary focal element above filters and results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content card; it moves naturally with page scroll. It acts as the anchor for the search-suggestions panel, which is visually attached to its bottom edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search suggestions panel is visually anchored directly beneath the tarot search form’s input field, sharing the same horizontal alignment and width as the search pill (roughly 80–90% of the content column). When visible, it appears as a floating, rounded-rectangle dropdown panel with a subtle shadow, layered above the main content card’s background but below the search field’s focus state. It starts immediately at the bottom edge of the search input, with no visible gap or a very small 4–8px gap to keep a soft, airy feel. The panel extends downward just enough to display several suggestion rows (each row with 8–12px vertical padding), but never beyond the top of the arcana/suit filter controls; if suggestions are longer, the panel becomes internally scrollable. Left and right padding inside the panel is about 16–20px, keeping suggestion text away from the edges. The components directly beneath (filters, summary bar, list) appear visually pushed downward but do not actually change position; the panel overlays them while active. On narrow screens, the panel still aligns perfectly under the search field, matching its width and maintaining the dropdown effect.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays content below the search form when active; positioned relative to the search input, not fixed to the viewport. Appears and disappears based on search focus and input, without affecting the underlying layout’s structural spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The arcana and suit filter controls form a full-width horizontal band inside the main tarot index card, positioned below the search form (and below the suggestions panel when that is visible). There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the search area and the top of this filters band. The filters are presented as a collection of pill-shaped buttons or toggles, arranged from left to right within the content column. On wider screens, Major/Minor Arcana filters appear first, followed by suit filters, all in a single or double row, with 8–12px horizontal and vertical gaps between pills. The entire filters block respects the card’s inner padding, so pills do not touch the card edges. The band itself visually reads as part of the card body rather than a separate card: no heavy border, just the pills on a light background. On smaller screens, pills naturally wrap to multiple lines while staying left-aligned, and the bottom of the filter section sits about 16px above the summary/sort row below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked section within the main content card. It grows vertically as pills wrap on smaller widths, pushing the summary/sort row and the results list downward in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls occupy the upper-right portion of a slim horizontal row just beneath the arcana/suit filter controls. This row spans the full width of the content card and sits about 16px below the filters. On the left side of the row is the results summary bar, while the sort controls align to the right edge, creating a balanced left-right composition. The sort controls may be a pill-shaped dropdown or segmented control, with a compact footprint: about 32–40px tall and sized just wide enough to hold their label (for example, ‘Sort: A–Z’), leaving a small margin from the card’s right inner edge. There is approximately 8–12px of vertical spacing between the bottom of this row and the start of the tarot card index list. On small screens, the sort controls stack below the summary bar: the summary remains at the top of the row, left-aligned, and the sort control moves directly beneath it, still right-aligned or full-width with a small margin above the list, preserving a clean vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the summary/sort row. It aligns right on larger screens and reflows to a stacked alignment on smaller screens; otherwise scrolls with the page like standard inline content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary bar is placed directly beneath the arcana/suit filter controls, sharing a horizontal row with the sort controls. It sits on the left side of this row, aligned with the card’s left inner padding. The bar is relatively low in height, approximately 24–32px tall, and spans about 50–60% of the card’s width before yielding space to the sort controls on the right. It can be rendered as either plain text or a subtle pill-shaped badge with soft background color, containing a concise summary such as ‘24 cards • Major Arcana • Suit: Cups’. Vertical margins are modest: about 16px above it (from filters) and 8–12px below it (above the results list). The summary’s typography is slightly smaller and more subdued than the main title but clear enough to support quick scanning at a glance. On small screens, when the sort controls wrap to the next line, the summary bar remains the top element of this section, left-aligned and occupying the full available width above the sort controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static textual band that sits in the normal vertical flow just above the results list and below the filters. It reflows together with the sort controls but does not overlay or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tarot card index list is the dominant block in the lower portion of the main tarot index card. It begins approximately 8–12px beneath the summary/sort row and extends downward, occupying all remaining vertical space within the card and continuing as the user scrolls. The list spans nearly the full width of the card’s inner content area, leaving consistent padding on both sides (16–24px). Each list item is a horizontally oriented, rounded mini-card or row with a soft, slightly elevated appearance: about 56–72px tall on average, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. Items are stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps between them, allowing the lavender card background to peek through. Within each row, content is aligned left-to-right: card name in a slightly bolder, colorful text on the left, followed by smaller metadata (arcana, suit, maybe a brief keyword) below or to the right, and the card-detail navigation link occupying the far right side. The list clearly follows the overall pastel, handcrafted aesthetic, with occasional colored accents to differentiate suits or arcana. On small screens, each item still fills the width but may increase its height slightly to accommodate wrapped text, while preserving the consistent vertical spacing between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Main scrolling content inside the page; it flows vertically and may extend beyond the initial viewport, requiring page scroll. Each row is fully clickable or contains a clear navigation link to drill into card details, but the list itself remains a standard stacked section with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message is positioned exactly where the tarot card index list would normally appear, starting at the same vertical point beneath the summary/sort row. When there are no results, the list is replaced entirely by this message. The empty state occupies the central portion of the main content card, with generous padding around it: about 32–48px of space above and below the message inside the card. The content is center-aligned both horizontally and, where vertical space allows, vertically within the card’s body area. It may include a small whimsical icon or illustration at the top, a slightly larger heading (e.g., ‘No cards match your search’), and one or two lines of explanatory copy, followed by a subtle suggestion link or button to clear filters. The message block itself does not span the full width; instead, it uses around 60–70% of the card’s width and is centered to enhance focus and maintain the airy feel. The lavender page background still frames the outer card, preserving the stacked-card aesthetic. When results become available again, this message disappears and the tarot card index list reappears in its place.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the tarot-card-index-list section: only one is visible at a time. It is static and scrolls with the page, occupying the same structural space as the list without overlaying other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The card detail navigation link is embedded within each row of the tarot card index list rather than existing as a separate block. Within a result row, it is positioned toward the far right edge, aligned vertically to the center of the row’s content. The link appears as a compact, pill-like text button or an underlined text label (e.g., ‘View meaning’ or ‘Open’) accompanied by a small right-pointing chevron icon, using a bright accent color (purple or green) that stands out against the softer background. It maintains a small margin from the row’s right inner padding (about 12–16px) and does not extend beyond the row’s vertical bounds. In some variants, the entire row can be clickable, but the explicit link still provides a clear focal target and reinforces the navigation affordance. On narrow screens, if content wraps, the link may move under the title/metadata block but remains right-aligned within the row, preserving a left-to-right reading order that ends in an action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element anchored inside each results-list item. It does not affect overall layout structure beyond reserving space on the right side of each row. It scrolls with the list and does not float or stick independently.

name: Tarot Card Detail
title: Tarot Card Detail
description: Shows detailed information for a single tarot card identified by a URL parameter, including upright and reversed meanings, symbolism, journaling prompts, and tools to save shortened prompts as notes, plus navigation to related spreads, articles, and the notes list.

core_features: Load tarot card details and journaling prompts using getTarotCardDetail based on the URL parameter id.
Display core card information, including arcana type, suit, number, keywords, and symbolism.
Show upright and reversed meanings with user-controlled toggling between positions.
Display a Journaling Prompts section listing prompts associated with the loaded tarot card.
Provide a Save as Note action for each journaling prompt that opens a modal/popup to edit a shortened version of the prompt.
Enforce a maximum character length for the shortened prompt content before allowing note creation.
Create notes from shortened prompts using createNote, including source metadata linking back to the original tarot card prompt.
Provide inline confirmation after saving a note and a View All Notes navigation to notes.html.
Offer navigation back to the tarot card index filtered by the card’s arcana type via tarot_card_meanings.html with an arcana URL parameter.
Provide a link to tarot_spreads.html pre-filtered by the current card name via a query URL parameter.
Surface related articles as clickable items that navigate to article.html with an id URL parameter for each related article.
user_workflows:
1) View tarot card detail from index:
   1. User clicks a card from tarot_card_meanings.html, which navigates to tarot_card_detail.html?id={TarotCard.id}.
   2. On page load, the tarot-card-detail-loader component reads id from the URL and calls getTarotCardDetail({ tarotCardId: id }).
   3. When the response resolves, the page renders the card-summary-header with the card’s name, arcana type, suit, and any other available metadata.
   4. The card-meanings-section shows the upright meaning by default, with controls to switch to the reversed meaning.
   5. The journaling-prompts-list displays all prompts returned in getTarotCardDetail.prompts in their configured order.

2) Toggle between upright and reversed meanings:
   1. User clicks on a control in the card-meanings-section to select Upright or Reversed.
   2. The component updates its internal selected-position state (optionally reflected in a URL parameter such as position=upright or position=reversed).
   3. The visible meaning text updates to show either card.upright_meaning or card.reversed_meaning. Symbolism remains available regardless of position.
   4. No additional API calls are made; the displayed data comes from the original getTarotCardDetail response.

3) Save a shortened journaling prompt as a note:
   1. In the journaling-prompts-list, the user clicks Save as Note on a specific prompt.
   2. The prompt-note-modal opens, prefilled with an editable version of the prompt text (which the user can modify) and optional title field.
   3. As the user types, the modal shows a running character count for the content and validates that the content length does not exceed the maximum allowed characters.
   4. If the content exceeds the limit, the component shows a validation message and disables the Save button until the content is within the limit.
   5. When the user submits, the modal calls createNote({ title, content, sourceType: 'tarot_card_prompt', sourceReferenceId: prompt.id, tags }) where tags may include dynamically constructed tags related to the card and/or prompt.
   6. On success, the modal closes, the journaling-prompts-list receives the created Note object, and an inline note-save-confirmation appears near that prompt.
   7. The user can click View All Notes in the confirmation to navigate to notes.html.

4) Access Tarot Card Detail from Notes and return to index:
   1. On notes.html, the user selects a note that originated from a tarot card prompt, which navigates to tarot_card_detail.html?id={TarotCard.id}.
   2. The page loads card and prompt data via getTarotCardDetail as usual.
   3. The user can review meanings and prompts, and optionally create additional notes.
   4. When finished, the user clicks Back to card index in the card-page-breadcrumbs component.
   5. The page navigates to tarot_card_meanings.html?arcana={card.arcana_type}, using the loaded card’s arcana_type to determine the filter.

5) Navigate to related spreads and articles for the card:
   1. After reading the card meanings, the user scrolls to the related-spreads-link-block and clicks a link such as “View spreads featuring this card.”
   2. The page navigates to tarot_spreads.html?query={encoded card.name}, where the target page handles filtering based on the query parameter.
   3. In the related-articles-list section, the user sees a dynamically supplied list of Article items associated with the card (data is bound into the component without additional API calls on this page).
   4. The user clicks a related article item, which navigates to article.html?id={Article.id}.
interactions: On initial load, the tarot-card-detail-loader reads the id URL parameter and triggers getTarotCardDetail; if id is missing or invalid, it displays an error state with no calls to the interfaces.
Clicking Back to card index in the card-page-breadcrumbs constructs a URL tarot_card_meanings.html?arcana={card.arcana_type} and navigates the browser there.

Switching between Upright and Reversed tabs in the card-meanings-section updates which meaning text is shown without additional API calls; optionally, the selected position can be synchronized with a position URL parameter to preserve state on refresh.
Clicking Save as Note on a prompt opens the prompt-note-modal with that prompt’s data; the modal is bound to the specific TarotCardPrompt selected.

Typing in the shortened prompt content field in the prompt-note-modal updates the displayed character count and validation status; if the length is within the allowed maximum, the Save button becomes enabled.
Submitting the modal’s form calls createNote with the current field values; during the call, the modal shows a loading state and disables inputs to prevent duplicate submissions.

If createNote returns successfully, the modal closes, and the journaling-prompts-list marks the corresponding prompt as having a saved note (using the Note returned) and displays the note-save-confirmation.
If createNote fails, the modal stays open and shows an error message, allowing the user to retry or cancel without losing typed content.
Clicking View All Notes in the note-save-confirmation navigates to notes.html with no additional parameters, relying on that page to show the full notes list.
Clicking the related spreads link navigates to tarot_spreads.html?query={card.name}, using the currently loaded card’s name.
Clicking a related article item in the related-articles-list navigates to article.html?id={Article.id} for the selected article.
If the getTarotCardDetail call returns no prompts, the journaling-prompts-list displays an empty state message and hides Save as Note controls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from URL parameters and API responses. The id URL parameter (tarot_card_detail.html?id={TarotCard.id}) determines which card to load. On load or when id changes, the tarot-card-detail-loader calls getTarotCardDetail({ tarotCardId: id }) and updates the page with the returned card and prompts. The arcana_type value from TarotCard is used when constructing the Back to card index link as tarot_card_meanings.html?arcana={card.arcana_type}. The card.name value is used when constructing the related spreads link as tarot_spreads.html?query={card.name}. Each related article link uses the Article.id bound into the related-articles-list to construct article.html?id={Article.id}. Optional intra-page UI state, such as which meaning position is selected, can be represented by a position URL parameter (e.g., position=upright or position=reversed); when present, the card-meanings-section reads this parameter to set the initial tab, and when the user changes tabs, the component can update the URL accordingly without reloading the page. Note creation state is transient: after createNote is called and returns a Note, the Note object is used locally to show confirmation and mark a prompt as saved, but no persistent state beyond what the Notes page manages is stored here. No state is stored in localStorage; any state that must survive page reloads is encoded into URL parameters and re-derived from getTarotCardDetail or navigation.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the id URL parameter, calling getTarotCardDetail to fetch the TarotCard and associated TarotCardPrompt list, managing loading and error states, and passing the data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when the component mounts; reads id from the URL and, if present, calls getTarotCardDetail({ tarotCardId: id }).
onIdChange: Triggered when the id URL parameter changes (e.g., via internal navigation); refetches data via getTarotCardDetail.
onLoadSuccess: Invoked when getTarotCardDetail returns successfully; provides card and prompts data to dependent components.
onLoadError: Invoked when getTarotCardDetail fails or id is missing/invalid; sets an error state that child components can use to display appropriate messages.

components.functionality: Displays the main identifying information for the tarot card (name, arcana type, suit, number, keywords) and integrates the Back to card index navigation link.
event_handlers: onBackToIndexClick: Constructs and navigates to tarot_card_meanings.html?arcana={card.arcana_type} when the user clicks Back to card index.
onCardInfoRender: Runs when TarotCard data becomes available to ensure the header is updated, including any SEO-relevant metadata such as document title.

components.functionality: Shows upright and reversed meanings for the card, along with symbolism, and allows the user to switch between positions using tabs or toggles.
event_handlers: onTabChange: Updates the selected position (upright or reversed) based on user interaction and optionally updates a position URL parameter to reflect this state.
onInitialPositionResolve: On mount, reads any position URL parameter and sets the default active tab; if the parameter is absent or invalid, defaults to upright.
onContentRender: Ensures that the appropriate meaning text (upright_meaning or reversed_meaning) is visible based on the current tab.

components.functionality: Lists all TarotCardPrompt items associated with the card, in the order defined by order_index, each with its full prompt text and a Save as Note action.
event_handlers: onPromptRender: Displays each prompt text from TarotCardPrompt.text and associates each list item with its TarotCardPrompt.id.
onSavePromptClick: Triggered when the user clicks Save as Note; opens prompt-note-modal with the selected prompt’s data.
onNoteCreatedForPrompt: Receives a Note object after createNote succeeds and associates it with the relevant TarotCardPrompt, enabling a Saved indicator and displaying note-save-confirmation.
onEmptyPrompts: If the prompts array is empty, shows an appropriate empty state messaging and disables Save as Note actions.

components.functionality: Provides a popup form to create a new Note from a selected TarotCardPrompt, allowing the user to edit and shorten the prompt content to meet the character limit before saving.
event_handlers: onOpen: Initializes the modal with the selected TarotCardPrompt.text as the starting content (or a trimmed version if necessary) and populates any derived default tags based on the card or prompt.
onChangeTitle: Updates the local title field state as the user edits the optional title.
onChangeContent: Updates the local content state, recalculates character count, and triggers validation for the maximum allowed length.
onValidationCheck: Evaluates whether the content length is within the allowed limit; if not, sets validation errors and disables submission.
onSubmit: When validation passes, calls createNote({ title, content, sourceType: 'tarot_card_prompt', sourceReferenceId: selectedPrompt.id, tags }) and enters a loading state.
onSubmitSuccess: Receives the created Note from createNote; closes the modal and notifies journaling-prompts-list via onNoteCreatedForPrompt.
onSubmitError: Displays an error message within the modal and allows the user to retry without clearing their input.
onCancel: Closes the modal without calling createNote and discards unsaved changes.

components.functionality: Shows inline confirmation that a Note was created successfully for a given TarotCardPrompt and provides navigation to the full notes list.
event_handlers: onShow: Triggered when a Note is successfully created for a prompt; displays a confirmation message associated with that prompt.
onViewAllNotesClick: Navigates to notes.html when the user clicks the View All Notes link.
onDismiss: Allows the user to hide or collapse the confirmation message without affecting the underlying Note.

components.functionality: Provides a call-to-action link to view tarot spreads that feature or relate to the current card, by passing the card name as a query parameter to the spreads page.
event_handlers: onRender: Constructs the link URL tarot_spreads.html?query={card.name} based on the current TarotCard data.
onClick: Navigates to the constructed tarot_spreads.html URL when the user clicks the link.

components.functionality: Displays a list of related Article items associated with the tarot card, allowing the user to navigate to each article’s detail page.
event_handlers: onArticlesDataBind: Receives an array of Article entities from the page context or server-side rendering (no additional API calls from this page) and renders them as a list.
onArticleClick: Navigates to article.html?id={Article.id} when the user clicks a specific related article item.
onEmptyArticles: If no related articles are provided, shows an empty state message or hides the section.

components.functionality: Provides basic breadcrumb-style navigation for the tarot card detail page, including a link back to the tarot card index page filtered by arcana.
event_handlers: onRender: Uses TarotCard.arcana_type to construct the Back to card index URL as tarot_card_meanings.html?arcana={card.arcana_type}.
onBackClick: Navigates to the constructed tarot_card_meanings.html URL when the user clicks the breadcrumb link.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single centered column within a fixed max-width content area (approx. 960px), stacked vertically from top to bottom. Above this Tarot Card Detail page content there is the site’s illustrated header and primary nav (from the mockup). Within the page itself, the order from top to bottom is: (1) card-page-breadcrumbs as a full-width pill bar, (2) one main content card that visually behaves like an 'article card'. Inside that main card, components appear in a vertical reading order: card-summary-header at the top, then card-meanings-section (tabs), then journaling-prompts-list (with note-save-confirmation inline where relevant), followed by related-spreads-link-block, and finally related-articles-list at the bottom. The data loader and modal sit above this flow as overlays when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single centered column within a fixed max-width content area (approx. 960px), stacked vertically from top to bottom. Above this Tarot Card Detail page content there is the site’s illustrated header and primary nav (from the mockup). Within the page itself, the order from top to bottom is: (1) card-page-breadcrumbs as a full-width pill bar, (2) one main content card that visually behaves like an 'article card'. Inside that main card, components appear in a vertical reading order: card-summary-header at the top, then card-meanings-section (tabs), then journaling-prompts-list (with note-save-confirmation inline where relevant), followed by related-spreads-link-block, and finally related-articles-list at the bottom. The data loader and modal sit above this flow as overlays when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The Tarot detail page follows the mockup’s proportional feeling: the global watercolor header at the very top visually occupies roughly the top quarter of the first viewport. Immediately below, the breadcrumb pill takes about 8–10% of initial viewport height, with 16–24px vertical margin separating it from both the header and the main content card. The main card for the tarot content then occupies the remaining height, easily extending beyond the fold. Inside this main card, each subsection (summary, meanings tabs, journaling, related content) gets 16–24px padding on all sides and 16px vertical spacing between subsections. The loader and modal, when active, take over visual focus by centering within the viewport and dimming the background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Adopt a moderate content density: macro-level elements (breadcrumb pill, the main content card, and any secondary cards) are clearly separated by generous lavender background gaps of 16–24px. Inside cards, text is set with normal paragraph spacing and line length, without excessive whitespace. Section headers within the main card (e.g., for meanings, journaling, related content) receive slightly more top and bottom padding than body text to create a clear hierarchy, but everything still feels like a continuous article-style read rather than isolated widgets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the watercolor illustration and site identity at the top, then drops down to the breadcrumb bar which anchors where the user is in the Tarot system. From there, the strong main content card with a bold, colorful card title in the card-summary-header becomes the primary focal point. Within that card, the eye moves down from the summary information to the tabbed meanings section (upright/reversed/symbolism), then to practical journaling prompts with inline save feedback, and finally to the related spreads link block and related articles list at the bottom. Overlays like the loader and prompt-note-modal interrupt this flow only temporarily, centered on the screen and clearly above all other content.

layout.overall_description: The Tarot Card Detail page sits within a playful, fantasy-flavored blog shell: a full-width lavender background with a watercolor illustration and site logo near the top, and a simple navigation band just below it. Centered on this stage is a single, fixed-width content column with softly rounded cards. Near the top of this column, a pill-shaped breadcrumb bar stretches almost the full width, floating on the lavender background with comfortable space above and below. Beneath it, the main tarot content appears as one large, rounded article-like card with a mint or pale panel background, generous 16–24px padding, and subtle shadow. At the top of this card, a bold, colorful title and key card summary details anchor the page. Directly below, a tabbed meanings area allows the user to switch between upright, reversed, and symbolism views without leaving the card. Scrolling down reveals a journaling prompts section, presented as a neat list of prompts with save actions; when a prompt is saved, a small inline feedback pill appears just beneath the relevant area. Lower down, a compact block of links invite the user to explore tarot spreads using this card, followed by a short, vertically stacked list of related articles. Throughout, elements are centered, aligned, and spaced to maintain a friendly, handcrafted blog feel. When loading or when saving a prompt note, overlays appear centered in the viewport—either as a translucent loading state over the main card or as a rounded modal form with its own padding—temporarily taking focus while the lavender surroundings still softly frame the experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears only while the tarot card detail data is being fetched. It overlays the main content column rather than the entire browser window. Visually, it sits centered both horizontally and vertically over the area where the main tarot content card will appear, within the fixed-width column. A semi-transparent lavender-tinted scrim covers the breadcrumb pill and the underlying main content card region, slightly dimming them while still allowing their shapes to be visible beneath. In the exact center of this masked region, a small rounded loader panel (about 280–320px wide) contains a spinner or animated icon and a short status message. This loader panel has 16–24px internal padding, rounded corners, and enough whitespace around the loader icon to feel calm and uncluttered. Once the data has loaded, this overlay fades out, revealing the breadcrumb and main content card beneath in their normal stacked arrangement.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main content column with a centered, semi-transparent scrim and a fixed-position loader panel within the viewport; removed from the layout flow when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The card-summary-header occupies the top portion of the main tarot content card, sitting directly below the card-page-breadcrumbs with a 16–24px margin of lavender background between them. It spans nearly the full width of the main content card, respecting 16–24px left and right padding within that card. The card title appears prominently near the top, aligned left within the main column, using a bright accent color (e.g., purple or green) and surrounded by a bit of extra vertical padding above and below (around 16px above, 8–12px below) to give it headline presence. Below the title, a single metadata row (e.g., card suit, arcana type, keywords, and maybe an icon of the card) runs horizontally, still within the same card, using a moderately dense layout: labels and values are spaced with 8–12px gaps so they feel readable and not crowded. If there is a thumbnail or symbolic illustration of the card, it is placed to the right of the summary text, at the same vertical level as the metadata, occupying roughly 25–30% of the card’s internal width on larger screens. On smaller screens, this thumbnail stacks above or below the text while preserving vertical flow. The bottom of the card-summary-header section transitions smoothly into the card-meanings-section, separated by about 16px of inner vertical spacing and possibly a faint divider line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main article card, participating in normal vertical flow; its height expands as needed for title and metadata content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The card-meanings-section sits immediately below the card-summary-header inside the same main tarot content card, becoming the next major block in the vertical reading order. It spans the full inner width of the card, maintaining the same left and right padding (16–24px) as the header. At the top of this section, a horizontal tab bar allows switching between views (e.g., Upright, Reversed, Symbolism). The tab bar is aligned left, occupies about 60–80% of the card’s width, and uses pill-shaped tabs with 8–12px internal padding, and 8–16px of space above and below. The active tab is emphasized with a stronger accent color and maybe a slightly larger pill. Below the tabs, the meanings content appears as a text block with normal line length and paragraph spacing, taking up at least one third to one half of the visible card height on first view, depending on content length. Paragraphs are stacked with 8–12px spacing between them, creating a comfortable reading rhythm. Any subheadings within this content (e.g., 'Love & Relationships', 'Career') are styled a step down from the page title and given a bit more top padding (12–16px) to break up long text. The bottom edge of this section leaves 16–24px of vertical space before the journaling-prompts-list begins, preserving the sense that these are distinct yet related sections within the same card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main content card; the tab bar stays at the top of this section but scrolls away with the page (no sticky behavior). Content height grows with text length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The journaling-prompts-list appears directly beneath the card-meanings-section within the same main tarot content card, separated by around 16–24px of vertical whitespace. A subsection heading such as 'Journaling Prompts' sits at the top of this block, left-aligned, using a smaller but still colorful heading style and extra top padding (16px) to signal a new section. Below this heading, prompts are arranged in a vertical list that spans the full internal width of the card, with each prompt presented either as a single paragraph line or as a short multi-line block. Each list item has 12–16px of padding above and below and at least 8–12px of vertical space between items, following the moderate density pattern. On the right side of each prompt row (or just beneath it on smaller screens), a compact 'Save as note' action button or icon pill is placed, aligned to the same horizontal grid as the text to keep the layout orderly. The bottom of the journaling-prompts-list leaves 16–24px of padding before the related-spreads-link-block, keeping the sections visually distinct while preserving the continuous article-like feeling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main content card; items stack in normal document flow and grow vertically based on the number and length of prompts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The prompt-note-modal appears when the user chooses to save or edit a journaling prompt. It overlays the entire viewport, centered both horizontally and vertically above all other components. A semi-transparent lavender or darkened scrim covers the full browser window, subtly dimming the illustrated header, navigation, breadcrumb bar, and main tarot content card while still allowing their shapes to show through. In the center, a rounded-rectangle modal panel appears, occupying about 60–70% of the main column width on desktop and up to 90% of the viewport width on smaller screens; its maximum width is around 480–600px to maintain an intimate scale. The modal has generous internal padding (24px on all sides), with the title at the top, a close icon aligned to the top-right corner, a multi-line textarea in the middle, and primary/secondary actions (e.g., Save / Cancel) aligned right at the bottom. Vertical spacing inside the modal follows the 8–16px scale, with more space above the title and below the content area so the form feels breathable. As it is centered in the viewport, scrolling the background does not move the modal; the user must explicitly close it to return to the normal vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport, with a full-screen scrim; removed from document flow and scrolls independently if content exceeds viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The note-save-confirmation is a small inline feedback element that appears close to where the user just interacted, inside the journaling-prompts-list section of the main tarot content card. When a prompt is successfully saved, a pill-shaped confirmation bar appears directly beneath that specific prompt’s row (and its Save button), aligned to the same left margin as the prompt text. This pill spans roughly 60–80% of the inner card width, leaving some lavender or card background visible to the right; it has 8–12px of internal padding, slightly rounded corners, and uses a bright but friendly accent color for the text (e.g., green for success). There is a modest vertical gap (4–8px) between the prompt row and the confirmation pill so it feels attached but separate. If multiple confirmations are present, each sits under its respective prompt, vertically stacked with the normal 8–12px spacing between prompts. The confirmation disappears or fades after a short duration or when the user takes a new action, without altering the overall structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element that temporarily occupies space within the normal layout flow beneath the relevant journaling prompt; it does not overlay or shift other sections beyond standard reflow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-spreads-link-block is placed below the journaling-prompts-list inside the main tarot content card, separated from it by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. It begins with a small heading such as 'Try these spreads with this card', aligned left with similar styling to the Journaling heading but slightly less prominent than the main card title. Under this heading, one or more link items are presented in a compact vertical stack or in a horizontal row that wraps; each link is styled like a pill or mini-card, with 8–12px of internal padding and rounded edges, and aligned to the left edge of the main text column. The entire block spans the full inner width of the card’s content area, but the links themselves may collectively occupy only 50–70% of that width, leaving comfortable negative space on the right to preserve the airy, handcrafted look. A 16px gap separates this block from the related-articles-list below so each section is clearly distinguishable while still feeling part of a single flowing article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block nested within the main content card; links stack or wrap within the given width and adjust naturally to responsive changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-list sits at the bottom of the main tarot content card, directly beneath the related-spreads-link-block with around 16–24px of vertical separation. It acts as the final section within this card, encouraging further reading. A heading such as 'Related Articles' appears at the top of this block, left-aligned, using the same heading level as the spreads block. Below, 3–5 related article entries are arranged in a simple vertical list, each spanning nearly the full inner width of the card with 12–16px of vertical padding. Each item contains a title (primary), optional short description, and possibly a subtle arrow or icon aligned at the right edge to indicate navigation. Items are separated by small gaps (8–12px) or faint divider lines to avoid a crowded feeling. The bottom of the related-articles-list leaves extra padding (24px) before the bottom edge of the main content card, giving the entire card a soft, finished boundary against the lavender background. Below this card, the lavender page background continues with a comfortable gap before any footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list contained at the bottom of the main content card; items expand vertically based on text length and follow normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The card-page-breadcrumbs component appears near the top of the page content column, directly beneath the site’s main navigation bar and above the main tarot content card. It is presented as a full-width pill-shaped bar that stretches almost the entire width of the centered column (roughly 90–95% of the max content width), with equal left and right margins to keep it visually centered. The breadcrumbs pill has rounded corners and a background color that contrasts gently with the lavender page background (such as mint or a lighter lavender), and it sits on the lavender backdrop with about 16–24px of margin above and below, creating a distinct band. Inside the pill, the breadcrumb trail (e.g., 'Home / Tarot / Major Arcana / The Fool') is left-aligned, with each segment spaced by 4–8px and a subtle chevron or slash separator. The vertical height of the pill is moderate, around 40–56px, providing enough padding so text does not touch the edges. This bar visually mirrors the mockup’s breadcrumb pattern: a strong horizontal strip that anchors the user’s sense of location and leads the eye down to the main content card immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation band within the main page flow; remains at the top of the content area and scrolls away with the rest of the page (no sticky behavior).

name: Compatibility
title: Compatibility
description: Page where users select two zodiac signs, view compatibility articles for that pair, sort the results, and navigate to full article details.

core_features: Two zodiac sign selectors that allow users to choose a first and second sign for compatibility comparison, mapped to the URL parameters signOne and signTwo.
A Show Results button that submits the selected sign pair, updates the URL parameters, and triggers a call to getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.
A compatibility results list that displays articles returned by getCompatibilityArticlesForPair for the current signOne, signTwo, and sortBy values.
Display of article metadata for each result, including items such as estimated reading time and comment count when available from the Article model.
Sorting controls that allow users to change the sort order (for example, by most commented or most recent), mapped to the sortBy URL parameter and used in getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.
Clickable article titles (and/or cards) that navigate to article.html with the corresponding article id in the URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Load compatibility page from navigation without preselected signs
1. User clicks the 'Compatibility' link in the site header or a compatibility tile on the homepage.
2. Browser navigates to compatibility.html without signOne or signTwo URL parameters, or with only a subset of parameters.
3. Page initializes: compatibility-sign-selector reads current URL parameters (if any) and populates the two sign dropdowns accordingly; missing parameters result in no default selection for those dropdowns.
4. Since one or both signs are not selected, compatibility-results-list does not call getCompatibilityArticlesForPair yet and instead shows a prompt (via an empty state) to select two signs.
5. User selects a value in the first sign dropdown, then selects a value in the second sign dropdown.
6. User clicks the Show Results button.
7. The form submission updates the URL parameters signOne and signTwo (and optionally sortBy if a value is set or defaulted), causing a navigation or history update to compatibility.html with the new query string.
8. After navigation/URL update, compatibility-results-list detects the new URL parameters and calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair(signOne, signTwo, sortBy).
9. When the API returns, the results list displays the compatibility articles with their metadata, and each article title becomes a link to article.html?id=Article.id.

Workflow 2: Deep link with preselected signs and sorting
1. User receives or saves a direct link such as compatibility.html?signOne={value1}&signTwo={value2}&sortBy={valueSort} (exact values determined at runtime, not hardcoded here).
2. User opens this URL directly.
3. On page load, compatibility-sign-selector reads signOne and signTwo from the URL and preselects the corresponding values in the dropdowns; compatibility-sort-controls reads sortBy and highlights the matching sort option.
4. compatibility-results-list immediately calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair using the signOne, signTwo, and sortBy values from the URL.
5. While loading, a loading state is shown until the API response is received.
6. When the API returns a list of Article items, the results list and individual result cards are rendered with article metadata and navigation links.
7. User clicks an article title or card, triggering navigation to article.html?id={article.id}.
8. The user can use the browser Back button to return to the compatibility page, which will still reflect the same URL parameters and thus reload the same results state as needed.

Workflow 3: Change sorting for an existing sign pair
1. User is viewing compatibility results for a sign pair (signOne and signTwo are already defined in the URL, and initial results are loaded).
2. User interacts with compatibility-sort-controls to choose a different sort option (for example, selecting a 'Most Commented' or 'Most Recent' option).
3. The sort control updates the sortBy URL parameter while preserving the current signOne and signTwo values, either via a full navigation or a history/route update.
4. compatibility-results-list detects the updated sortBy value in the URL and calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with the same signOne and signTwo but new sortBy.
5. The results list displays the re-ordered article list according to the sortBy parameter from the new API response.
6. User may repeat this process, switching between sort modes; each change updates the URL and re-fetches via getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.

Workflow 4: Change sign selection while viewing results
1. User is viewing results for a particular sign pair with signOne, signTwo, and sortBy currently present in the URL.
2. User changes the first sign dropdown or the second sign dropdown in compatibility-sign-selector.
3. The form does not immediately call the API; instead, the new values are staged in the form component state.
4. User clicks the Show Results button.
5. The page updates the URL parameters signOne and signTwo to match the new selections, retains the current sortBy (or applies a default if none exists), and navigates or updates the browser history.
6. After the URL change, compatibility-results-list calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair(signOne, signTwo, sortBy) with the updated sign pair.
7. Results list refreshes with compatibility articles specifically for the new pair.
8. User may then adjust sorting again if desired.

Workflow 5: No results or error handling
1. User submits a sign pair and the page calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with signOne, signTwo, and sortBy from the URL.
2. If the API returns an empty array, compatibility-results-list shows the compatibility-empty-state component, which informs the user that no articles are available for this combination and may suggest adjusting the sign selection or sort (text is defined by the application, not hardcoded here).
3. If the API call fails (for example, network or server error), compatibility-results-list displays compatibility-error-state with a retry action.
4. User clicks the retry button, which triggers another call to getCompatibilityArticlesForPair using the same URL parameters.
5. On success, the error state is replaced by the refreshed results list.
interactions: User selects or changes the first zodiac sign using a dropdown control; this updates the internal state of compatibility-sign-selector but does not immediately fetch results until the form is submitted.
User selects or changes the second zodiac sign using a dropdown control; this also updates internal state only until the user submits the form.

User clicks the Show Results button in compatibility-sign-selector; this validates that both signs are selected, updates the signOne and signTwo URL parameters (and sortBy where applicable), and triggers a reload of compatibility-results-list via getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.

User interacts with sorting controls (for example, clicking a sort option such as 'Most Commented' or 'Most Recent'); this interaction updates the sortBy URL parameter while preserving signOne and signTwo, then re-fetches results using getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.
User clicks an article title or the main clickable region of a compatibility-result-card; this interaction navigates to article.html with the query parameter id set to the corresponding Article.id.

User may use the browser Back and Forward buttons; since page state is fully encoded in URL parameters (signOne, signTwo, sortBy), the page can reconstruct the same results by re-invoking getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with those parameters on each load.
User can click a retry button within the compatibility-error-state component; this interaction re-uses the current URL parameters to retry the getCompatibilityArticlesForPair call.
page_functionality.state_logic: The compatibility page state is driven entirely by the URL query parameters signOne, signTwo, and sortBy. On every page load or URL change: 1) The page reads the current values of signOne, signTwo, and sortBy from the URL. 2) compatibility-sign-selector initializes its dropdown controls from signOne and signTwo; if a parameter is absent or invalid, that dropdown starts with no selection. 3) compatibility-sort-controls initializes its selected sort option based on sortBy; if sortBy is absent or invalid, the component uses an application-defined default sort mode and reflects that in its UI, but this default is not hardcoded in the page specification. 4) If both signOne and signTwo are present and valid, compatibility-results-list calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair(signOne, signTwo, sortBy) and manages loading, error, and empty states. 5) If either signOne or signTwo is missing, the page does not call getCompatibilityArticlesForPair; instead, it shows a prompt to select both signs before displaying results. 6) When the user submits the sign selector form, the page updates the URL parameters signOne and signTwo to match the selected values and preserves or initializes sortBy; this change in URL is the sole trigger for re-fetching results. 7) When the user changes the sort option, the page updates only the sortBy parameter in the URL while leaving signOne and signTwo unchanged; this also triggers a fresh getCompatibilityArticlesForPair call. 8) No state related to selected signs, sorting, or article data is stored in localStorage or other client-side persistence; all shareable and restorable state is encoded in the URL parameters, and article data is always retrieved via getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.

components.functionality: Manages selection of two zodiac signs and submission to load compatibility results. Reads existing signOne and signTwo from URL parameters on load to prefill the dropdowns, and when submitted, writes new signOne and signTwo values to the URL (and optionally initializes sortBy) to trigger a refresh of results.
event_handlers: onInitReadParams: On page load, read signOne and signTwo from the URL query string and set the initial selected values of the first and second sign dropdowns if present.
onChangeSignOne: When the first sign dropdown value changes, update the internal form state for signOne; do not call getCompatibilityArticlesForPair directly here.
onChangeSignTwo: When the second sign dropdown value changes, update the internal form state for signTwo; do not call getCompatibilityArticlesForPair directly here.

onSubmitShowResults: On form submission (Show Results button), validate that both signs are selected, then update the browser URL query parameters signOne and signTwo to match the current dropdown selections and preserve or initialize sortBy. This may be implemented via a full page navigation or a history API update but must result in the URL reflecting the new state so that other components can react.

components.functionality: Provides user-selectable sort options for the compatibility article list (e.g., most commented or most recent). Reflects the current sortBy value from the URL and updates sortBy when the user chooses a new sort mode.
event_handlers: onInitReadSortBy: On load, read the sortBy URL parameter; set the initially active sort option to match this value, or fall back to an application-defined default if sortBy is not present or invalid.

onChangeSortOption: When the user selects a different sort option, update the sortBy URL parameter in the query string while preserving signOne and signTwo values. This triggers a new fetch in compatibility-results-list, but this component itself does not call getCompatibilityArticlesForPair.
