onSyncFromUrlChange: If the URL sortBy parameter changes due to navigation (e.g., back/forward buttons), update the active sort option UI to stay in sync with the URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates fetching and displaying the list of compatibility Article items for the current sign pair and sort order. Calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair whenever relevant URL parameters change, and manages loading, error, and empty states.
event_handlers: onInitOrUrlChange: On initial page load and whenever the URL parameters signOne, signTwo, or sortBy change, determine whether both signOne and signTwo are present and valid. If they are, invoke getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with these values; if not, clear any existing results and show the prompt to select two signs.

onFetchStart: Before calling getCompatibilityArticlesForPair, set an internal loading state so that a loading indicator is shown and previous results are either hidden or marked as stale according to application rules.

onFetchSuccess: When getCompatibilityArticlesForPair returns successfully with an array of Article items, clear loading and error states, store the returned list, and render a sequence of compatibility-result-card components, each bound to one Article.

onFetchEmpty: If getCompatibilityArticlesForPair returns an empty array, clear loading and error states, set the internal results list to empty, and display the compatibility-empty-state component instead of cards.
onFetchError: If getCompatibilityArticlesForPair fails, clear loading state, store error details, and display the compatibility-error-state component in place of the results.
onRetryFromError: When compatibility-error-state triggers a retry action, re-invoke getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with the current signOne, signTwo, and sortBy values from the URL.

components.functionality: Displays a single compatibility Article item in the results list, including its title and available metadata such as summary, estimated reading time, publish date, rating, and comment count. Provides a click target to navigate to the article detail page.
event_handlers: onBindArticleData: When the parent results list passes in an Article object, bind its fields (such as id, title, summary, reading_time_minutes, publish_date, rating, rating_count, comment_count, hero_image_url, and tags) to the card UI. Fields that are null or undefined are simply omitted from display.

onClickOpenArticle: When the user clicks the article title or main card click area, navigate to article.html with the URL parameter id set to the Article.id value. This uses standard link navigation so that the target page can read id from the URL.
onHoverCardOptional: Optionally expose hover interactions (if implemented by the application) such as highlighting the card, without affecting data or navigation state.

components.functionality: Shows a user-facing message when getCompatibilityArticlesForPair returns no Article items for the current sign pair and sortBy. Encourages users to adjust their selections but does not itself change state.
event_handlers: onShowWhenNoResults: Render this component only when compatibility-results-list determines that the fetched article array is empty and there is no loading or error state active.

components.functionality: Displays an error message and a retry action when getCompatibilityArticlesForPair fails for the current URL parameters.
event_handlers: onShowOnError: Render this component when compatibility-results-list encounters an error during the getCompatibilityArticlesForPair call.

onClickRetry: When the user clicks the retry button, emit an event back to compatibility-results-list, which then re-calls getCompatibilityArticlesForPair with the current signOne, signTwo, and sortBy parameters from the URL.

components.functionality: Provides a simple, functional page heading such as 'Compatibility' and can optionally reflect the currently selected sign pair for context (for example, by showing the selected signs if both are present in the URL). Does not perform any data fetching itself.
event_handlers: onInitReadParams: On load or URL change, read signOne and signTwo from the URL and, if both are present, update the header subtitle or contextual text to indicate which pair the results are for.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single centered column with stacked, full-width cards that echo the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the content column sits the watercolor-inspired compatibility page header, followed by a prominent sign selector card, then a slimmer sort-controls pill bar, and finally a tall results region where result cards (or empty/error states) stack vertically. All components align to the same max-width and sit inside a full-width pastel background.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single centered column with stacked, full-width cards that echo the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow. At the top of the content column sits the watercolor-inspired compatibility page header, followed by a prominent sign selector card, then a slimmer sort-controls pill bar, and finally a tall results region where result cards (or empty/error states) stack vertically. All components align to the same max-width and sit inside a full-width pastel background.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page header occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height, creating a strong visual entry. The sign selector card below it takes about 15–20% of the initial viewport, with generous vertical margin separating it from the header. The sort-controls pill is compact (around 8–10% height), sitting directly above the results region. The results list area occupies the remaining visible space and extends down the page as the user scrolls. Each stacked section has 16–24px vertical margins between them, and each card/pill uses 16–24px inner padding, consistent with the design DNA.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: macro elements (page header, sign selector card, sort pill, results list) are clearly separated with visible lavender background between them, but inside each card the content is standard density. Text blocks have normal line spacing, with headings given extra breathing room above and below. Controls (sign dropdowns, sort toggles) have comfortable hit areas and spacing so they never feel cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mimics the mockup: the eye lands first on the illustrated compatibility page header, then is pulled down to the bold sign selector card as the main interactive focus. From there, the eye moves down to the slim horizontal sort-controls pill, acting like a secondary navigation bar for results. Finally, the user’s attention flows into the tall vertical results region, scanning down through stacked result cards (or a central empty/error state) in a straightforward reading order.

layout.overall_description: The Compatibility page sits on a soft lavender full-width background with a single centered content column (around 960–1040px max width). At the very top of this column is a playful, watercolor-flavored page header that sets the astrology mood and clearly labels the page. Below it, with a noticeable gap of lavender space, a rounded sign selector card stretches nearly the full width of the column, feeling like a friendly, interactive panel where users pick two zodiac signs. A smaller gap leads to a slimmer, pill-shaped sort-controls bar that spans the same width and visually resembles a navigation strip just above the results. The main body of the page is a tall results area where compatibility result cards stack vertically, each card a rounded, softly bordered container with a prominent title and preview text, echoing the article card from the mockup. When no results or errors occur, a centered, illustrated empty or error state occupies this same results region. The entire page feels like a stack of rounded, pastel cards and pills floating on a lavender canvas, guiding the user’s gaze smoothly from top illustration to interactive filters and down into rich compatibility content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The compatibility page header is the first component in the centered column, positioned just below any global site chrome. It spans the full width of the content column (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on large screens, constrained by a max width around 960–1040px), leaving wide lavender gutters on the left and right. Vertically, it occupies about the top 20–25% of the initial viewport, with a tall, rounded rectangle or lightly framed area that features the watercolor illustration across the upper half and the page title and subtitle in the lower half. The title "Compatibility" appears in a large, colorful, slightly retro type centered or slightly right of center, with a short descriptive line below it. There is generous padding (24px top/bottom, 24–32px sides) so text and illustrations sit comfortably away from edges. Beneath the header, a vertical gap of roughly 24px of blank lavender background separates it from the next component, clearly defining it as its own visual band at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the normal document flow; it scrolls away as the user moves down the page, acting as an immersive top-of-page hero section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sign selector sits directly under the page header, centered in the same content column, separated by a 24px lavender gap. It appears as a large, rounded card spanning nearly the full width of the column (again roughly 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop, aligned with the header edges), with prominent border radius and a soft pastel fill (e.g., mint or a lighter lavender) to make it feel interactive and important. Inside the card, there is 24px padding on all sides. At the top of the card, a concise heading like "Choose your signs" is left-aligned or center-aligned, with a bit of extra space above and below (e.g., 8–16px) to separate it from controls. Below the heading, two equal-width sign selection controls (dropdowns or pill selectors) are arranged side by side on larger screens, each occupying about 45% of the card width with an 8–16px gap between them. On smaller screens, these controls stack vertically with 8–16px spacing. An optional "Swap" control or small helper text can sit centered below the sign fields with a 16px gap. The bottom of the card has a subtle extra padding so the controls don’t feel cramped. Below the sign selector card, another 16–24px vertical gap opens onto the sort-controls pill, maintaining the stacked-card rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and central in the flow; it visually anchors the functional part of the page near the top and maintains its full width as the page resizes, while internal controls reflow responsively.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-controls component is positioned directly beneath the sign selector card with approximately 16–20px of vertical lavender space between them, acting as a thin, secondary bar above the results. It appears as a horizontally elongated pill or shallow card, spanning the same width as the sign selector (full content column width with rounded corners and a subtle pastel background or border). The height is modest, visually taking about 8–10% of the initial viewport if seen in isolation, but functionally it’s a compressed strip. Inside, padding of around 12–16px on all sides ensures controls don’t touch the edges. The main sort dropdown or segmented control sits left-aligned inside the pill, with label text (e.g., "Sort by:") followed by the control. To the right, aligned with the same baseline, secondary controls like a view toggle or filter chip can sit, leaving some breathing room near the right edge (at least 16px). On narrow screens, these controls stack or wrap, but still stay within the pill boundaries. Immediately below this bar, a 16–24px gap leads into the results list area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static bar in the vertical flow, visually similar to a navigation pill; it scrolls with the page and does not stick, but consistently appears just above the results region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main lower portion of the page, sitting directly under the sort-controls pill with a 16–24px vertical gap of lavender background in between. It shares the same max-width and centered alignment as the components above, effectively forming an invisible container that stretches down the page. Internally, this area simply stacks multiple compatibility result cards vertically in a single column, each card spanning nearly the full width of the content column. Each result card is separated from the next by a 16–24px vertical gap, allowing the lavender background to show between them and keeping the rhythm consistent with the mockup’s article card spacing. The results list itself doesn’t have a visible card boundary; instead, it serves as the logical region where result cards, or alternatively the empty or error state, appear. When populated, this region can extend well beyond the initial viewport, encouraging vertical scrolling through content. When there are no results or an error, this same region is occupied by the respective state indicator instead of cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A flexible, vertically expanding region within the normal document flow; it scrolls naturally and adapts its height to the number of result cards or to the size of the empty/error state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each compatibility result card appears as a rounded rectangle card within the results list, aligned to the center of the page and spanning the full content column width (minus small side padding/gutters on very small screens). The card’s visual style closely mirrors the mockup’s article card: a soft white or very light pastel background, gentle drop shadow or border, and corner radii that match the sign selector and sort pill. Padding inside the card is generous—approximately 20–24px on all sides. At the top, a bold, colorful title (e.g., "Aries & Libra: Electric Harmony") is left-aligned and allowed to stretch across most of the card width, with a bit of extra top/bottom spacing (8–12px). Directly below the title, a metadata row runs horizontally: small, muted text for sign icons, category, or reading length, aligned left with subtle spacing between items. Beneath the metadata row sits one or two short preview paragraphs of body text, each separated by standard paragraph spacing (8–12px), providing a teaser of the article. At the bottom right or bottom left (depending on the chosen convention), a prominent "Read full compatibility" link or button sits with at least 12–16px clearance from the edges. The overall height of the card is moderate to tall, similar to a blog article preview, and when multiple cards are stacked, the eye reads them downward in order. A 16–24px vertical gap of lavender background separates this card from the next in the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level card within the results list; it does not float or stick and simply participates in the vertical stack, repeating for each compatibility article.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state replaces the list of result cards and occupies the same horizontal space as the results list area. It appears directly below the sort-controls pill with the usual 16–24px vertical gap. Instead of multiple cards, a single, more spacious card-like container is centered within the content column. This empty-state card has a soft pastel background (perhaps a paler mint or lavender), rounded corners, and generous padding (24–32px all around). Within it, an illustration or simple icon that fits the fantasy/astrology mood sits centered near the top, taking up roughly the upper third of the card’s height. Below the illustration, a friendly headline like "No matches yet" is center-aligned, followed by a short explanatory paragraph and an inline suggestion (e.g., "Try choosing different signs"), all stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. The card’s height is enough that it feels significant—roughly equivalent to 1–1.5 typical result cards stacked—but still leaves visible lavender background above and below so it feels like one self-contained panel within the list area. If there is additional helper action (e.g., a small button to reset filters), it appears centered below the text with at least 16px clearance from card edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional content within the results list region; it appears only when there are no results and is otherwise hidden. It scrolls normally and does not affect the layout of components above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also occupies the results list region, appearing in place of result cards or the empty state when an error occurs. It is positioned directly beneath the sort-controls pill with the same 16–24px vertical gap, matching the alignment and max-width of other components. The error state is presented as a rounded card similar in shape to the empty-state card, but with a more attention-grabbing accent: perhaps a soft red or warm peach pastel background band at the top or a colored border that echoes the bright accent colors used in the design DNA. Padding is generous (24–32px) to keep content from feeling tense. Inside, an expressive icon or small illustration representing a glitch or cloudy stars sits at the top left or centered, followed by a bold error headline (e.g., "Something went off in the stars"), and a concise explanatory line in smaller text. A retry button or link is placed below, left-aligned or center-aligned, with enough spacing (16px) to stand on its own. The card height is similar to or slightly shorter than the empty-state card, clearly distinct but not overwhelming. Lavender background remains visible above and below to maintain the airy, card-based rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional content within the results list region; it replaces result cards when an error is present. It scrolls normally and does not introduce fixed or sticky behavior.

name: Moon Phases / Lunar Calendar
title: Moon Phases Calendar
description: Displays a monthly lunar calendar with moon phases and zodiac signs, allowing users to switch views, navigate between months, and open detailed moon event pages.

core_features: Load all moon events for the selected month and year using getMoonCalendarMonth, based on URL parameters.
Render a month-view lunar calendar grid where dates with moon events show the event name, phase, and zodiac sign.
Provide an alternate list view of all moon events for the selected month, toggled via the view URL parameter.
Allow users to change month and year via a selector or previous/next controls, updating URL parameters and reloading data.
Enable clicking on a moon event (from either calendar or list view) to navigate to the Moon Event Detail page with the event ID in the URL.
Offer a 'View in Planner' control that navigates to planner.html configured to show the same month and year in month view.
Optionally show a summarized legend of key or major moon events for the selected month, derived from the same monthly data.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Landing on the lunar calendar
1. User opens moon_phases.html (via header, footer, or a direct URL).
2. The page reads URL parameters month, year, and view.
3. If month or year is missing, the page determines the current calendar month and year and uses those as defaults.
4. The page calls getMoonCalendarMonth with the resolved month and year.
5. After data is returned, the page renders either the calendar view or list view depending on the view parameter (defaulting to calendar when not provided).
6. The legend of key events and the planner link are populated for the selected month based on the loaded data and URL state.

Workflow 2: Changing month/year
1. User interacts with the month/year selector or previous/next month controls.
2. The selector computes the new month and year values (handling year rollover when moving across year boundaries).
3. The page updates the browser URL to moon_phases.html with the new month and year (and preserving the current view value) as query parameters.
4. The page reloads (or re-initializes state) and calls getMoonCalendarMonth with the new month and year.
5. The calendar or list view updates to show moon events for the newly selected month.

Workflow 3: Switching between calendar and list views
1. User clicks a control to switch the view mode.
2. The control determines the alternate view value (calendar or list).
3. The page updates the URL parameter view accordingly while preserving month and year.
4. The page re-renders using the already loaded data (if compatible with the implementation) or re-calls getMoonCalendarMonth with the current month and year.
5. The user now sees the same month’s moon events in the new view format (grid or list).

Workflow 4: Opening a moon event detail page
1. User clicks on a date cell or list item representing a specific moon event.
2. The clicked element retrieves the associated MoonEvent.id from the bound data.
3. The browser navigates to moon_event.html with the id URL parameter set to that MoonEvent.id.
4. The Moon Event Detail page uses the id to show detailed information (handled on moon_event.html, not on this page).

Workflow 5: Viewing the month in the ritual planner
1. User reviews the moon events for a given month on moon_phases.html.
2. User clicks the 'View in Planner' control.
3. The control reads the current month and year from the page state (derived from URL parameters).
4. The browser navigates to planner.html with query parameters view=month, month=currentMonth, and year=currentYear.
5. The planner page opens configured to show scheduled rituals for that same month.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameters month, year, and view, compute defaults when needed, and trigger getMoonCalendarMonth(month, year).
On month/year selector change, update the URL with the selected month and year (preserving view) and reload the calendar data via getMoonCalendarMonth.
On previous/next month control click, calculate the adjacent month/year, update URL parameters, and refresh data using getMoonCalendarMonth.
On view toggle control click, update the view parameter in the URL between calendar and list while preserving month and year, and re-render the page in the selected view.
On moon event click in the calendar grid, navigate to moon_event.html?id={MoonEvent.id}.
On moon event click in the list view, navigate to moon_event.html?id={MoonEvent.id}.
On 'View in Planner' control click, navigate to planner.html?view=month&month={currentMonth}&year={currentYear}.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven exclusively by URL parameters and the data returned from getMoonCalendarMonth. On initialization, the page reads month, year, and view from the query string. If month or year are not present or invalid, the implementation determines the current calendar month and year at runtime and uses those as implicit defaults, but it may optionally normalize the URL to include these defaults. The resolved month and year are then passed to getMoonCalendarMonth to retrieve all MoonEvent records for that month. The view parameter controls which main layout is rendered: when view='list', the list component is shown; otherwise, the calendar grid is shown. Any change in month, year, or view is performed by updating the URL parameters (via full navigation or history manipulation) rather than using local storage or hidden in-memory-only state. Visual components (calendar grid, list, legend, planner link) bind directly to the MoonEvent data returned by getMoonCalendarMonth and to the current URL-derived month/year/view, ensuring the page can be deep-linked and refreshed without losing state.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading URL parameters (month, year, view), applying defaults for missing values, and triggering the initial data load for the lunar calendar.

Calls getMoonCalendarMonth with the resolved month and year, stores the returned list of MoonEvent records, and exposes them to child components (calendar grid, list view, legend, and planner link). Handles reloading when month or year changes.

Allows the user to select a specific month and year. When the selection changes, it updates the URL query parameters month and year (preserving view) and thereby causes the lunar calendar data to be reloaded for the new period.

Provides controls for navigating to the previous or next month without manually changing the selectors. When activated, it computes the adjacent month and year, updates the URL (including month, year, and current view), and triggers a data reload via the loader.
Switches between calendar and list representations of the same monthly moon event data by updating the view parameter in the URL and re-rendering the main content accordingly.

Displays a calendar grid for the selected month, placing each MoonEvent on its corresponding date cell with key information such as name, phase, and zodiac sign. Handles days with no events and days with multiple events. Clicking an event navigates to the moon event detail page.
Shows all MoonEvent records for the selected month in a list format, including event date, name, phase, and zodiac sign. Each list item is clickable and opens the corresponding moon_event.html page.

Derives a subset or summary of notable moon events for the selected month (for example, using MoonEvent attributes such as flags for importance). Presents them as a concise legend or highlight list to support quick planning.

Provides a control that, when clicked, navigates to planner.html with view=month and the currently selected month and year in the URL parameters, so users can see or manage rituals aligned with the same lunar month.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered main column sitting on a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog-style mockup. Above this page’s components lives the existing watercolor site header and primary navigation (not part of this spec). Directly under that global header, stack full-width rounded 'pill' control bars (month/year + view toggle, then month navigation), followed by one large rounded 'article-style' card that holds the calendar grid, legend, event list, and CTA in a clear top-to-bottom reading order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered main column sitting on a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog-style mockup. Above this page’s components lives the existing watercolor site header and primary navigation (not part of this spec). Directly under that global header, stack full-width rounded 'pill' control bars (month/year + view toggle, then month navigation), followed by one large rounded 'article-style' card that holds the calendar grid, legend, event list, and CTA in a clear top-to-bottom reading order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve the top ~25–30% of the viewport for the existing watercolor header area. Immediately below, the first control pill (month-year selector + view-mode toggle) and second pill (month navigation) each occupy roughly 6–10% of the viewport height with generous vertical spacing between them. The main calendar card consumes the remaining vertical space in the initial viewport. Within the card, the calendar grid gets roughly the top half, the legend and event list together take most of the remaining height, and the CTA sits with comfortable breathing room at the bottom. The central column is constrained to a fixed max width (e.g., 960–1040px) and centered, with 24–32px of lavender background margin on left and right at common desktop widths.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: macro elements (pills and the calendar card) are clearly separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. Inside each pill or card, use 16–24px padding on all sides. Controls and text are comfortably spaced, with no elements touching container edges. The calendar grid cells and event list lines are reasonably compact to keep information scannable, but still have at least an 8px inner padding rhythm, maintaining the handcrafted, friendly feel rather than a tight dashboard look.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the colorful watercolor header down into the first strong horizontal pill with the month/year controls, then to the slimmer second pill with month navigation, and then into the large, white/cream calendar card. Within the card, the eye follows a vertical flow: calendar grid at the top, then a compact key-events legend explaining symbols, then the chronological moon events list, and finally the prominent planner CTA at the bottom. Accent colors (mint, lavender, green, purple) and rounded cards reinforce this flow without introducing competing focal points.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a soft lavender background with a fixed-width, centered content column. At the very top of the page, the existing watercolor illustration header and site logo span full width, setting a dreamy astrology tone. Just below, the main Moon Phases / Lunar Calendar section begins with a wide, pill-shaped mint control bar that feels like a friendly navigation chip rather than a rigid toolbar. This pill holds the month and year controls on the left and the view mode toggle on the right, all horizontally aligned on desktop and stacked more loosely on small screens. A modest gap below it reveals a second, slightly slimmer pill that behaves like a breadcrumb: left and right arrow controls flank a centered month label, inviting the user to step through months. Beneath these two bars, a large rounded card in a light cream or very pale lavender rests in the center, echoing a blog article card. Inside this card, the top half is dominated by a 7-column lunar calendar grid, with clear cells and subtle phase and zodiac icons. Below the grid, a concise legend in a smaller rounded sub-panel explains the icons, followed by a vertically scrolling list of key moon events for the month, all full-width within the card. At the very bottom of the card, a bold, colorful CTA strip with generous padding invites the user to open a more detailed moon planner, acting as the final anchor of the page’s vertical flow. Throughout, rounded corners, pastel fills, and ample padding keep the layout playful and approachable while preserving a simple, linear reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and sits logically at the very top of the page structure, just after the global site header in the DOM. It does not occupy any visible space or alter the visual layout. Conceptually, it wraps the entire centered content column, initializing state before any of the visible pills, calendar card, or grid render.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered in the UI; behaves as a logical wrapper around the main content column with no visual footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader overlays the main lunar calendar card area when data is being fetched. Visually, it appears as a semi-transparent lavender or mint-tinted wash that covers the entire width and height of the calendar card (including the grid, legend, events list, and CTA), with a small, centered circular spinner or simple moon-phase animation in the middle. The overlay respects the rounded corners of the underlying card so it feels integrated rather than a separate dialog. Outside of loading states, it is hidden and does not consume layout space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned inside the main calendar card container, stretching to its padding box; toggled on/off based on loading state, blocking interaction with underlying elements when visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month-year selector lives inside the first, most prominent pill-shaped control bar directly beneath the global header/navigation. This bar is centered in the page, spans roughly 80–90% of the main content width, and has 16–24px of internal padding with rounded corners (24–32px radius). Within that pill, the month-year selector is left-aligned: the month dropdown or segmented control appears first, followed by a year dropdown or small numeric selector, separated by about 8–16px. On desktop, these controls sit in a single horizontal row. On narrow screens, they may stack vertically while still hugging the left side of the pill, with at least 8px vertical spacing between them. There is at least 16px of space between the rightmost edge of the month-year selector and the view-mode toggle that shares this bar, giving the pill a calm, uncluttered feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow as part of the top control pill. It shrinks and wraps responsively, but remains within the bounds of the shared pill container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month-navigation controls sit in their own dedicated pill-shaped bar directly beneath the first control pill, separated from it by a vertical gap of about 16–24px. This pill spans the same centered width (80–90% of the content width) and uses a slightly lighter or more neutral pastel background than the top pill to read visually as a secondary bar, similar to a breadcrumb. Inside this pill, a left arrow (previous month) is aligned to the far left, a right arrow (next month) to the far right, and the current month label (e.g., "January 2026") is centered both horizontally and vertically in between, giving a balanced, three-part layout. Padding of 12–16px top/bottom and 16–24px left/right ensures that icons and text have breathing room. On smaller screens, the arrows and label still sit in one horizontal row but with slightly increased spacing and possibly smaller icon size to maintain clarity within the pill.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical stack, following the first control pill. It scrolls away with the rest of the content and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-mode toggle shares the top mint pill with the month-year selector, positioned on the right side of that same bar. The pill itself is centered and full-width (within the fixed max content width), and the toggle is right-aligned inside it, visually balancing the month-year controls on the left. The toggle may be a small segmented control (e.g., "Calendar" vs. "List" or "Month" vs. "Week"), styled as rounded buttons that echo the pill’s overall curvature. It sits in a single row horizontally on desktop, with at least 16px of right padding between the control and the pill’s edge. On narrow screens, the view-mode toggle wraps onto its own line inside the same pill, appearing below the month-year selector and right-aligned, with around 8–12px of vertical gap separating them. This keeps the pill from feeling cramped while preserving the single-pill composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the shared control pill, aligning to the right via flex or similar layout, and reflows responsively as the pill narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moon-calendar grid is the primary content within a large, article-like card placed directly below the month navigation pill. This card is centered in the column, matches the width of the pills, and is separated from the month-navigation pill by about 24px of vertical space to create a clear band of lavender background. The card has a soft white or very pale pastel background with 24px of padding on all sides and generous rounded corners (16–24px). At the top of the card, optionally, a simple heading or small label (e.g., "Lunar Calendar") can sit, but the grid itself occupies most of the upper half of the card. The grid spans the full inner width of the card, laid out as 7 equal-width columns for the days of the week, with 4–6 rows depending on the month. Each cell has approximately 8–12px of inner padding, with subtle pastel lines or softly tinted blocks separating the days. Moon phase icons and zodiac symbols sit inside each cell, aligned toward the top-left of the cell, with small, legible labels. In the default viewport, the grid takes roughly 50–60% of the card's vertical height, leaving clear space below for the legend and event list. On mobile, the card width shrinks but remains a single column; the grid cells become taller while maintaining their 7-column structure to preserve the calendar metaphor.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main card, expanding vertically as needed for more weeks while preserving its place at the top of the card’s content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moon-events list appears inside the same main calendar card, positioned below the monthly key-events legend to maintain a clear vertical narrative. It spans the full inner width of the card and is separated from the legend above it by around 16px of vertical space. Each event row is presented as a single, horizontally oriented line or small block with the date, time, moon phase, zodiac sign, and any short description. Rows are evenly spaced with approximately 8–12px vertical padding and maybe a faint dividing line or alternating subtle background tints to keep the list readable without feeling heavy. The list occupies most of the lower half of the card, and if there are many events, the card grows vertically and the list simply continues downward, preserving the single-column reading flow. On smaller screens, everything remains stacked, with event text wrapping to multiple lines but still respecting the left alignment and vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and vertically stacked within the card; naturally extends the card’s height as more events are present. No internal scrolling—scroll is handled by the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The monthly key-events legend sits inside the main calendar card, directly beneath the calendar grid and above the moon-events list, acting like a compact, explanatory band. It is separated from the grid by about 16px of vertical space. Visually, it appears as a smaller rounded sub-card or strip with a slightly tinted pastel background (e.g., very soft mint or lavender) that contrasts gently with the main card’s background. This sub-card spans roughly 100% of the card’s inner width but uses 16px of internal padding. Inside, small icon-and-label pairs (for phases and zodiac signs) are arranged in a horizontal row that wraps to multiple lines if needed. On desktop, these pairs may be laid out in 2–3 columns to avoid excessive width per item, while on mobile they stack in one or two columns with consistent 8–12px spacing between each item. This legend is visually subtle but clearly separated, so users can quickly reference it while scanning the grid or events list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sub-section within the main card, expanding vertically as needed when more legend items are present. It does not scroll independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner link CTA is anchored at the bottom of the main calendar card, forming the final block in the vertical stack. It is separated from the moon-events list above by about 24px of space so it feels like a distinct conclusion to the card’s content. Visually, it is a wide, full-width horizontal strip within the card, with a more saturated accent background (e.g., a slightly brighter purple or green) and rounded corners that align with the card’s inner radius. The strip has 16–24px of padding and contains a concise, friendly line of text encouraging deeper planning (e.g., "Plan your month with the full Moon Planner"), with a prominent button-style link aligned to the right on desktop. On small screens, the text appears first and the button stacks below it, centered, with around 8–12px vertical spacing. The CTA feels inviting and playful, not aggressive, and visually echoes the site’s retro-astrology personality.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main card. It does not stick to the viewport; it scrolls naturally with the card and page content.

name: Moon Event Detail
title: Moon Event Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected lunar event and lets users open related ritual guides or schedule a ritual in the planner.

core_features: Load a specific moon event using the id URL parameter via getMoonEventDetail and display its key details (name, date, phase, zodiac sign, description).
Show a structured overview of the moon event context, including indicators such as whether it is a major event.
List related ritual guides and other articles from the moon event’s related_article_ids, with navigation to their Article Detail pages (article.html?id=...).
Highlight which related articles are ritual guides (article_type = 'ritual_guide') so they can be used for planning rituals tied to this moon event.
Provide a quick-add ritual planner form that lets the user select a ritual guide, choose date/time and optional note, and create a RitualPlannerEntry via createRitualPlannerEntry.
After a planner entry is successfully created, offer an action to navigate to the planner month view for the event’s calendar_month and calendar_year (planner.html?view=month&month=...&year=...).
Handle loading, missing-id, and error states when retrieving moon event data, and handle scheduler form validation and API errors when creating planner entries.
user_workflows: View moon event details from lunar calendar: 1) User clicks a moon event on moon_phases.html, which navigates to moon_event.html?id=<MoonEvent.id>. 2) Page reads id from URL and calls getMoonEventDetail(moonEventId=id). 3) On success, the page displays the event name, date, phase, sign, and description. 4) User reviews the core information and thematic description to understand the energetic context of the event.

Open a related ritual guide or article: 1) After the event loads, the page lists related articles linked to the moon event. 2) User scans the list and clicks a ritual guide or other related article. 3) The page navigates to article.html?id=<selected Article.id> using the clicked item’s id. 4) User reads the ritual guide or article in the Article Detail page.

Quickly plan a ritual for this moon event: 1) User scrolls to the planner quick-add section. 2) The event’s date is prefilled (or used as default) in the planner form; the moon event is implicitly associated via moonEventId. 3) User selects one of the ritual-guide-type related articles from a dropdown or list in the form. 4) User optionally adjusts scheduled start/end date-time and enters an optional note. 5) User submits the form, which calls createRitualPlannerEntry with articleId (selected ritual guide), moonEventId (current event), scheduledStart, scheduledEnd, note, and status (e.g., planned). 6) On success, the page shows a confirmation state summarizing the created entry. 7) User clicks the “View in Planner” action, and the page navigates to planner.html?view=month&month=<MoonEvent.calendar_month>&year=<MoonEvent.calendar_year> so the user sees the newly scheduled ritual in the monthly planner.

Access moon event from planner and refine ritual: 1) From planner.html, user clicks an indicator or link for a moon event associated with a planner entry, which navigates to moon_event.html?id=<MoonEvent.id>. 2) User reads deeper event details and related articles. 3) If the user wants to schedule an additional or modified ritual, they use the quick-add planner form again to create another RitualPlannerEntry tied to the same moon event but possibly a different ritual guide or time.

Handle missing or invalid moon event: 1) User lands on moon_event.html without an id parameter or with an id that does not match any existing MoonEvent. 2) The page either skips calling getMoonEventDetail (if id is missing) or receives an error from the interface. 3) An error component informs the user that the event could not be loaded and may present navigation back to moon_phases.html or index.html via simple links.
interactions: On page load: Read id from the URL query string; if present, call getMoonEventDetail(moonEventId=id).
When getMoonEventDetail is in progress: Show a loading indicator and disable interactions that depend on MoonEvent data.
When getMoonEventDetail succeeds: Populate all bound components (event header, description, related articles list, and planner quick-add defaults) with the returned MoonEvent data.
When getMoonEventDetail fails or returns no event: Show an error state with messaging and links back to other pages; hide planner-related actions that depend on valid MoonEvent data.
Click on related article item: Trigger navigation to article.html?id=<clicked Article.id> using standard link behavior.

Click on a specific ritual guide’s quick-plan action within the related articles list: Preselect that Article.id as the articleId in the planner quick-add form (without immediate API call), then scroll or focus the planner form to ease scheduling.

Change ritual guide selection in planner quick-add form: Update the selected articleId state used when calling createRitualPlannerEntry; validate that a ritual guide is selected before enabling submit.
Change date/time fields in planner quick-add form: Update scheduledStart and scheduledEnd fields in the local form state; ensure fields are valid before enabling submit.
Enter or edit optional note in planner quick-add form: Update note in form state; the content is sent as the note parameter when createRitualPlannerEntry is called.

Submit planner quick-add form: Validate required fields (articleId, scheduledStart at minimum) and, if valid, call createRitualPlannerEntry with articleId (selected ritual guide), moonEventId (current MoonEvent.id), scheduledStart, scheduledEnd (if provided), note (if provided), and status (e.g., planned). Disable submit and show in-progress state while the request is pending.
Handle successful createRitualPlannerEntry response: Store the returned RitualPlannerEntry in page state for confirmation display; show a success message and the 'View in Planner' link/button.
Handle failed createRitualPlannerEntry response: Display an error message within the planner form, keep user input intact, and re-enable the submit button so the user can adjust and retry.

Click 'View in Planner' after successful planner entry creation: Construct a URL using the MoonEvent.calendar_month and MoonEvent.calendar_year from the loaded event, and navigate to planner.html?view=month&month=<calendar_month>&year=<calendar_year>.
Click a generic back or navigation link: Use simple anchor tags (e.g., to moon_phases.html or planner.html) without additional API calls, relying on those pages to manage their own states.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the id query parameter in the page URL. On initial load and on any change to the id parameter (e.g., when the user navigates via a link that changes id but reuses the same page instance), the page calls getMoonEventDetail(moonEventId=id). If id is absent, the page does not call getMoonEventDetail and instead shows an error or guidance state. The MoonEvent returned from getMoonEventDetail populates all moon-event-related UI: event name, date, moon_phase, zodiac_sign, is_major_event, description, calendar_month, calendar_year, and related_article_ids (with article details provided by the interface). The page maintains transient in-memory state for UI concerns such as loading flags, selected ritual guide for planning, planner form field values, and the last-created RitualPlannerEntry; this transient state does not use URL parameters or localStorage. For cross-page navigation, the page relies on URL parameters exclusively: article navigation uses article.html?id=<Article.id>, and planner navigation uses planner.html?view=month&month=<MoonEvent.calendar_month>&year=<MoonEvent.calendar_year>. If the id parameter changes (e.g., via internal navigation to another moon event), existing planner form state and success messages are reset, and a new getMoonEventDetail call is made to reflect the newly selected event.

components.functionality: Reads the id query parameter from the URL, calls getMoonEventDetail with that id, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded MoonEvent data to child components.
Displays key headline information for the loaded moon event, such as name, formatted date/time, moon_phase, zodiac_sign, and an indicator if it is a major event.

Shows structured metadata for the moon event (e.g., phase, zodiac_sign, calendar_month and calendar_year, and a concise summary extracted from MoonEvent.description). Provides quick reference without scrolling.
Displays the full descriptive text of the moon event, giving thematic context and guidance drawn from MoonEvent.description.

Renders a list of articles related to the moon event, based on MoonEvent.related_article_ids and associated Article data returned by getMoonEventDetail. Distinguishes ritual guides from other article types and exposes actions to open each article or preselect it for planning.

Filters the MoonEvent’s related articles to identify which ones are usable as ritual guides (article_type = 'ritual_guide') and provides this filtered subset to components such as the planner quick-add form.

Allows the user to quickly create a RitualPlannerEntry associated with the current moon event. It provides controls to select a ritual guide article (from related ritual guides), choose scheduledStart and optional scheduledEnd, and enter an optional note. On submit, it calls createRitualPlannerEntry and manages validation and feedback.

Displays confirmation information for the most recently created RitualPlannerEntry, such as its title and scheduled date, and exposes a 'View in Planner' action that navigates to planner.html with month and year based on MoonEvent.calendar_month and MoonEvent.calendar_year.
Shows a loading state while getMoonEventDetail is in progress and hides main content until the MoonEvent is loaded or an error occurs.

Displays user-friendly error messages when the id URL parameter is missing or when getMoonEventDetail fails (e.g., event not found). Provides navigation links back to pages such as moon_phases.html or index.html.

Encapsulates validation logic for the planner quick-add form, ensuring that a ritual guide articleId and a valid scheduledStart are present before enabling submission, and optionally checking consistency of scheduledEnd relative to scheduledStart.
Provides simple navigational links back to moon_phases.html and planner.html without additional API calls, helping users move between the lunar calendar, planner, and this moon event detail.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width lavender background. Within that column, components are stacked vertically in a linear reading order: global illustrated site header (outside this spec), then the moon-event breadcrumb bar near the top, followed by the main moon event detail card (header + meta + description), then a separate related-articles card, and finally a ritual planner quick-add card at the bottom. Logic helpers and loaders wrap or overlay these visible components without adding new visual blocks. Error and loading states temporarily replace the main content stack with a single prominent status card.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width lavender background. Within that column, components are stacked vertically in a linear reading order: global illustrated site header (outside this spec), then the moon-event breadcrumb bar near the top, followed by the main moon event detail card (header + meta + description), then a separate related-articles card, and finally a ritual planner quick-add card at the bottom. Logic helpers and loaders wrap or overlay these visible components without adding new visual blocks. Error and loading states temporarily replace the main content stack with a single prominent status card.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is dominated by the illustrated site header (not part of this component list but assumed), with the moon-event breadcrumbs nestled just below it. The main event detail card occupies roughly the next 35–40% of vertical space on initial load, depending on description length. The related articles and planner cards occupy the remaining visible height, with each major card separated by 16–24px of vertical margin. Horizontally, all primary cards (breadcrumbs, event detail, related list, planner) span about 90–100% of the content column, which itself is constrained to a fixed max-width (around 960–1040px) and centered within the viewport, leaving generous lavender gutters on both sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Macro-level density is airy: each card is clearly separated from the others with 16–24px vertical gaps, and generous padding (16–24px) inside each card keeps content away from edges. Inside the main event description, paragraph spacing is standard (around 1 base unit between lines and 2 base units between paragraphs), similar to a classic blog article. Metadata and list items are well spaced but not sparse, following a moderate density that keeps the layout readable without feeling empty.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye flows from the colorful site header down to the breadcrumb pill, then into the large moon event detail card where the event title is the first strong focal point. From the title, the eye drops to the metadata panel just below, then naturally down into the body description. After finishing the description, the visual path continues downward to the related articles card (a secondary focal area that suggests further reading), and finally to the ritual planner quick-add card as a clear action area. Loading and error states interrupt this flow with a centered status panel, while success feedback appears close to the planner form to reinforce the connection between action and outcome.

layout.overall_description: The Moon Event Detail page appears as a cozy, single-column blog layout floating on a soft lavender background. At the top of the viewport, the site’s watercolor illustration and logo set a whimsical, astrology-infused mood. Just below, a rounded breadcrumb pill runs almost the full width of the centered content column, hinting at the page’s place in the broader astrology and moon events section. Beneath a comfortable gap, a large rounded card holds the moon event details: the event title in a rich purple headline, small playful astrology icons, and a pastel metadata strip showing timing, sign, and visibility. Below that, the main description reads like a journal entry, with clean paragraphs and breathing room around the text. A further scroll reveals a separate rounded card listing related ritual and moon articles, styled like a curated reading list. At the bottom of the column, a final card invites the user to schedule a ritual in their planner, with chunky inputs and a friendly call-to-action button. Throughout, the cards are cushioned by generous margins, soft pastel fills, and rounded corners that echo the handcrafted, retro-fantasy mood. Loading and error states simplify this stacked column into a single centered status card, while success and validation feedback appear inline, gently nudging the user without breaking the vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader conceptually wraps the entire visible moon event content area, spanning the full width of the centered content column. It does not have a visible card or dedicated space of its own; instead, it governs whether the main stack of cards (breadcrumbs, event detail, related articles, planner) is shown, or whether the loading indicator or error panel takes over that central area. Spatially, it is aligned with the top of the content column just below the illustrated site header and above the lowest visible component, but it remains invisible in the final UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible wrapper component; controls conditional rendering of the central content stack but does not occupy visible space or alter the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The moon event header sits at the very top inside the main moon event detail card. The card itself is centered in the page, spanning roughly 90–100% of the content column width, with 16–24px of padding on all sides. Within this card, the header occupies the top 20–25% of the card’s vertical space on initial load. The event title is left-aligned (or slightly inset from the left edge) and stretches across most of the card width, set in a large, bold purple heading that immediately draws attention. Above or to the left of the title, a small circular or crescent moon illustration may appear, tucked into the top-left corner with about 8–16px breathing room from the card edge. Any subtitle or phase label sits just below the main title with a smaller font size and a brighter accent color. The header area has a bit more top padding (around 24px) than the rest of the card to give the title room to breathe, echoing the blog-like hero feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main event detail card; occupies the top portion of that card and participates in the normal vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meta panel sits directly beneath the moon event header inside the same main event detail card, separated from the title by a small vertical gap of about 8–12px. It appears as a rounded, pill-like strip or compact card spanning roughly 100% of the card’s inner width, with 12–16px padding and a pastel background (mint or soft lavender) to distinguish it from the white or lighter card background. The meta content (date, exact time, moon phase, zodiac sign, visibility) is arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens, with 2–4 evenly spaced chunks separated by soft vertical dividers or generous spacing. On narrower screens, these chunks wrap into two rows, maintaining equal left and right padding so text never touches the panel edges. This panel visually occupies about 10–15% of the event detail card’s height, but is visually light and compact, supporting the title rather than competing with it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the event detail card; expands vertically as needed for multiple rows but always aligns flush with the card’s inner left and right padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section fills the lower portion of the main event detail card, beginning approximately 16–24px below the bottom of the meta panel. It uses the full inner width of the card, with text aligned to the left and consistent 16–24px padding maintained on the sides. The first paragraph sits slightly closer to the meta panel for continuity, while subsequent paragraphs are spaced with about 8–12px between them, creating a comfortable reading rhythm. If there are subheadings (e.g., 'Emotional Themes', 'Ritual Suggestions'), each subheading is given an extra top margin of around 16–24px and possibly a colored accent underline to break up the text without adding extra containers. The description portion typically occupies 50–60% of the card’s vertical space when content is moderate in length, and it scrolls naturally with the page. The bottom of this section ends with slightly more padding (about 24px) before the rounded bottom of the card, echoing the breathing room at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls as part of the main page; expands vertically depending on text length but never exceeds the card’s horizontal bounds.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related articles list is housed in its own rounded card, stacked directly beneath the main event detail card. It is centered in the same content column, matching the width and alignment of the event card. There is a clear lavender gap of about 16–24px between the two cards to visually separate them. Inside, a small header row at the top of the card (occupying about 15–20% of the card height) contains a section title like 'Related Ritual Guides' aligned to the left, with optional small decorative icons aligned to the right. Below this, a vertical list of 3–6 related items fills the remaining space. Each item appears as a mini row with the article title in a medium-sized accent color and a subtle secondary line for type or phase (e.g., 'Full Moon Ritual', 'Tarot Spread'). Items are separated by 8–12px vertical spacing or light dividers. The card’s padding is moderate (16–20px), and the overall card height typically occupies 20–30% of the visible stack, depending on the number of items. The card reads like a curated reading list, clearly secondary to the main event article but still visually substantial.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card within the content column; expands vertically with the number of list items while preserving consistent top and bottom padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual filter helper operates entirely behind the scenes, influencing which related rituals and default planner options appear but not presenting any visible UI of its own. Logically, it sits between the moon event data and both the related articles list and the ritual planner quick-add form, but it does not alter the spatial arrangement. No extra card, pill, or spacing is introduced for this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible logic component; does not occupy layout space and does not change the column structure or card positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual planner quick-add form is placed in a distinct rounded card stacked beneath the related articles card, maintaining the same centered alignment and width within the content column. There is a 16–24px vertical gap of lavender background separating it from the related articles card above. This planner card is slightly more compact horizontally, with comfortable 20–24px padding on all sides, making it feel like a friendly, focused action area. At the top of the card, a short heading and brief explanatory line occupy the upper 20–25% of the card, left-aligned and using accent colors for emphasis. Below this, form fields (e.g., ritual name prefilled from the event, date/time picker, intention notes) are stacked vertically, each spanning 100% of the card’s inner width. Each input has around 8–12px vertical spacing from the next, with labels placed above the fields. The primary action button (e.g., 'Add to Planner') is anchored near the bottom of the card, aligned left or centered, with extra top margin (16–24px) to visually separate it from the last input. Overall, this card occupies roughly 20–30% of the vertical space of the stacked layout, clearly signaling an interactive step after reading and browsing related content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the main flow; expands vertically with form complexity but remains within the single centered column. Button stays inline, not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner entry success panel appears conditionally in close proximity to the ritual planner quick-add form. When shown, it renders as a compact, rounded pill or mini card with a soft green-tinted background and a subtle checkmark icon. It is positioned inside the planner card, directly above the form heading, maintaining the same inner left/right padding as the rest of the card. The panel spans nearly the full inner width of the planner card and is about 48–64px tall, with 12–16px padding and text centered vertically. This keeps the success feedback visually tied to the form without significantly increasing page height. If the layout is constrained, the success panel pushes the form content downward slightly but does not overlap; the overall card simply grows taller by roughly one panel height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed inline within the planner card; participates in normal flow, pushing form content down when visible. Not fixed or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the page is loading moon event data, the loading indicator replaces the main content stack within the centered column. It appears as a rounded, medium-sized card or panel located roughly in the vertical center of the viewport within the content column, spanning about 60–70% of the column width. Inside, a circular or crescent-shaped spinner sits centered horizontally, with a short 'loading' message below it. The panel has 24–32px of padding, giving it an airy feel similar to other cards, and is surrounded by generous lavender background on all sides. No event detail, related articles, or planner cards are shown beneath it during this state, so the eye is drawn immediately to this central status element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the main content stack; centered static panel within the column. It does not scroll independently but moves with the page if scrolled.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In an error state (for example, when the moon event fails to load), the error panel occupies the same central position as the main content would, effectively substituting the event detail and its associated cards. It appears as a prominent rounded card centered in the content column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the column width with generous 24–32px padding. The top of the card contains a bold, red-tinted error title aligned to the left, with a small expressive icon near it (e.g., a cracked moon), taking up about the top 20–25% of the card. Below, a concise explanatory message and a clear primary 'Try Again' or 'Back to Events' button are stacked vertically, with 12–16px spacing between them. The overall card height is moderate—about 25–35% of the viewport—so it feels substantial but not overwhelming, clearly communicating the problem while maintaining the soft, playful aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces the main content stack; static card that sits within the centered column and participates in normal scrolling if the page is longer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner form validation helper controls how error messages appear around the fields inside the ritual planner quick-add form but does not occupy a separate block in the layout. Its influence is visible as small red or accent-colored validation messages that appear just below each problematic input field, aligned with the left edge of the field and wrapped at the same width. These messages add about 16–24px of vertical height beneath each affected field, pushing subsequent fields and the button downward. The overall planner card grows slightly taller to accommodate these messages, but the structure and alignment of the card remain intact.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible logic component; introduces inline, field-level validation messages within the existing vertical stack of the planner form without adding independent containers.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs appear as a full-width rounded pill bar positioned near the top of the content column, immediately below the global illustration header and any primary site navigation (not part of this component list). This bar spans about 90–100% of the content column width and is centered, with 12–16px of inner padding and about 16–24px of lavender background above and below it. Its height is relatively compact, using about 8–10% of the initial viewport height. Breadcrumb links are left-aligned inside the pill, separated by small decorative separators (e.g., '>' or tiny stars). The text is moderately sized and spaced so that each breadcrumb has comfortable breathing room, giving the bar a simple, unobtrusive feel. The main event detail card begins roughly 16–24px below the bottom of this breadcrumb bar, preserving the stacked-pill pattern from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation bar; anchored near the top of the main content column and scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page. Not sticky or floating.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Search and refine astrology and tarot articles, apply date and content filters, sort by rating or recency, and quickly open, favorite, or save articles to your reading list.

core_features: Execute a global article search based on the query term, filters, date range, and sort order using the searchArticles interface.
Allow users to refine results with date range presets and advanced filters (article type, zodiac sign, tags, etc.), encoded in the filters parameter.
Provide sort options such as rating descending, newest first, or relevance, bound to the sortBy parameter.
Display search results as a list of articles with title, snippet, rating, publish date, reading time (if available), and tag metadata.
Enable saving any search result article to the Reading List via saveArticleToReadingList, including a shortcut to open the Reading List page.
Enable favoriting any search result article via favoriteArticle, including a shortcut to open the Favorites page.
Support deep-linkable, shareable searches by encoding query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters in URL parameters and re-running searchArticles on load.
user_workflows: Run a new search from the header: User types a search term into the global search field in the header and submits. The page navigates (or reloads) to search_results.html with a query URL parameter (and optional sortBy/filters). On load, the page reads query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters from the URL, invokes searchArticles with these values, and renders the resulting Article list.

Refine by date range: On the Search Results page, the user selects a date range preset or defines a custom range in the filters bar. The page updates the dateRange URL parameter and merges the chosen range into the encoded filters parameter. The URL is updated, searchArticles is called again using the updated query, filters, and sortBy, and the results list refreshes.

Refine by article type or zodiac sign: The user selects or deselects options such as article_type or zodiac_sign in the filters bar. The page updates the filters URL parameter (encoded to include the selected options), preserves the current query, dateRange, and sortBy, updates the URL, calls searchArticles with the new filters, and re-renders the results.

Change sort order: The user chooses a sort option (e.g., rating_desc, newest, relevance) from the sort selector. The page updates the sortBy URL parameter while keeping query, dateRange, and filters unchanged, calls searchArticles with the new sortBy parameter, and updates the results list accordingly.

Open an article from results: The user clicks a result title or a "Read More" action within a result item. The page navigates to article.html with the appropriate id URL parameter taken from the Article.id of the clicked result.

Save an article to Reading List from results: The user clicks a "Save to Reading List" action on a result item. The page calls saveArticleToReadingList with articleId from the selected Article and source set to "search_results". On successful response, the UI reflects the saved state for that article and shows a contextual action (e.g., a banner or inline link) that allows navigation to reading_list.html.

Favorite an article from results: The user clicks a "Add to Favorites" action on a result item. The page calls favoriteArticle with articleId from the selected Article and source set to "search_results". On successful response, the UI toggles or confirms the favorited state and shows a contextual action that allows navigation to favorites.html.

Share or revisit a search: The user copies the URL from the browser or bookmarks it. When the URL is later opened (by the same or another user), the page reads query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters from the URL, calls searchArticles with these values, and reconstructs the same filtered and sorted search view.
interactions: Submitting the global search form triggers a navigation or reload of search_results.html with a query URL parameter (and optionally sortBy and filters) and invokes searchArticles on page initialization.
Changing date range options triggers an update of the dateRange URL parameter and the filters URL parameter (to include date range constraints), followed by a call to searchArticles.
Toggling or adjusting article-type, zodiac-sign, or tag filters updates the filters URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles.
Selecting a sort option updates the sortBy URL parameter and re-runs searchArticles while maintaining existing query and filters.
Clicking an article title or "Read More" link navigates to article.html with the id parameter set to the clicked Article.id.

Clicking a "Save to Reading List" control on a result item calls saveArticleToReadingList; on success, the UI for that item switches to a saved state and a control to navigate to reading_list.html becomes available.
Clicking a "Add to Favorites" control on a result item calls favoriteArticle; on success, the UI for that item indicates it is favorited and a control to navigate to favorites.html becomes available.
If searchArticles returns an empty list, the page shows an empty-state message and keeps the search and filters active so the user can easily adjust query, dateRange, or filters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully driven by URL parameters. On load, the page parses query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters from the current URL. The filters parameter is treated as a serialized structure (for example, JSON-like) that can encode multiple filter dimensions, including but not limited to article_type, zodiac_sign, tags, and date constraints. The dateRange parameter reflects the user's chosen date preset or custom range in a normalized string form for readability and sharing. To execute a search, the page calls searchArticles with: query taken directly from the query URL parameter; filters taken directly from the filters URL parameter (ensuring that any chosen date range is embedded in this encoded filter structure); and sortBy taken directly from the sortBy URL parameter. Whenever the user changes any control that affects search (query, date range, filters, or sortBy), the page updates the corresponding URL parameters and pushes a new browser history entry or replaces the current one. After each state change, the page calls searchArticles again with the updated parameters and re-renders the results. Using the browser's back/forward buttons changes the URL, which the page listens to; it re-parses the active URL parameters and re-invokes searchArticles so the UI stays synchronized with the URL state. No search or filter state is persisted in localStorage or other client storage; the URL is the single source of truth for this page's state.

components.functionality: Accepts a free-text query from the user, binds it to the query URL parameter, and triggers a new search by navigating to or reloading search_results.html. When the page loads, it initializes the input value from the current query URL parameter so the user sees the term that produced the current results.
event_handlers: onInit: Read query from URL parameters and set the form input value.
onChangeQueryInput: Update a local query value used for the next submission without changing the current URL yet.

onSubmit: Construct a new URL for search_results.html including query and any preserved dateRange, sortBy, and filters parameters as appropriate, then navigate to that URL so that the page reloads and calls searchArticles with the updated query.

components.functionality: Provides interactive controls for setting the date range and additional filters such as article_type, zodiac_sign, and tags. Encodes the chosen filter values into the filters URL parameter and keeps the dateRange URL parameter in sync with any date range selection. After updates, it triggers a new search via searchArticles.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse filters and dateRange from URL parameters, decode the filters structure (if present), and pre-populate filter controls to match the active state.

onChangeDateRangePreset: Update dateRange in the URL to the selected preset value and merge an equivalent date constraint into the encoded filters structure before calling searchArticles with the updated filters and existing query and sortBy.

onApplyCustomDateRange: Convert the user-selected start and end dates into a normalized string stored in dateRange, inject an appropriate representation of this range into the filters structure, update the filters URL parameter, update the dateRange parameter, then call searchArticles.
onChangeArticleTypeFilter: Toggle or set article_type values within the filters structure, update the filters URL parameter, and re-run searchArticles.
onChangeZodiacSignFilter: Toggle or set zodiac_sign values within the filters structure, update the filters URL parameter, and re-run searchArticles.
onChangeOtherFilter: Update the relevant key within the filters structure (for example, tags or rating thresholds), update the filters URL parameter, and re-run searchArticles.
onResetFilters: Clear filter-related fields in the filters structure and, if appropriate, clear dateRange in the URL, then call searchArticles using only the query and sortBy parameters.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select how search results are ordered (for example, by rating, recency, or relevance) and synchronizes the choice with the sortBy URL parameter. Changing the sort order triggers a new call to searchArticles.
event_handlers: onInit: Read sortBy from the URL and set the selected sort option accordingly.
onChangeSortBy: Update the sortBy URL parameter to the newly selected value, keep the existing query, dateRange, and filters parameters intact, and call searchArticles with the updated sortBy.

components.functionality: Displays a concise summary of the current search state, such as the active query and the number of results currently returned by searchArticles, and reflects active filters and sort order for user clarity.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsLoaded: Receive the latest array from searchArticles, compute the number of results, and read query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters from the URL to display an updated textual summary.
onClearQuery: Optionally clear the query parameter in the URL and trigger a new search if the user chooses to remove the search term.

components.functionality: Renders the list of search results returned from searchArticles. For each Article, it creates a corresponding result item component that shows metadata and interaction controls for reading, favoriting, and saving to the reading list.
