event_handlers: onSearchResultsLoaded: Receive the array of Article objects from searchArticles and generate or update a set of search-result-item components, one per article.
onEmptyResults: When the Article array is empty, trigger the empty-state component to show guidance and hide or disable result-specific actions.

components.functionality: Represents a single Article returned by searchArticles. Shows the article's title, summary snippet, rating, publish_date, reading_time_minutes (if available), and tags. Provides actions to open the article, add it to Favorites, and save it to the Reading List.
event_handlers: onClickTitle: Navigate to article.html with id set to the Article.id of this result.
onClickReadMore: Navigate to article.html with id set to the Article.id of this result.

onClickSaveToReadingList: Call saveArticleToReadingList with articleId from this Article.id and source set to "search_results". On successful response, mark this result as saved in the UI and notify the reading-list-cta component with the returned reading_list_item_id and message.

onClickAddToFavorites: Call favoriteArticle with articleId from this Article.id and source set to "search_results". On successful response, mark this result as favorited in the UI and notify the favorites-cta component with the returned favorite_item_id and message.

onInitItemState: Optionally, when the result item is created, use available in-memory state (from prior successful calls on this page) to reflect whether this article was saved to the Reading List or favorited in this session, without relying on localStorage.

components.functionality: Shows contextual feedback and navigation options after an article is saved to the Reading List from the search results, and provides an entry point to open the Reading List page.
event_handlers: onReadingListSaveSuccess: Receive the response from saveArticleToReadingList (including success, reading_list_item_id, and message) and display an appropriate notification or banner.
onClickViewReadingList: Navigate to reading_list.html without additional parameters.
onDismissNotification: Hide the current notification without affecting saved state.

components.functionality: Shows contextual feedback and navigation options after an article is favorited from the search results, and provides an entry point to open the Favorites page.
event_handlers: onFavoriteSuccess: Receive the response from favoriteArticle (including success, favorite_item_id, and message) and display an appropriate notification or banner.
onClickViewFavorites: Navigate to favorites.html without additional parameters.
onDismissNotification: Hide the current notification without affecting favorited state.

components.functionality: Displays an informative message and basic suggestions when searchArticles returns no Article results while keeping all active search parameters visible and editable.
event_handlers: onSearchResultsLoaded: If the Article array is empty, render the empty-state message; if not empty, hide or collapse this component.
onClickAdjustFilters: Focus the search-filters-bar component or otherwise guide the user to modify filters or dateRange to broaden the search.
onClickEditQuery: Focus the global-search-form input to encourage the user to adjust the query.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters URL parameters, triggers searchArticles whenever these parameters change, and coordinates state updates among the other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse query, dateRange, sortBy, and filters from the current URL, then call searchArticles with these values. Distribute the returned Article array to the search-results-list and related components.

onUrlChange: Listen for browser history events (such as back and forward navigation), re-parse URL parameters, re-invoke searchArticles, and notify all dependent components of the new state and results.

onSearchParametersUpdate: When any component requests a parameter change (query, dateRange, sortBy, filters), update the URL accordingly, manage history (push or replace), then call searchArticles with the updated parameters and propagate results to dependent components.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog-style mockup. Above this search layout, the existing watercolor site header and site-wide navigation remain. Directly below that header, search-specific components are stacked in a clear vertical order: first the global search form (playing the role of the main navigation pill), then a dedicated filters bar (analogous to the secondary nav/breadcrumb band), then a compact summary + sort band, followed by the main search results list (which visually replaces the article card in the mockup). CTAs and empty state messages are treated as additional cards inserted into this vertical flow, always full-width within the content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog-style mockup. Above this search layout, the existing watercolor site header and site-wide navigation remain. Directly below that header, search-specific components are stacked in a clear vertical order: first the global search form (playing the role of the main navigation pill), then a dedicated filters bar (analogous to the secondary nav/breadcrumb band), then a compact summary + sort band, followed by the main search results list (which visually replaces the article card in the mockup). CTAs and empty state messages are treated as additional cards inserted into this vertical flow, always full-width within the content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The same loose 12-column, single-column feel is preserved: the main content column spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width up to a fixed max-width (around 960–1040px), centered horizontally. The site’s illustrated header occupies roughly the top 25–30% of the viewport. Within the search layout: the global search form band uses about 10–12% of the viewport height with generous inner padding; the filters bar and the summary/sort band together consume roughly another 12–15% of vertical space near the top; the search results list then stretches to fill the remaining vertical space, extending down the page as the user scrolls. Each major band (search form, filters, summary/sort, results list, CTAs/empty state) is separated by 16–24px of vertical margin, and each pill/card has 16–24px of internal padding, keeping ample lavender background visible between elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and readable, following the mockup: macro-elements (the various bars and cards) are spaced apart with comfortable gaps, while the content inside each card is reasonably compact and legible. Text lines in summaries and result items stay within a comfortable reading width (around 60–70 characters). The global search form has room around the input and buttons, avoiding crowding. The filters bar and summary/sort band keep controls well spaced horizontally, with small gaps between chips and dropdowns but not so much that they feel sparse. Result items use standard paragraph spacing and a compact metadata row, echoing the article card’s internal density from the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins at the existing watercolor site header and logo at the top. The eye then drops naturally to the prominent global search form band centered below it. From there, it flows down to the filters bar (a strong horizontal pill), then to the lighter summary + sort band. This band subtly shifts attention from filtering to viewing the actual results. From that point, the eye moves straight down into the stacked list of result cards, reading each title, metadata line, snippet, and action row in sequence. Any inline CTAs appear as distinct but harmonious cards interspersed within or just above the results, and the empty state card (when shown) stands in the same results region, creating a continuous top-to-bottom reading path consistent with the mockup’s classic blog article flow.

layout.overall_description: The search results page appears as a friendly, pastel-toned column of rounded cards and bars floating over a lavender background, directly beneath the site’s watercolor header. Centered in the viewport, a wide mint or cream pill holds the global search field, inviting the user to refine their query. Just below, a slightly slimmer filters bar stretches across the same column, with chip-like controls and dropdowns laid out from left to right, echoing a navigation/breadcrumb band. A third, even more compact pill follows: on its left it states how many results were found and what they’re for, and on its right a small, rounded sort selector lets the user toggle between recency and rating. Below this summary band, the layout opens into the main results region: a tall, softly shaded container where individual result cards stack vertically with even gaps, each card showing a title, metadata line, short description, and quick actions. Occasional notification banners for reading lists or favorites appear as additional pastel cards slotted into this column, never breaking the single-column rhythm. When no results are available, a larger, illustration-led empty state card appears in place of the results list, comfortably centered within the same column. Throughout, generous margins between bands, rounded corners, and pastel backgrounds maintain the playful, handcrafted, slightly retro astrology-blog atmosphere, while a strict top-to-bottom flow keeps the experience simple and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form sits directly below the site’s watercolor header and any primary site navigation, becoming the first search-specific element in the centered column. It appears as a wide, rounded pill-shaped container that spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width up to the column’s max-width (around 960–1040px), and is horizontally centered. There is a generous 24px margin above it (separating it from the header) and a 16–24px margin below it (before the filters bar). Inside the pill, there is 16–24px of padding on all sides. The main search input occupies the central majority of the pill, set as a large, easily tappable field running left-to-right, with a soft outline and slightly retro styling. To the right (within the same pill) sits the primary search button and possibly a secondary icon button (e.g., for advanced options), aligned vertically to the center of the input. Any helper text, such as “Search tarot spreads, horoscopes, and guides…”, is placed beneath the input within the same container, in a smaller font and lighter color, but still with enough breathing room so it doesn’t feel cramped. On smaller screens, the input and button stack vertically within this pill but keep the same rounded, airy aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The search form scrolls naturally with the page and is not sticky; it anchors the top of the search layout. On responsive breakpoints, it maintains its centered alignment and full-column width, while internal elements (input, buttons) reflow from horizontal alignment on desktop to a stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-filters bar is positioned immediately beneath the global search form, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical lavender background. It visually echoes the navigation/breadcrumb bars from the mockup: a full-width, rounded rectangle (pill-like) that fills nearly the entire content column width, centered, with 16–20px of inner padding. The height of this band is moderate—roughly 8–10% of the initial viewport height on desktop—enough to fit one main row of controls comfortably. Filter chips and dropdowns are arranged from left to right: for example, a prominent filter label like “Filters” or an icon sits at the far left, followed by pill-shaped chips for content type (Astrology, Tarot), date range, and perhaps a tag or category selector. Small, 8px gaps separate each chip. Any “Clear filters” text link is placed toward the right end, aligned vertically with the controls but visually lighter. On narrow screens, this bar can wrap into two rows, with filters stacking while still contained within the same rounded bar. The overall impression is a horizontal control strip, cleanly separated from both the search form above and the summary band below by consistent vertical margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filters bar becomes sticky at the top edge of the viewport once the user scrolls past the header and search form, so it remains visible as a persistent control band. When sticky, it pins just beneath any fixed site header, maintaining its full width within the content column. On very small screens, horizontal scrolling is allowed if many filter chips are present, while the bar itself remains fixed at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-sort selector is not isolated as a separate band; instead, it is visually integrated into the same horizontal band as the search results summary, forming a compact card directly under the filters bar. Within this shallow pill-shaped card, the sort selector is anchored to the far right, aligned on the same baseline as the summary text on the left. It appears as a small, rounded dropdown or segmented control, about one-quarter to one-third of the card’s width, with concise labels such as “Newest”, “Top Rated”, or “Most Relevant”. The control has 8–12px of internal padding and enough surrounding whitespace to distinctively separate it from the summary text. On mobile, when space is tight, the sort selector wraps underneath the summary, still right-aligned but appearing on its own line within the same card, maintaining the vertical rhythm of a title row followed by a control row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The sort selector scrolls with the page as part of the summary band. It does not become sticky on its own, but remains just below the sticky filters bar, so it is still near the top of the viewport when users are interacting with the top of the results list. Responsively, its width shrinks and its label may truncate or use shorter text, but the control itself maintains touch-friendly padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results summary sits immediately below the filters bar, separated by about 16px of vertical space. It takes the form of a low-profile, full-width rounded card that spans the content column, visually lighter than the search form and filters bar. The card has 12–16px of padding on all sides, making it shallower than other components to signal its role as a status line rather than a main interaction area. On the left side, the text reads something like “18 results for ‘Venus retrograde’” in a slightly emphasized style, potentially followed by small inline badges indicating key active filters (e.g., a tiny chip reading “Tarot only”). These badges align in a horizontal row directly under or following the main summary text, with small 4–8px gaps between them. On the right side of this same band, sharing the horizontal space, sits the search-sort selector. The band feels like a bridge between filters above and the results list below, with its reduced height and lightweight styling maintaining clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The summary band scrolls with the page and does not stick; it sits just below the sticky filters bar so that users see it when they first reach the top of the results. On narrower screens, the layout inside the band reflows, with the summary text occupying the top row and the sort selector moving onto a second row beneath it, both still contained within the same rounded card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results list begins right below the summary band, separated by about 24px of vertical space to emphasize the transition from controls to content. It occupies the full width of the content column, but unlike the pill-like bands above, the list is conceptually a tall vertical area that contains multiple stacked result-item cards. The background behind the list remains the lavender page color, while each result item appears as its own white or pale-cream card floating within the column. The list extends downward as the user scrolls, potentially spanning several viewport heights. Between each result card there is a consistent 16–24px vertical gap, ensuring that the cards are clearly separated yet still feel like part of a cohesive series. The first visible result item sits close to the top of this region, with just enough top padding to avoid feeling cramped beneath the summary (roughly 8–12px). Inline CTAs (like reading-list-cta and favorites-cta) can be visually treated as additional cards interleaved with these result items, occupying the same width and alignment within this list region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list is a scrollable content region that grows as additional results are loaded. It does not have its own internal scrolling; it simply lives within the page flow. Infinite scroll or “Load more” controls, if present, would appear at the bottom of this list as additional cards. On smaller screens, each result item stretches edge-to-edge inside the content column while maintaining consistent gutters from the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each search-result item is rendered as an individual card within the search-results list. The card has a softly rounded rectangle shape, with a white or very pale pastel background and a subtle shadow or border to lift it off the lavender page. It spans the full width of the content column, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and a 16–24px vertical margin separating it from adjacent items. Inside the card, content follows a classic article-like vertical structure. At the top, a bold, purple or dark-ink title sits left-aligned, occupying the full width and allowing up to two lines before truncation. Directly beneath the title, a horizontal metadata row shows the publication date, rating (stars or score), and possibly a small tag like “Tarot Spread” or “Daily Horoscope”, separated by small dot separators or subtle dividers; this row is lighter in color and uses smaller text. Below the metadata, a brief summary paragraph runs nearly the full width, set in standard body size and line height, limited to two or three lines to keep each card compact. At the bottom of the card, a secondary row houses quick actions: on the left, a subtle text link like “Open reading” or “View details”, and on the right, small icon buttons for favoriting (heart), saving to reading list (bookmark), or sharing. These bottom actions are aligned with the card’s left and right padding respectively, forming a neat horizontal line. On larger screens, an optional small square thumbnail or icon (e.g., a tarot card or zodiac symbol) may be placed to the left of the text block, with the title and metadata aligned to its right; on smaller screens this thumbnail stacks above the text so that the card remains one clean column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Result-item cards scroll normally as part of the list. They do not float or overlay other components. However, interactions within the card (like favoriting or saving) may trigger subtle visual feedback (e.g., changing the heart icon color) without affecting the card’s layout. On responsive breakpoints, any optional thumbnail repositions from side-by-side to stacked, maintaining generous padding and the same top-to-bottom content order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-cta appears as a notification-style banner card that visually matches the other pill and card elements but uses a slightly more vibrant pastel (e.g., mint or pale yellow) to stand out. It is placed within the main vertical flow of the search results area, typically either directly above the first result item or inserted after the first few results, depending on user behavior. The card spans the full width of the content column and has 16–24px of internal padding, with 24px of vertical margin above and below to clearly separate it from neighboring result cards. Inside, the layout is horizontally oriented on desktop: a small, whimsical icon or badge sits at the far left, the main message text (“Save your favorite readings to a personal list”) occupies the center in one or two short lines, and a primary action button (“Start your reading list”) sits aligned to the right. The text is left-aligned, and the button is visually strong but compact. On mobile, the elements stack: icon on top, message text in the middle, and the button full-width at the bottom of the card, each separated by 8–12px. The overall height of this banner is slightly taller than a typical result card snippet but still fits naturally into the scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The reading-list-cta scrolls with the results and does not stick to the viewport. It may be dismissible: when dismissed, the card collapses and the surrounding result items close the gap, maintaining the same vertical rhythm. Its insertion point within the results list may be dynamic, but wherever it appears it respects the same column width and margins as result items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-cta is a second notification-style banner card, slightly more understated than the reading-list-cta to avoid overwhelming the page. It shares the same full-width, rounded card format within the content column and similar padding (16–20px) but may use a softer pastel (e.g., muted lavender or pale pink) and slightly smaller text. It is typically placed further down the results list—either after a certain number of result items or near interactions with favorite icons—so it feels contextual. Inside the card, a concise message (“Tap the heart to favorite readings for quick access”) is left-aligned and may be accompanied by a small inline heart icon. A lighter secondary action link (“View all favorites”) can be placed on the right or beneath the message. The banner’s height is modest, closer to that of a single result item’s content, ensuring it doesn’t dominate the list but still catches the eye as users scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Like the reading-list-cta, the favorites-cta is part of the normal scroll flow and may be dismissible, collapsing out of the list when closed. It does not become sticky and has no special fixed positioning. On smaller screens, any right-aligned link or secondary action wraps onto a second line to preserve a comfortable minimum width for the main message text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-empty-state replaces the search-results list when no matching articles are found. It is positioned in the same place where the results list would begin: directly below the summary band, with 24px of vertical space separating it from the filters/summary region. The empty state appears as a single, larger card centered within the content column, spanning the full column width but with slightly more generous internal padding (24–32px) to give it a spacious, welcoming feel. At the top of the card, there is room for a whimsical illustration (e.g., an empty tarot table or a sleeping constellation), centered horizontally and taking up roughly the top third of the card’s height. Below the illustration, a bold heading such as “No readings match this search yet” sits centered, followed by a short explanatory sentence in a lighter tone. Under that, a vertical stack of suggestions—like a list of alternative popular searches or a row of pill-shaped buttons to clear filters, broaden date ranges, or explore featured content—fills the lower portion of the card. The card itself sits high enough on the page that users see it immediately after the filters and summary, but it also includes a bit of extra bottom padding so the illustration and text aren’t compressed against the edges. On taller screens, the surrounding lavender background above and below the card helps visually center it within the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty-state card scrolls with the page but is typically fully visible without scrolling on most desktop viewports once the user has the filters and summary in view. When results become available (e.g., after changing filters), this card disappears and is replaced by the normal results list. It does not use sticky positioning or overlays; it simply occupies the vertical space where results would otherwise appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is a non-visual component that does not produce any visible UI elements on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the page level, wrapping or orchestrating the search-related components (global-search-form, filters bar, summary, sort selector, and results list). It ensures that the state of the search query, selected filters, sort mode, and pagination are encoded into and restored from the URL query string. While it has no height, width, or visible position in the layout, its presence is reflected indirectly: when the user modifies the search, filters, or sorting, the page does not visually jump; instead, the existing layout remains stable while the results list content updates according to the state represented in the URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component occupies no physical space and has no spatial behavior in the visual layout. It operates in the background as a controller, tying UI state to the URL so that browser navigation (back/forward) and sharing links reproduce the same search configuration. From a layout perspective, it is effectively invisible and does not impact spacing or alignment of any visible components.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: Displays the user’s saved astrology and tarot articles, with controls to sort, filter, open, and remove items.

core_features: Load reading list items using current URL parameters for sorting and filtering via getReadingListItems(sortBy, filters).
Display each saved ReadingListItem with its associated Article details (title, summary, article_type, zodiac_sign, reading_time_minutes, rating, publish_date, tags, saved_at, source).
Provide sorting controls that update the sortBy URL parameter and reload the list.
Provide filtering controls (by article_type, zodiac_sign, and tag/topic) that update the filters URL parameter and reload the list.
Allow users to open any saved article by navigating to article.html with the article id as a URL parameter.
Allow users to remove individual items from the reading list by calling removeReadingListItem and updating the displayed list.
Handle empty, loading, and error states for the reading list in a functional way (e.g., show no-results state when no items are returned).
Preserve current sorting and filtering in the URL so the state can be shared, bookmarked, and restored on reload or navigation back from other pages.
user_workflows:
1. Initial page load
   - User navigates to reading_list.html, possibly with existing sortBy and filters URL parameters.
   - The page controller reads sortBy from the URL; if absent, it derives a default value and uses it for the initial call to getReadingListItems.
   - The page controller reads filters from the URL; if present, it parses the encoded filters into a usable in-page state for the filter controls.
   - The page calls getReadingListItems(sortBy, filters) and waits for the response.
   - On success, the page renders the list of items and passes sorting/filtering values into the controls.
   - On failure, the page shows an error state with a retry interaction that re-calls getReadingListItems using the same URL parameters.

2. Change sort order for saved items
   - User interacts with a sort control (for example, choosing a new sort option such as one of the supported sortBy values).
   - The sort control updates the sortBy parameter in the URL, replacing any existing sortBy value while preserving filters.
   - The browser navigates or updates history to the new URL containing the updated sortBy parameter.
   - The page controller detects the URL change and calls getReadingListItems with the new sortBy and the current filters.
   - The list component receives the updated items and re-renders the reading list according to the chosen sort order.

3. Apply or change filters
   - User interacts with one or more filter controls (e.g., selects a zodiac_sign, one or more article_type options, or topics represented by tags).
   - The filter component composes an encoded representation of the filter selections and sets/updates the filters parameter in the URL while preserving the current sortBy.
   - The browser navigates or updates history with the new filters value.
   - The page controller reads the updated filters from the URL and calls getReadingListItems(sortBy, filters).
   - The reading list items component re-renders based on the API response constrained by the new filters.
   - If no items are returned, the empty-state component is shown with functional guidance to clear or adjust filters.

4. Open an article from the reading list
   - User clicks on the title (or dedicated open action) of a specific reading list item.
   - The item row component retrieves the article.article_id for that row.
   - The page triggers navigation to article.html with the article id passed as the id URL parameter (for example: article.html?id={article.article_id}), without modifying the current reading_list.html URL parameters.
   - When the user later navigates back to the reading list (e.g., via browser back or My Library navigation), the previous sortBy and filters values in the reading_list.html URL restore the same list state.

5. Remove an item from the reading list
   - User clicks a remove action on a specific reading list item.
   - The item row component calls removeReadingListItem(readingListItemId) using the reading_list_item_id for that row.
   - If the API indicates success, the client removes that item from the in-memory list for the current view OR re-calls getReadingListItems with the existing sortBy and filters from the URL to refresh the list from the server.
   - The list component re-renders without the removed item.
   - If the last item is removed, the empty-state component is shown for the current filters.
   - If the API call fails, the error-state behavior is invoked for that specific row or for the page, allowing the user to attempt the removal again.

6. Clear filters and reset sorting
   - User clicks a control to reset filters and sort order.
   - The page controller or controls component constructs a URL for reading_list.html without the filters parameter and optionally with a default sortBy value.
   - The browser navigates to this URL.
   - The page controller reads the (now default) sortBy and empty filters and calls getReadingListItems.
   - The reading list items component shows the unfiltered, default-sorted reading list.

7. Retry after an error
   - If getReadingListItems fails, the error-state component is shown with a retry interaction.
   - When the user triggers retry, the controller calls getReadingListItems again with the same sortBy and filters read from the current URL.
   - On success, the error state is hidden and the list re-renders with the returned items.
   - If removeReadingListItem fails for a specific item, the item row can show a local error and offer a retry that re-calls removeReadingListItem for that readingListItemId.
interactions:
Page initialization
- Event: onPageLoad
- Behavior: Read sortBy and filters from the URL query string. Determine default sortBy if not present. Invoke getReadingListItems(sortBy, filters). Pass the response to the reading list components.

Sorting interactions
- Event: onChangeSort (user selects a new sort option from a control such as a dropdown or segmented control)
- Behavior: Update the sortBy value in the URL query string while preserving filters. Trigger navigation or history update. The page reload logic or URL-change listener re-invokes getReadingListItems with the new sortBy.

Filtering interactions
- Event: onChangeFilterValue (e.g., selecting or deselecting a zodiac_sign, article_type, or topic/tag)
- Behavior: Compose a representation of all active filters into the filters parameter. Update the filters query parameter in the URL while preserving sortBy. Trigger navigation/URL change, which leads to a new getReadingListItems call.

- Event: onClearFilters
- Behavior: Remove the filters parameter from the URL (or set it to an encoded empty state). Optionally reset sortBy to a default value. Trigger navigation/URL change and reload data via getReadingListItems.

Item interactions
- Event: onClickArticleTitle
- Behavior: Read the article.article_id for the clicked item and navigate to article.html?id={article.article_id}. This does not alter reading_list.html’s parameters.

- Event: onClickOpenItem (if separate from title)
- Behavior: Same as onClickArticleTitle.

- Event: onClickRemoveItem
- Behavior: Read the reading_list_item_id for that row. Call removeReadingListItem(reading_list_item_id). On success, either remove the item from the current in-memory list or re-fetch items via getReadingListItems using the current URL parameters. On failure, display an error message for that row and offer a retry.

Empty state and error state
- Event: onNoItemsReturned (items array length is zero after getReadingListItems)
- Behavior: Show a functional empty-state component that can surface controls to adjust or clear filters (which update the URL parameters and re-trigger getReadingListItems).

- Event: onGetItemsError
- Behavior: Display an error-state component with a retry action.

- Event: onRetryGetItems
- Behavior: Re-call getReadingListItems with the current sortBy and filters from the URL.

- Event: onRemoveItemError
- Behavior: Display a localized error on the affected row, and expose a retry action that calls removeReadingListItem again for that readingListItemId.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the single source of truth for reading-list view state.

1) sortBy parameter
- The page reads sortBy from the query string when initializing or when the URL changes.
- If sortBy is absent, the page determines and uses an internal default sort order and may or may not explicitly add it to the URL (implementation choice), but any subsequent changes must update the URL.
- All calls to getReadingListItems include the active sortBy value.
- When the user changes sorting, only the sortBy parameter is modified; filters is preserved as-is. This ensures the combination of sorting and filtering remains consistent.

2) filters parameter
- The filters parameter is a single encoded string representing multiple possible filter values (e.g., article_type, zodiac_sign, tag-based topics). The exact encoding format is handled consistently on read and write but is not hardcoded in this specification.
- On page load or URL change, the page decodes filters into an internal structure used by filter controls and then passes the original filters string to getReadingListItems.
- When a user modifies any filter control, the page recomputes the filters encoding and updates the filters parameter in the URL. If no filters are active, the filters parameter can be omitted or set to an encoded empty value, and that value is passed into getReadingListItems.

3) Combined state and navigation
- The pair (sortBy, filters) completely defines the data state for the reading list view. Any time either parameter changes in the URL, the page must re-call getReadingListItems using the current parameter values.
- Returning to the Reading List from other pages should retain and reapply the last used sortBy and filters, since they are encoded in the URL rather than stored in localStorage.
- All operations that impact which items are shown (applying filters, changing sort, clearing filters, resetting sorting) are implemented as URL mutations, not as local-only in-memory state.

4) Removal and data refresh
- Removing items via removeReadingListItem does not change sortBy or filters in the URL. Instead, the page either mutates the in-memory list or re-fetches using the existing URL parameters.
- Because getReadingListItems is always called with the current sortBy and filters, reloading the page after removals will show the updated list without requiring any local persistence beyond what is encoded in the URL.

5) Error and empty states
- Error and empty states do not change URL parameters by themselves; they are transient UI states based on the result of getReadingListItems and removeReadingListItem.
- If a user chooses to clear filters or change sorting from an empty state, that interaction updates the URL and therefore triggers new calls to getReadingListItems.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, data loading via getReadingListItems, and high-level state transitions between loading, error, empty, and list views.
Provides user controls to adjust sort order and filters (article_type, zodiac_sign, and topics/tags) and updates the URL parameters accordingly.
Displays a dynamic summary of the current reading list view, such as number of items returned and which filters/sorting are active, based solely on the current items and URL parameters.
Renders the list of saved reading list items returned by getReadingListItems, delegating item-specific actions (open and remove) to child components.
Represents a single ReadingListItem with its associated Article details and provides actions to open the article and remove the item.
Displays when getReadingListItems returns an empty items array, offering functional controls to clear or modify filters and reload the list.
Indicates that the reading list data is being fetched from getReadingListItems and hides when loading completes.
Displays a functional error message when getReadingListItems fails and provides a retry control.
Displays an inline error for a specific item row when removeReadingListItem fails, and allows retry for that item.
Centralizes reading and writing of sortBy and filters URL parameters, ensuring that any change to sorting or filters is reflected in the query string and triggers data reload.
Supports navigation from the Reading List to article detail pages using the article id and ensures that outgoing links preserve or do not conflict with the reading list’s URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered content column over a full-width lavender background. At the top of the viewport, the existing watercolor site header occupies the upper band. Directly beneath it, stack full-width, rounded bars in a strict vertical sequence: a navigation pill (reading-list-navigation-links) first, then a breadcrumb-like summary pill (reading-list-summary), followed by a large main card that contains the functional body of the page (reading-list-toolbar at the top and reading-list-items/content states below). Supporting components (errors, empty/loading states, inline errors) appear inside this main card in place of, or directly beneath, the list content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered content column over a full-width lavender background. At the top of the viewport, the existing watercolor site header occupies the upper band. Directly beneath it, stack full-width, rounded bars in a strict vertical sequence: a navigation pill (reading-list-navigation-links) first, then a breadcrumb-like summary pill (reading-list-summary), followed by a large main card that contains the functional body of the page (reading-list-toolbar at the top and reading-list-items/content states below). Supporting components (errors, empty/loading states, inline errors) appear inside this main card in place of, or directly beneath, the list content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The watercolor header should still visually occupy roughly the top 25–30% of the viewport. Below that, the navigation pill and the summary pill each take about 8–10% of the vertical height, with 16–24px of lavender background margin between each band. The main reading list card then occupies the remaining vertical space, expanding as needed to fit the list. Inside the main card, the toolbar takes a compact band at the top (~15–20% of the card height when the list is short), and the list/empty/loading/error content fills the rest. Each major card or pill has 16–24px inner padding and rounded corners, with visible lavender gutters around it.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: macro elements (navigation, summary pill, main card) are separated by noticeable vertical gaps (16–24px). Inside the main card, controls and text are spaced with 8–16px gaps: the toolbar controls are not cramped, list items have comfortable padding, and inline messages fit without feeling crowded. Rows within the list are compact enough to see multiple saved readings at once, but each row retains 12–16px of vertical padding so text doesn’t touch edges.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands on the colorful header illustration, then immediately drops to the bold navigation pill centered below it. From there, it moves down to the summary pill that clearly labels this as the Reading List and shows counts. Next, the eye is drawn to the main card with its purple or mint title text and the filter/sort toolbar at the top. The visual flow continues straight down through the list of items in a strong vertical rhythm: each item row reads top-to-bottom (title, metadata, controls), repeated in a column. When errors or empty/loading states are present, they occupy the same central area in the main card, preserving the same vertical reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Reading List page sits inside the site’s existing watercolor-and-lavender frame, with a single centered column over a soft lavender background. At the top, the existing illustrated header fills the upper quarter of the screen, with the site’s logo and playful watercolor textures. Just below, a wide, pill-shaped navigation bar floats in the center, spanning most of the content width and carrying key navigation links in bright, punchy text. A short distance beneath it, another rounded pill acts like a breadcrumb and summary: it states “Reading List” alongside a friendly item count and perhaps a small astrology icon, clearly anchoring the page’s purpose. Further down, a large, rounded main card takes over—the visual equivalent of the article card in the mockup. Its interior is softly padded, with a filter-and-sort toolbar row at the top and the user’s saved items listed beneath in vertically stacked sub-rows. Each saved reading appears as its own rounded mini-card with a bold title, metadata, and action buttons, all following the same playful, handcrafted style. When the list is empty or in error, a single, centered empty or error illustration and message fills that main card instead. The entire composition is calm but lively, with generous gaps between the stacked bands, a clear center alignment, and a top-to-bottom reading flow that feels like paging through a whimsical astrology journal.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Acts as the overarching layout container for the Reading List page, sitting directly under the global watercolor site header. It defines a single centered content column (for example, a max-width of about 960–1040px) with automatic horizontal margins so the column is centered on large screens and nearly full-width on small screens. All other Reading List components—navigation links pill, summary pill, main card with toolbar and items, and state elements—are stacked vertically inside this controller with 16–24px of vertical margin between each major section. The background outside this column remains lavender, while the components inside are mostly white or very pale mint/lavender cards with rounded corners. The controller itself contributes the consistent vertical rhythm and spacing rules for the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-scrolling, static wrapper element that grows vertically with its contents. It constrains the layout to the centered column and governs section spacing but is not visually distinct as its own card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits inside the top portion of the main reading list card, spanning the full inner width of that card. It is positioned immediately below the card’s top padding—roughly 16–24px from the card’s top edge—forming a horizontal band of controls. On larger screens, filters (e.g., category, tag, reading type) align left, while sort controls (e.g., ‘Newest’, ‘Oldest’) align right; they share one visual row with 8–16px gaps between controls so nothing feels cramped. The toolbar itself is not a separate card; instead, it appears as controls and small pill-shaped chips sitting directly on the card’s background. There is about 12–16px of vertical space below the toolbar before the list or content state begins, giving it breathing room and mirroring the spacing between metadata and main content in the mockup’s article card. On smaller screens, controls wrap to a second row but remain grouped tightly at the top, preserving the stacked reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main card. It scrolls out of view with the content; it is not sticky. It always appears at the top of the card above list items or fallback states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary component is a full-width, pill-shaped bar positioned directly beneath the navigation links pill and above the main reading list card. It is centered within the page controller column and spans roughly 90–100% of that column’s width, leaving a narrow lavender margin on both sides. Vertically, it is shorter than the navigation pill but taller than a simple label—approximately 48px height with 12–16px vertical padding inside. The content is left-aligned within the pill, with the text ‘Reading List’ displayed in a slightly larger, colorful heading style on the left and a secondary line or badge indicating the number of saved items and active filters on the right or directly beneath on small screens. There is a 16–24px gap of visible lavender background between this summary pill and the main card below, echoing the distinct stacked bands from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky pill that scrolls away with the rest of the content. It behaves like a compact, stand-alone card between the navigation links and main card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component occupies the bulk of the main reading list card beneath the toolbar. It fills the card’s inner width, minus the card’s side padding (16–24px). The list content begins approximately 12–16px below the toolbar, creating a clear separation. Each child item (reading-list-item-row) is stacked vertically in a single column with 8–16px vertical gaps between rows, forming a clean, scrollable column of mini-cards. The last row has a bit of extra padding (e.g., 16–24px) below it so the card doesn’t feel truncated at the bottom. When there are many items, this area drives page height and becomes scrollable as part of the normal document flow. The background behind the rows is the main card’s surface, visible as a soft border around each mini-card row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column that expands vertically to fit all list items. When other states (loading, empty, error) are active, this component’s area is reused or replaced by those states and does not occupy separate space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each item row is a rounded mini-card inside the larger main card, spanning nearly the full inner width of the main card (minus a small horizontal gap of 4–8px on either side for visual separation). It has 12–16px padding on all sides. The content within follows a vertical micro-layout similar to a blog excerpt: at the top left, the saved article’s title appears in a bold, colorful style, allowed to wrap to two lines if needed. Directly beneath the title, a metadata row contains details like date saved, category (Tarot, Horoscope, etc.), and an optional small icon, arranged in a horizontal line with 8px gaps. On the right side of the row (aligned to the top or vertically centered depending on height), compact action controls—such as a ‘Open’ button and a subtle remove icon or pill—are aligned to the right edge. On narrower screens, the actions move to a second line below the metadata, still right-aligned, preserving vertical flow. There is 8–12px of space between the main row content and any inline error beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky mini-card within the list. Each row is individually clickable or partially clickable (title area) but does not float; it simply stacks in order, creating a familiar vertical list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state replaces the list area inside the main card when the user has no saved items. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the available space of the main card beneath the toolbar. The content is arranged in a narrow vertical column: a small whimsical illustration or icon (e.g., an empty tarot spread or a sleeping moon) sits at the top center, taking about 20–25% of the card’s height; beneath it, a short, playful heading (e.g., ‘Your starshelf is empty’) and a supporting line of explanatory text are center-aligned. A primary or secondary action button (like ‘Discover readings’) is placed 16px below the text. There is generous whitespace around this column, with at least 24–32px of space before the card’s edges, reinforcing the light, airy feeling of an empty state within the handcrafted theme.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the full list area within the main card instead of reading-list-items. It is static and scrolls with the page; if the viewport is tall, it appears roughly centered within the card’s visible area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When loading, this component appears in the same area where list items or the empty state would sit—directly beneath the toolbar inside the main card. The indicator is centered horizontally, with either a circular spinner or a gentle animated constellation motif, sized modestly (about 32–48px). A short ‘Loading your readings…’ message sits beneath it, center-aligned, with 8–12px vertical spacing between spinner and text. Around this pair, there is ample whitespace within the card, so the indicator feels like a focused, temporary state rather than a cramped element. The toolbar above may remain visible and static, creating a clear separation between controls and loading feedback.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the list/empty/error content area inside the main card. It does not change the position of the toolbar or summary above; it scrolls as part of the page if content above pushes it down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main error state occupies the body of the main reading list card beneath the toolbar when loading the list fails. Visually, it behaves like a slightly more urgent version of the empty state. Centered horizontally within the card, an error icon or small illustration (e.g., a cracked crystal ball or clouded stars) appears at the top of this state, followed by a short, clear error headline and a concise explanation line. The text is center-aligned and uses a slightly stronger accent color (red or dark magenta) for the headline while keeping the mood friendly. A primary ‘Retry’ button sits 16px below the text. The component is surrounded by 24–32px of negative space within the card, ensuring it does not feel cramped and remains clearly separated from the toolbar above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the list/empty/loading content area within the main card. It is static and scrolls with the rest of the page, anchored to the same position where list items normally begin.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This inline error appears attached to a specific item row when removal fails. It is positioned directly below that item’s mini-card content, inside the same visual block. A narrow, rounded pill or banner stretches nearly the full inner width of the item row, inset slightly (4–8px) from the row’s left and right padding so it feels nested. It uses a subtle but clear error background tint (soft red or pink) with red text, and has 8px of vertical padding inside. There is 4–8px of vertical space between the bottom of the main row content and this pill, and then another 8–12px before the next item row begins, so the error does not visually merge with adjacent items. The text is concise and left-aligned, possibly accompanied by a small warning icon at the far left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and local to its specific item row. It expands that row’s effective height but does not shift the main card, toolbar, or other rows beyond the normal reflow of the column. It scrolls naturally with the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no visible UI. Conceptually, it sits behind the scenes within the page controller, synchronizing filter, sort, and pagination state with the URL. It occupies no visual space and does not introduce padding, margins, or any card-like structure. The layout is unaffected whether it is present or not; all visual components appear exactly the same.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-sized element. It participates only in logic and routing, not in spatial layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation links are presented as a prominent pill-shaped navigation bar positioned directly under the global watercolor header and above the summary pill. This bar is centered within the content column and spans about 90–100% of its width, with a height around 56–64px including 16px of vertical padding. The bar has a soft white or pale mint background with rounded corners, floating against the lavender page background with 16–24px of vertical margin above and below. Inside, navigation links (e.g., ‘Home’, ‘Daily Reading’, ‘Saved’, ‘Profile’) are arranged horizontally, centered or slightly left-biased, with 16–24px spacing between each label. The current section (‘Saved’ or ‘Reading List’) can appear in a more vivid accent color or pill to help orient the user. On narrow screens, links wrap into two rows or transform into a horizontally scrollable list, but the element retains its identity as a rounded navigation bar stacked as the first pill below the watercolor header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation band that scrolls away with the rest of the page. It is not sticky; it always appears above the summary and the main card as the first major pill-shaped card in the stack.

name: Collections
title: Collections
description: Page for viewing, creating, renaming, and deleting named collections of saved horoscope and astrology articles, and for managing the articles within each collection.

core_features: Retrieve and display a list of all existing collections using getCollectionsList.
Allow users to select a collection from the list and load its details and articles using getCollectionDetail, based on the collectionId URL parameter or user selection.
Provide a form to create a new collection with name and description using createCollection, and then update the list and URL state to reflect the new collection.
Allow users to rename an existing collection using renameCollection, updating both the collection list and the currently viewed collection detail.
Allow users to delete an existing collection using deleteCollection, removing it from the list and clearing or updating the collectionId URL parameter if necessary.
Display articles within a selected collection (including metadata from getCollectionDetail.items.article) and allow navigation to article.html with the appropriate article ID in the URL.
Allow users to remove an article from a collection using removeCollectionItem and immediately reflect this change in the collection detail view.
Support deep links from other flows (such as Save to Collection) by opening a specific collection when a collectionId URL parameter is present.
user_workflows:
Viewing all collections:
1. User navigates to collections.html without specifying a collectionId.
2. The page calls getCollectionsList.
3. The list of collections is displayed in the collections list panel.
4. No collection detail is shown until the user selects one from the list.

Opening a specific collection via URL:
1. User arrives at collections.html with a collectionId query parameter in the URL.
2. The page calls getCollectionsList to populate the list.
3. The page reads the collectionId from the URL and calls getCollectionDetail with that ID.
4. The matching collection is highlighted in the list and its details and items are shown in the collection detail panel.
5. If the collection is renamed or deleted, the page updates the URL and re-renders accordingly.

Creating a new collection:
1. User clicks a 'New collection' action that reveals or focuses the create-collection-form.
2. User enters a collection name and optional description and submits the form.
3. The form calls createCollection with the entered name and description.
4. On success, the page updates the collections list (using the returned Collection), and updates the URL to collections.html?collectionId={newCollection.id}.
5. The page then calls getCollectionDetail for the new collectionId and displays the new collection’s (initially empty) items.

Renaming and deleting collections:
1. From the collections list or collection detail header, the user triggers a rename action for the current collection.
2. An inline rename form is shown, pre-populated with the existing collection name.
3. On submission, renameCollection is called with collectionId and newName.
4. On success, the collections list and the detail header are updated with the new name, without reloading the whole page.
5. If the user chooses to delete a collection, a confirmation dialog is shown.
6. On confirmation, deleteCollection is called with the current collectionId.
7. On success, the collection is removed from the list; if the deleted collection was currently open, the page clears the collectionId from the URL and hides the detail panel. The user can then select another collection or create a new one.

Managing articles within a collection:
1. With a collection selected (via list click or URL), the page displays its items from getCollectionDetail.items.
2. Each item shows article metadata (title, summary, type, sign, week_number, month, year, reading_time_minutes) and actions.
3. When the user clicks an article title or 'Open' action, the browser navigates to article.html?id={article.article_id}.
4. When the user clicks 'Remove from collection' on an item, removeCollectionItem is called with the corresponding collection_item_id.
5. On success, the item is removed from the in-memory items list and the collection detail view is re-rendered without that article.

Handling deep links from 'Save to Collection' flows:
1. Another page (such as article.html or horoscopes.html) completes a 'Save to Collection' flow using addArticleToCollection and then navigates the user to collections.html?collectionId={targetCollectionId}.
2. On load, collections.html reads collectionId and calls getCollectionsList followed by getCollectionDetail for that ID.
3. The collection detail view shows the updated set of items, including any newly added article.
4. The user can now further manage the collection (rename, delete, remove items, or navigate to the saved article).
interactions: Selecting a collection: Clicking a collection entry in the collections list updates the collectionId in the URL (using query parameters) and triggers a call to getCollectionDetail to display its details.

Creating a collection: Submitting the 'New collection' form triggers createCollection; on success the collections list is updated and the URL is updated to include the new collectionId, which then drives loading of collection details.

Renaming a collection: Clicking a 'Rename' action activates an inline rename form. Submitting this form triggers renameCollection; on success, the UI updates the collection name in both the list and the detail header without changing the collectionId URL parameter.

Deleting a collection: Clicking 'Delete' opens a confirmation dialog. Confirming triggers deleteCollection; on success, the collection is removed from the list, and if it was currently selected, the collectionId URL parameter is removed and the detail panel is reset.
Navigating to an article: Clicking an article item within the collection navigates to article.html with a query parameter id set to the clicked article’s article_id value.

Removing an article from a collection: Clicking 'Remove' on a collection item triggers removeCollectionItem with the item’s collection_item_id. On success, the item is removed from the local collection items list; the page may optionally re-fetch getCollectionDetail to ensure consistent state.

Updating URL-driven state: When the collectionId parameter in the URL changes (either by direct navigation or via history API updates), the page re-evaluates which collection is selected and calls getCollectionDetail for that ID if present.

Handling empty states: If getCollectionsList returns no collections, the page will show only the create-collection-form and no collection detail until at least one collection is created. If a specific collectionId is present but getCollectionDetail indicates the collection does not exist or cannot be loaded, the page clears the invalid collectionId from the URL and prompts the user to select an existing collection or create a new one.
page_functionality.state_logic: On page initialization, collections.html reads the query parameters from the URL, specifically checking for collectionId. The page immediately calls getCollectionsList to populate the list of available collections. If a collectionId is present in the query string, the page calls getCollectionDetail with that ID. The currently active collection in the UI is determined solely by the collectionId query parameter. When the user selects a different collection from the list, the page updates the query string to ?collectionId={selectedId} (preserving other existing parameters as needed) using the browser history API, and then calls getCollectionDetail with the selected ID. When a new collection is created, the page sets the URL to ?collectionId={newCollection.id} and loads its detail. When a collection is deleted and it matches the current collectionId, the page removes the collectionId parameter from the URL and hides the detail section (until the user selects another collection). No persistent state is stored in localStorage or similar; all persistent data is retrieved via the assigned interfaces, with the URL serving as the single source of truth for which collection is currently in focus.

components.functionality: Initializes the Collections page, reads URL parameters, orchestrates data loading with getCollectionsList and getCollectionDetail, and coordinates interactions between the list panel, detail panel, and creation/rename/delete actions.

Displays all collections returned by getCollectionsList, highlights the currently selected collection based on collectionId from the URL, and exposes actions to select, rename, and delete a collection.

Shows metadata for the currently selected collection (name, description, timestamps) and passes the list of items to the collection-items-list component. It reloads data whenever collectionId changes in the URL or when an item is added/removed.

Renders the items array from getCollectionDetail for the active collection, showing each article’s metadata and providing navigation to article.html as well as the ability to remove items using removeCollectionItem.
Provides inputs for a new collection’s name and optional description, calls createCollection on submit, and notifies the page root to update the list and URL state to focus on the new collection.
Allows inline editing of the selected collection’s name. On submission, it calls renameCollection and then updates both the list and the detail header with the returned collection data.

Asks the user to confirm deletion of the active collection and, on confirmation, calls deleteCollection. On success, it triggers removal of the collection from the list and clears the collectionId URL parameter if it matches the deleted collection.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single centered content column on a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog layout. Assume the watercolor header, main navigation pill, and breadcrumb pill already sit above this page’s content. Within the Collections page, stack full-width rounded cards vertically: first the collections-list-panel as an overview card, then the collection-detail-panel as the main 'article-like' card. Inside the detail panel, stack a title/controls row on top of the collection-items-list content. Auxiliary forms (create and rename) are inline within these cards, while destructive actions use a modal dialog.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single centered content column on a full-width lavender background, mirroring the blog layout. Assume the watercolor header, main navigation pill, and breadcrumb pill already sit above this page’s content. Within the Collections page, stack full-width rounded cards vertically: first the collections-list-panel as an overview card, then the collection-detail-panel as the main 'article-like' card. Inside the detail panel, stack a title/controls row on top of the collection-items-list content. Auxiliary forms (create and rename) are inline within these cards, while destructive actions use a modal dialog.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Respect the mockup’s proportions: the existing watercolor header and nav/breadcrumb consume the upper portion of the viewport. Below that, the collections-page-root occupies the remainder. Within it, the collections-list-panel card near the top takes roughly 25–35% of the available vertical space (depending on the number of collections), while the collection-detail-panel beneath it is the largest block, taking roughly 45–60% of the vertical space when populated with items. Cards span the full width of the content column (about 80–90% of page width on desktop, bounded by a fixed max-width). Vertical gaps of 16–24px separate major cards, maintaining visible bands of lavender background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: each card (list and detail) has 16–24px of inner padding on all sides, with card headings given a bit more top/bottom breathing room. Lists of collections and items are vertically stacked with standard line-height and 8–16px between rows. Forms are compact but not cramped: labels and fields have 8–12px vertical separations. Nothing touches card edges; text and controls sit comfortably inside the rounded containers.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a strong top-to-bottom flow consistent with the mockup: from the colorful site header and global navigation down into the Collections section. First, the user sees the collections-list-panel card at the top of the page content as an entry point. From there, attention naturally moves downward to the larger, more prominent collection-detail-panel card where the main reading and management occur, then continues down through the collection-items-list. Inline forms (create, rename) are positioned at the ends of these flows (bottom of the collections list, or inline with the detail title) so they feel like natural extensions rather than interruptions. If triggered, the delete-collection-dialog temporarily dominates the flow by appearing centered over everything with a dimmed backdrop.

layout.overall_description: The Collections page sits within the same playful, astrology-themed frame as the rest of the site: a full-width lavender backdrop, topped by a watercolor illustration header, then a chunky navigation pill, and a breadcrumb pill, all centered within a fixed-width column. Directly beneath this global structure, the Collections content appears as two main rounded cards stacked in a single, centered column. The first card, closer to the top, is a softer, mid-height overview listing all the user’s collections, with their names arranged in a clean vertical list and an inline 'create new collection' form anchored at its bottom edge. A generous strip of lavender space separates this card from the second, larger card below: the collection-detail-panel. This lower card visually echoes a blog article: the selected collection’s name is presented as a bold, colored title line at the top, with inline rename controls when needed, and a subtle metadata/action row. Beneath that, the collection-items-list occupies the bulk of the card, listing saved horoscope and astrology articles in a comfortable vertical flow. All cards have ample internal padding and rounded corners, letting the lavender background show through between them. When the user chooses to delete a collection, a smaller, centered rounded dialog floats above the dimmed page, maintaining the same handcrafted aesthetic while clearly taking focus. Overall, the layout feels like stacked, friendly content panels in a single fluid column, maintaining the mockup’s vertical storytelling rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collections-page-root occupies the main content area directly beneath the global breadcrumb pill, within the same fixed-width centered column as the rest of the site. It spans the full width of this content column (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at a max-width around 960–1040px), while the lavender site background remains visible on both sides. There is a 24px vertical gap between the breadcrumb pill above and the top edge of this root area, aligning with the mockup’s section spacing. Inside, it arranges its child components in a purely vertical flow: the collections-list-panel card first, then a 16–24px gap, then the collection-detail-panel card. On tall displays, the combined height of these cards plus gaps typically fills the remaining 60–70% of the viewport, but the page is free to scroll vertically as content grows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level container in the document flow, centered horizontally with auto side margins and a fixed max-width. It stretches vertically according to its children’s combined height and scrolls with the page; no fixed or sticky behavior is applied.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collections-list-panel is the first major card inside the collections-page-root, positioned at the top center of the content column. It appears as a wide, horizontally centered rounded rectangle spanning nearly the full width of the column, with 16–24px of padding on all sides. The top of this card is aligned roughly 24px below the breadcrumb pill, maintaining a clear band of lavender background above it. Inside the card, a heading row (e.g., 'Your Collections') appears at the top left with extra top/bottom padding, mirroring the visual weight of a section title. Below this heading, the collections themselves are listed in a single vertical column, each collection name occupying a full-width row with 8–16px vertical spacing between items. The list can extend downward as needed, but for a typical number of collections, the card’s total height occupies about 25–35% of the visible viewport. At the bottom of the card, separated from the list by approximately 16px of vertical space or a subtle divider line, the create-collection-form is anchored, spanning most of the card’s inner width. This bottom-anchored form makes the card feel like a self-contained panel for both viewing and adding collections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in the standard vertical flow of the page. Its height grows with the number of collections and the create form’s state, pushing the collection-detail-panel downward. The card scrolls away with the page; it does not stick to the top or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection-detail-panel sits directly below the collections-list-panel within the same centered column, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap of exposed lavender background to clearly differentiate the two cards. It is visually the largest and most prominent card, spanning the full width of the content column with rounded corners and 24px inner padding to echo an 'article' container. The card’s top edge is aligned under the collections-list-panel, forming a clean vertical stack. Inside, the topmost area is a header row: the collection title is left-aligned and rendered in a strong purple or accent color, occupying roughly the left 60–70% of the card’s width. When rename-collection-inline-form is active, it appears exactly in this title position, replacing the static text. On the right side of the header row, compact actions such as 'Delete' or secondary metadata align horizontally, but they are kept light so the title remains dominant. This header row has slightly more top and bottom padding, mimicking the spacing of a blog article title and meta. Below the header row, separated by around 16px of vertical space, the collection-items-list begins and extends downward to fill the remainder of the card. As a result, this detail card typically occupies roughly 45–60% of the viewport height, growing as needed if many items are present, and encourages vertical reading similar to a long article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal page flow, positioned beneath the collections-list-panel and above any content that may follow. Its height is flexible, driven by the number of items in collection-items-list. The page scrolls as the panel grows; no internal scrolling container is enforced unless required by implementation constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The collection-items-list occupies the main body of the collection-detail-panel, directly beneath the header row that contains the collection title and actions. Starting about 16px below that header, it spans the full inner width of the card minus the side padding, forming a vertical stack of saved horoscope and astrology articles. Each item appears as a row or mini-card with its own subtle rounding and 12–16px vertical spacing between items, preserving the moderate density. The list continues downward to fill most of the detail panel’s height, with the last item sitting about 16–24px above the card’s bottom edge to maintain padding consistency. On a typical screen, the list will feel like the dominant content area, taking up 70–80% of the vertical space inside the collection-detail-panel, mirroring a long article’s body text in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the collection-detail-panel, expanding the card’s height as more items are added. It does not scroll independently by default; instead, the entire page scrolls. If implementation requires a capped height, it can be turned into an inner scrollable area, but visually it should still read as a continuous vertical list within the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create-collection-form is placed inside the collections-list-panel card, anchored at its bottom as a natural extension of the collections overview. It spans most of the card’s inner width, left-aligned with the list of collections, and sits approximately 16px below the last collection entry or divider. A short section label or helper text may appear above the input with 8px spacing, then the main input field and action button are laid out horizontally (field on the left occupying 60–70% of the width, button on the right) or stacked vertically on smaller screens. The form maintains 16–24px padding from the bottom edge of the card so it doesn’t feel cramped. Visually, it feels like a gentle call-to-action at the base of the list, rather than a separate floating element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides inline within the collections-list-panel, expanding that card’s height as needed. When collapsed (if such a state exists), its absence reduces the card’s height; when expanded, it pushes the collection-detail-panel downward. It scrolls with the rest of the content and has no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rename-collection-inline-form appears inline within the header row of the collection-detail-panel, occupying the same position and approximate width as the static collection title it replaces. It is left-aligned at the top of the detail card, about 24px from the card’s top edge, and spans roughly 60–70% of the inner card width, leaving room on the right for any existing action buttons. The input field follows the card’s rounded aesthetic with soft edges, and its height matches or slightly exceeds the original title text height, ensuring the header row’s vertical rhythm remains balanced. Any confirmation/cancel icons or buttons are placed directly to the right of the field, maintaining a single horizontal line. When active, the rest of the header and the collection-items-list remain in place beneath it, preserving the vertical structure of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an inline replacement within the collection-detail-panel’s header row. It does not introduce new vertical sections; instead, it temporarily occupies the title’s space. Activating or dismissing the form only affects the header row’s content and minor height, without altering the positions of the cards above or below. It scrolls away naturally with the card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete-collection-dialog appears as a centered modal overlay that floats above the entire page when triggered, visually sitting on top of the collections-page-root and all other content. The background behind it (including the lavender page, header illustration, nav pills, and cards) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark curtain, focusing attention on the dialog. The dialog itself is a compact rounded card, approximately 320–400px wide on desktop (about 40–50% of the content column width), and vertically sized just enough to contain its message and controls, typically occupying about 20–30% of the viewport height. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport. Inside, 24px padding on all sides gives the text room to breathe. The warning title and explanatory copy are stacked at the top with 8–16px spacing, and the action buttons (e.g., 'Cancel' and 'Delete') are aligned to the right at the bottom in a horizontal row, separated by 8–12px.
