Conceptually, it sits behind the main Planner card, orchestrating which child components are visible (calendar vs. agenda view, whether the detail panel is open, and whether modals are shown). It introduces no additional spacing or container; instead, it controls the visibility and state of components that are already laid out within the centered, stacked column of content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering controller with no layout footprint; resides at the page or Planner-card level in the component tree and only influences the spatial behavior of visible components (show/hide, switching views) without adding its own DOM container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Inside the main Planner card, the planner-view-toggle appears near the top, directly below the Planner page title. It is aligned to the left within a horizontal control row, appearing as a rounded, pill-shaped segmented control labeled "Calendar" and "Agenda". The control row itself spans nearly the full inner width of the card (about 90–100% of the card’s content width), with 16–24px of white or very pale background above and below it, and 16–24px of card padding to the left and right. The toggle occupies roughly 30–40% of this row’s width, leaving enough room on the right side for the date navigation controls. On wider screens, the toggle and date navigation share a single horizontal line; on narrow screens, the toggle sits on the first line and the date navigation drops below it, left-aligned, with an 8–16px vertical gap between them, preserving the neat stacked look from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the document as part of the Planner card. It remains in a fixed position relative to the top of the card (not sticky), but it always appears above the calendar/agenda content. It responds to viewport width by allowing the surrounding layout to wrap, but the toggle itself remains compact and left-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-date-navigation sits in the same horizontal control row as the view toggle, aligned to the right edge of the Planner card’s content area. On desktop-sized widths, it appears on the same baseline as the view toggle, creating a balanced left-right composition: the view toggle on the left, and on the right a small cluster of date navigation elements (back/forward arrow buttons framing a month or week label). This block occupies about 30–40% of the control row width and is right-aligned with 16–24px of inner card padding from the right edge. The elements within this block are spaced in 8px increments, with the date label centered between arrows and enough horizontal breathing room so the row never feels cramped. On small screens, if horizontal space is limited, the date navigation wraps below the view toggle, becomes left-aligned, and spans roughly 60–80% of the card width, with an 8–16px vertical gap separating it from the toggle. There is always at least 16px of vertical space between this control row and the content below (calendar/agenda view).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the Planner card’s control row, with responsive behavior: right-aligned on wider screens; stacks below and left-aligns on narrow screens. It scrolls with the rest of the card content and is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-calendar-view occupies the main central area of the Planner card when the Calendar option is selected in the view toggle. It is positioned directly beneath the control row (view toggle + date navigation), separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. The calendar view spans nearly the full width of the card’s content area (about 90–100% of the inner width, respecting the card’s side padding), presenting a grid of days with soft borders and rounded hover/focus states that echo the pill shapes seen in the rest of the design. On a typical viewport, this grid fills roughly 50–60% of the Planner card’s visible height before scrolling, leaving room beneath it for the ritual-entry-detail-panel when present. Each calendar cell has comfortable internal padding (8–16px) and uses pastel fills that keep the grid airy rather than dense. The calendar as a whole feels like a framed, rounded section, but it remains visually subordinate to the overall Planner card frame that encapsulates it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies a single, flexible block within the Planner card. Only one primary view (calendar or agenda) is visible at a time; when the calendar view is active, it fully replaces the agenda view in this area. It grows vertically based on the number of week rows but remains within the card’s scrollable content—no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-agenda-view shares the exact same spatial slot as the calendar view, appearing in its place when the user selects the Agenda option from the view toggle. It sits directly beneath the control row, with the same 16–24px vertical gap above and the same side padding as the calendar view. When visible, it spans about 90–100% of the card’s content width and uses a vertical list of agenda items grouped by date. Each date section may appear as a small subheader with a slightly tinted background and rounded corners, followed by individual ritual entries stacked with 8–16px of vertical spacing between them. The agenda view’s total height is determined by the number of items; if the list is long, the Planner card simply scrolls. The agenda layout maintains a moderate density: entries are clearly separated, but the list remains efficient and readable for planning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the calendar view within the same block area. It appears or disappears based on planner-state-manager and the planner-view-toggle. It scrolls within the Planner card and has no fixed/sticky behavior. On all screen sizes it remains a single-column vertical list aligned to the page’s central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-focus-date-highlighter does not introduce a new container; instead, it overlays visual emphasis inside the existing calendar or agenda views. In the calendar view, the focused date’s cell receives a more pronounced rounded outline or pill-shaped mint background that stands out from the other pastel cells, perhaps accompanied by a small star or moon icon tucked into a corner of the cell. In the agenda view, the focused date’s group header may get a subtle colored band behind it or a rounded badge around the date text. Optionally, a thin, full-width strip or micro-banner just above the main view area (directly under the control row) can show a short line like "Focused on: March 3 – New Moon Rituals" in a small, pale card with 8–12px padding and rounded corners, spanning about 80–90% of the main card width and centered. This strip, if used, maintains the vertical rhythm: 8–16px below the control row and 8–16px above the main calendar/agenda content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a visual overlay and styling layer within existing components rather than a standalone block. It does not change the structural layout but affects the appearance of individual cells or list items, and optionally adds a slim inline banner that flows with the document (scrolls normally, no stickiness).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual-entry-detail-panel appears only when a specific ritual entry is selected, and it is positioned within the main Planner card, directly beneath the active view (calendar or agenda). It is separated from the view above by approximately 16–24px of vertical space, preserving the clear stacking seen in the mockup. Visually, it is a nested rounded card inside the larger Planner card: slightly tinted (for example, a pale mint or soft blush) with a subtle border, rounded corners, and 16–24px of internal padding. The panel spans roughly 90–100% of the Planner card’s content width and aligns with the main content column. Inside, the content flows vertically: at the top, the ritual title and date/time sit in a compact header row, possibly with a small linked article reference; below that, a short description or key details; and at the bottom, a horizontal row of action buttons (Edit, Reschedule, Delete) right-aligned or center-aligned, with 8–16px spacing between buttons. The entire detail panel grows in height to fit its content, but retains comfortable margins above and below so it feels like a distinct object within the card rather than just extended text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A conditional block-level element that appears within the Planner card’s document flow. It pushes content below it downward when visible and leaves an empty space when hidden; it scrolls with the page. It does not overlay or float on top of the calendar/agenda; instead, it extends the vertical flow of the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual-entry-form-modal appears as a central, rounded dialog card floating above the entire page when the user creates or edits a ritual. The background behind it (including the watercolor header, navigation pills, and Planner card) is dimmed with a semi-transparent lavender overlay, but remains visible to maintain context. The modal itself is centered both horizontally and vertically in the viewport. On desktop, it is approximately 400–480px wide (or around 40–60% of the viewport width, whichever is smaller) and has 24px of padding on all sides, with generously rounded corners and a slight drop shadow to emphasize its elevation. On smaller screens, it uses up to 90% of the viewport width and can extend vertically, but always maintains at least 16px margin from the top and bottom edges of the viewport, becoming scrollable internally if the form is long. Inside the modal, content is arranged in a simple vertical flow: a title at the top, then form fields (stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing), and finally a footer row with primary and secondary buttons aligned to the right or centered. The modal’s style borrows from the site’s playful palette, possibly using a subtle pastel header bar or accent-colored title text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, sitting above all other content layers. It prevents interaction with underlying components while open. The background use of a semi-transparent overlay gives it the typical modal behavior: the page behind does not scroll separately; scrolling (if needed) occurs inside the modal content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ritual-entry-delete-dialog is a smaller confirmation dialog that appears above the dimmed page, typically on top of or in place of the form modal when a delete action is triggered. It is centered in the viewport, like the form modal, but more compact: around 320–380px wide on desktop (or up to 80–90% width on small screens) with 16–24px internal padding and pronounced rounded corners. The dialog contains a short, focused message and two or three buttons (e.g., Delete, Cancel) stacked or placed in a horizontal row at the bottom. The message text and primary action can use a warm but clear warning color (soft red accents) while retaining the rounded, friendly feel. The dialog floats above all other content, including the form modal if both are present, creating a distinct layered effect yet still aligning visually with the central, stacked-card language of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, stacked above the modal layer when present. It uses the same dimmed background behavior as the form modal and does not scroll independently unless its content exceeds available height (which is unlikely for such a short dialog). Interaction with underlying components is blocked while this dialog is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The planner-error-and-loading-handler manifests in two primary ways within the existing layout. For loading states that affect the entire Planner view (e.g., initial load or date range change), a semi-transparent veil may cover just the interior of the Planner card, with a small circular spinner or whimsical astrology-themed loader centered within the card. This loader sits roughly at the vertical midpoint of the card’s visible area and does not alter the card’s layout—it overlays atop the calendar/agenda content while they are temporarily disabled. For error states, a rounded alert banner appears inside the Planner card near the top of its content area, typically positioned directly below the card’s title and above the control row, separated by 8–16px of vertical space. This banner spans about 90–100% of the card’s content width, has 12–16px of padding, and uses a soft warning palette (pale red or peach with darker text) while preserving the rounded, pill-like aesthetic. Multiple errors, if possible, are either stacked with 8px gaps or consolidated into one banner to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Uses overlay behavior for loading (a centered, semi-transparent layer within the Planner card) and inline block behavior for errors (banners that push content down). Both behaviors are constrained to the Planner card area rather than the entire viewport. They scroll with the Planner card’s content when positioned inline; the loading veil stays anchored to the card section it covers for the duration of the loading state.

name: Content Preferences
title: Content Preferences
description: Configure and save personalization settings for your content feed, including zodiac sign, interests, tarot/astrology balance, and preferred article length. Saved preferences influence the homepage feed and horoscope navigation.

core_features: Load existing content personalization settings by calling getContentPreferences on page initialization and bind the result to the preferences form.
Allow the user to select a primary zodiac sign through a controlled selector bound to ContentPreferences.primary_zodiac_sign.

Allow the user to select multiple interest topics through a controlled multi-select bound to ContentPreferences.interests (interest options are populated dynamically and must support the required topical categories defined by the product).

Provide a content type balance control bound to ContentPreferences.tarot_content_percentage and ContentPreferences.astrology_content_percentage, ensuring that both values remain consistent (e.g., total 100%).
Allow the user to choose a preferred article length via a control bound to ContentPreferences.article_length_preference, using the available enum values.
Persist changes by calling updateContentPreferences when the user submits the form, then redirect to index.html with URL parameters indicating that preferences were updated.

Provide a 'View Today’s Horoscope' navigation control that, when a primary zodiac sign is present, navigates to horoscopes.html with type=daily and zodiacSign set to the current primary_zodiac_sign value.

Handle loading, saving, and validation states (e.g., disabled actions during API calls, error messages on failure) without using local storage, relying instead on in-memory state and URL parameters for navigation context.
user_workflows: Initial load and form prefill: 1) User navigates to preferences.html (optionally with URL parameters like returnTo=index.html); 2) preferences-loader component parses URL parameters for navigation context; 3) preferences-loader calls getContentPreferences; 4) On success, preferences-form initializes its local form state from the returned ContentPreferences object (zodiac sign, interests, balance, article length); 5) Form controls display current values and mark the page as having no unsaved changes; 6) On error, the page shows a non-blocking error banner and presents empty or neutral controls for the user to fill in manually.

Editing and saving preferences: 1) User changes one or more fields in the preferences-form (zodiac, interests, balance, article length); 2) Each change updates local form state and sets a dirty flag in navigation-guard; 3) User clicks the save-preferences-button; 4) preferences-form validates the form (e.g., checks for required fields if any validation rules are defined) and, if valid, calls updateContentPreferences with the current in-memory values; 5) While the request is in flight, save-preferences-button shows a loading state and is disabled; 6) On success, the response ContentPreferences is used to refresh local form state (including last_updated_at if present), the dirty flag is reset, and the user is redirected to index.html with a query parameter indicating that preferences were updated; 7) On failure, an error message is shown and the dirty flag remains true so the user can retry.

Returning to an updated homepage: 1) After a successful update, the page navigates to index.html, appending URL parameters such as preferencesUpdated=true and optionally a preferencesVersion based on the returned ContentPreferences.last_updated_at or id; 2) index.html can read these parameters to trigger a refresh of its personalized feed; 3) The user sees content that reflects the saved preferences.

Viewing today’s horoscope from the preferences page: 1) User ensures a primary zodiac sign is selected in the preferences-form (either loaded from getContentPreferences or manually chosen); 2) User clicks the view-horoscope-cta; 3) If there are unsaved changes, navigation-guard optionally prompts the user to save before leaving; 4) If the user chooses to proceed without saving, or there are no unsaved changes, the page navigates to horoscopes.html with URL parameters type=daily and zodiacSign set to the current in-memory primary zodiac sign; 5) horoscopes.html uses these parameters to show the corresponding daily horoscope.

Handling unsaved changes when navigating away: 1) User modifies one or more fields in preferences-form, setting the in-memory dirty flag to true; 2) User tries to navigate away (e.g., via browser back, header link, footer link, or view-horoscope-cta); 3) navigation-guard intercepts the action, detects unsaved changes, and shows a confirmation dialog; 4) If the user confirms leaving, navigation proceeds without calling updateContentPreferences; 5) If the user cancels, navigation is prevented and the user remains on preferences.html to save or further edit the form.
interactions: On page initialization, preferences-loader invokes getContentPreferences with no parameters, then emits an onLoadSuccess event with the returned ContentPreferences or an onLoadError event if the call fails.

When getContentPreferences returns successfully, preferences-form receives the ContentPreferences object and uses it to set the initial values of zodiac-selector, interests-selector, content-balance-control, and article-length-selector; this also resets the dirty flag in navigation-guard.

When the user selects or changes the primary zodiac sign in zodiac-selector, the component updates the local primaryZodiacSign field in preferences-form’s state and marks the form as dirty; the view-horoscope-cta component listens to this state and enables or disables itself accordingly.
When the user selects or deselects interest tags in interests-selector, the component updates the interests array in preferences-form’s state and marks the form as dirty.

When the user adjusts the content-balance-control (e.g., a slider for tarot vs. astrology), the component updates both tarotContentPercentage and astrologyContentPercentage in the form state, applying any business rules such as keeping the sum consistent; navigation-guard marks the form as dirty.
When the user changes the article-length-selector, the component updates articleLengthPreference in preferences-form’s state and marks the form as dirty.

When the user clicks save-preferences-button, preferences-form validates the current state and, if valid, calls updateContentPreferences with arguments derived from its state (primaryZodiacSign, interests, tarotContentPercentage, astrologyContentPercentage, articleLengthPreference); on success it triggers a redirect to index.html with appropriate URL parameters; on error it shows an error in feedback-banner and leaves the form editable.

When the user clicks view-horoscope-cta and a primary zodiac sign is present, the component constructs a URL for horoscopes.html including type=daily and zodiacSign set from the current in-memory primaryZodiacSign; if there are unsaved changes, navigation-guard may intercept to ask the user whether to save first or proceed without saving.

When the page redirects to index.html after a successful update, it appends URL parameters that signal the update (e.g., preferencesUpdated=true and a version token); index.html can use these parameters to re-fetch and re-filter its content feed according to the new preferences.

If any API call (getContentPreferences or updateContentPreferences) fails, feedback-banner displays a contextual error; the user can dismiss this banner (onDismiss) and either retry the operation or leave the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is controlled by a combination of in-memory form state and URL parameters, without using localStorage. On load, preferences.html reads URL parameters such as returnTo (optional target page after saving, defaulting to index.html), focus (an optional field identifier to focus/highlight on load), and any status parameters passed from other pages (for example, to indicate that the user is being prompted to configure preferences). After parsing URL parameters, the page calls getContentPreferences and initializes the in-memory state with the returned ContentPreferences object. During editing, the form tracks a dirty flag that is not stored in the URL. When the user saves preferences successfully, updateContentPreferences returns an updated ContentPreferences instance; the page then redirects to index.html (or to the URL provided by returnTo) and appends URL query parameters such as preferencesUpdated=true and an implementation-defined token derived from fields like ContentPreferences.id or ContentPreferences.last_updated_at (e.g., preferencesVersion). These parameters allow index.html to know that preferences have changed and to refresh the personalized feed accordingly. When navigating to horoscopes.html via view-horoscope-cta, the page constructs a URL of the form horoscopes.html?type=daily&zodiacSign=<currentPrimaryZodiacSign>, relying solely on the in-memory primary zodiac sign from the form or loaded preferences. No preferences are persisted in URL parameters beyond what is required for navigation targets, and the ContentPreferences entity itself remains server-managed and accessed exclusively through getContentPreferences and updateContentPreferences.

components.functionality: Initializes the Content Preferences page by parsing URL parameters for navigation context, invoking getContentPreferences to fetch the current personalization settings, and passing the result to child components such as preferences-form.

Central controller for all preference fields, maintaining in-memory state for primary zodiac sign, interests, tarot/astrology balance, and article length, and orchestrating validation and saving via updateContentPreferences.

Provides a controlled selection input for the primary zodiac sign, with options mapped from the ContentPreferences.primary_zodiac_sign enum; updates the form’s primaryZodiacSign field and influences the availability and target of the 'View Today’s Horoscope' CTA.

Provides a multi-select interface for choosing content interests, binding to ContentPreferences.interests. Options are populated dynamically (not hardcoded) and must cover the configured key topical categories used by the personalization logic.

Manages the relative balance between tarot and astrology content by controlling ContentPreferences.tarot_content_percentage and ContentPreferences.astrology_content_percentage. When the user adjusts the slider, it updates both values in a consistent way (for example, keeping their sum constrained according to business rules).

Allows the user to choose their preferred article length based on the ContentPreferences.article_length_preference enum (such as short, medium, or long), storing the selected value in the form state for submission.

Triggers form submission to persist preferences. When clicked, it causes preferences-form to validate current values and call updateContentPreferences; on success, it initiates navigation to index.html (or an alternative URL provided via URL parameters) with query parameters indicating that preferences were updated.

Provides a call-to-action to view today’s horoscope for the currently selected primary zodiac sign. It is enabled only when a primary zodiac sign is present in the in-memory state and navigates to horoscopes.html using URL parameters type=daily and zodiacSign set from that value.

Tracks whether the preferences-form has unsaved changes and intercepts navigation actions (including clicks on view-horoscope-cta or external links) to optionally present a confirmation dialog before leaving the page without calling updateContentPreferences.

Displays transient success or error messages associated with getContentPreferences and updateContentPreferences operations, such as load failures or save errors, without storing any static content data.

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same stacked, single-column structure as the mockup: a full-width illustrated header at the top, followed by a navigation pill bar, then a breadcrumb pill, then a single large "preferences" card in the center column. All interactive components on this page (loader, form, fields, feedback banner, CTAs) live inside or immediately around that main card, arranged in a strict top-to-bottom reading order within the fixed-width content column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same stacked, single-column structure as the mockup: a full-width illustrated header at the top, followed by a navigation pill bar, then a breadcrumb pill, then a single large "preferences" card in the center column. All interactive components on this page (loader, form, fields, feedback banner, CTAs) live inside or immediately around that main card, arranged in a strict top-to-bottom reading order within the fixed-width content column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The watercolor header occupies roughly the top quarter of the viewport, the navigation and breadcrumb pills each take a slim but distinct horizontal band beneath it, and the main preferences card occupies most of the remaining vertical space in the initial viewport. Inside the card, the form fields stack vertically and each field spans roughly 60–80% of the card width. The feedback banner, when present, forms its own pill-shaped band between the breadcrumb bar and the main card, with generous lavender background space above and below to maintain separation.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate density: large elements (header, pills, card) are separated vertically by 16–24px of lavender background. Inside the preferences card, padding of about 24px on all sides keeps text away from edges. Form fields have 16–24px vertical spacing between groups, labels are not cramped above controls, and the action row (buttons/links) has comfortable breathing room above it so the bottom of the card never feels crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is drawn first to the colorful header and site identity, then down to the navigation pill and breadcrumb. From there, attention moves to the main preferences card with its prominent title. Within the card, the user reads and interacts in a strict vertical flow: intro text, then the zodiac selector, then interests, then the tarot/astrology balance slider, then article length options, and finally the action row with the primary save button on the left and the secondary horoscope link on the right. The loader, feedback banner, and navigation guard appear as overlays or inline pills that sit on this path without breaking the vertical rhythm.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a soft lavender background with a single, centered content column capped by a playful watercolor illustration at the top. Beneath this illustrated header, a rounded navigation pill spans most of the column width, followed by a slightly smaller breadcrumb pill, each separated by generous lavender gaps so they read as distinct bands. Below them, a pill-shaped feedback banner appears when needed, sitting like a colorful ribbon just above the main content. The core of the page is a large, rounded white preferences card centered in the column, occupying roughly the lower half to two-thirds of the initial viewport. Inside this card, a bold, slightly whimsical heading announces “Content Preferences,” followed by a short explanatory line. Under that, the form content flows down in a single column: zodiac selection at the top, then a roomy multi-select for interests, then a wide horizontal slider that balances tarot vs. astrology, then a radio group for article length. These controls are comfortably spaced, with ample padding between each group. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal action row anchors the experience: the primary save button sits at the lower left, while a lighter-weight “View Horoscope” link is aligned to the lower right, creating a visual endpoint. When data loads or saves, a soft overlay with a loader sits centered over the card contents without disturbing the overall stack. The whole layout feels like a friendly, handcrafted settings page that fits seamlessly into a fantasy/astrology blog, while remaining highly scannable and linear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences loader appears only when data is being fetched or saved and is visually confined to the main preferences card area. It is positioned absolutely within the card, stretching from edge to edge of the card’s inner content region (just inside the rounded corners) and covering the entire form: from just below the card title and intro text down to just above the bottom padding. The overlay slightly dims the underlying controls with a translucent lavender veil, and a circular spinner or whimsical astrological icon is centered both horizontally and vertically within this overlay. Margins around the card (to the lavender page background) remain fully visible so the user still perceives the card as a single object, but all interactive form elements beneath the loader appear clearly disabled while the overlay is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the preferences card, stacking above the form fields and CTAs. It does not shift surrounding layout; instead, it sits on top of existing content, matching the card’s width and inner height. When inactive, it has no spatial presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferences form occupies the main body of the large, rounded card centered in the column. Inside the card, the form starts just below the card’s heading and introductory text, leaving about 16–24px of space between the intro and the first field. The form is arranged as a single vertical column of controls that spans roughly 80% of the card’s width, centered horizontally so there are equal left and right paddings of about 24px between the form content and the card edges. Fields are stacked with 16–24px of vertical spacing between groups, following a top-to-bottom order: zodiac selector at the top, then interests selector, then content balance slider, then article length selector, and finally the action row with the save button and view-horoscope link. The form does not extend to the card edges; instead, it respects the card’s internal padding so no labels or controls touch the rounded boundaries. On taller screens, the form may occupy about half the card’s height; on shorter screens, the card and form scroll within the main column while maintaining their centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document layout inside the preferences card, expanding vertically as needed. It is constrained by the card’s max width and padding and scrolls with the page; it is overshadowed but not displaced when the loader overlay is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The zodiac selector is the first interactive field in the preferences form, placed near the top of the card’s content area. It sits directly under the form’s introductory copy with about 16px of space in between. The label is left-aligned and spans the same content width as the field below it. The control itself (dropdown or similar) occupies roughly 60–70% of the card’s inner width, aligned to the left edge of the form column, with comfortable right-side whitespace to avoid feeling cramped. The control’s height is in the medium range (about 40–48px), with rounded corners matching the site’s pill aesthetic. A small hint text or placeholder sits inside the field, centered vertically, while the field maintains at least 16px of vertical spacing above the next component (interests selector).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the natural vertical form flow, expanding slightly in height if helper text is present. It remains left-aligned within the form column and does not affect elements outside the card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The interests selector appears immediately below the zodiac selector, separated by about 16–24px of vertical space. It is visually larger because it anticipates multiple selections and possibly chips or tags. The label is left-aligned and uses the same width as the zodiac label, followed by the selection area which spans about 80% of the card’s inner width. If chips/tags are used, they wrap onto multiple lines within this width, creating a soft block of rounded pills that still maintains around 16px of margin on all sides within the card. The top of the interests field aligns with the left edge of the zodiac selector, maintaining a clean vertical edge down the form. Additional helper text, if present, sits directly beneath the label in a smaller, muted color and does not exceed the form column width. A minimum of 16px of space separates the bottom of this component from the content balance slider below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a block-level element within the form. Its height is flexible, expanding vertically as chips or options are added, but it does not exceed the card’s width or overlap neighboring elements. On narrower screens, tags wrap gracefully within the same column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content balance control (slider) is positioned below the interests selector, with about 24px of vertical space to emphasize it as a distinct, interactive grouping. Its label sits above the slider, left-aligned to the same vertical axis as previous labels. The slider track itself is centered horizontally within the form column and spans approximately 70–80% of the card’s inner width, leaving consistent side padding. End labels like “More Tarot” and “More Astrology” appear at the far left and right ends of the slider track, slightly above or below the track, echoing the playful typography with bright accent colors that align with the theme. The thumb is a small, rounded knob that visually stands out. There is about 16px of space below the slider area before the article length selector begins, preserving the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard horizontal control inside the form column, not fixed or sticky. It expands slightly vertically if helper text or end labels wrap, but remains confined to the card’s width and does not affect alignment of adjacent fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article length selector sits beneath the content balance slider, separated by about 16–24px of space. It consists of a label followed by a small group of radio options (for example, short, medium, long), arranged vertically in a single column to maintain the page’s linear flow. The entire group aligns with the left edge of the other fields and occupies about 60–70% of the card’s inner width, leaving comfortable whitespace to the right. Each radio option has slightly rounded selection indicators and enough vertical spacing (8–12px) between options to feel airy rather than cramped. The bottom of this group sits roughly 24px above the action row (save button and view-horoscope link), giving a visual pause before the CTAs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal vertical order. It does not influence elements outside the form and adapts modestly in height if more options are added, preserving consistent left alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save preferences button is the primary call-to-action and is positioned at the bottom-left of the preferences card, just below the last form field (article length selector). There is approximately 24px of space between the bottom of the radio group and the top of the button. The button is pill-shaped with generous horizontal padding, and its width auto-sizes to its label, ending roughly one-third of the way across the card, leaving ample space to its right. Vertically, it sits aligned with the baseline of the view-horoscope link, forming a cohesive action row. Extra bottom padding of about 24px is preserved beneath the button to maintain the card’s airy feel and ensure the button does not appear pressed against the card’s rounded bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the form’s normal flow but visually anchors the card’s bottom. It remains left-aligned within the form column and may show different visual states (disabled while loading, pressed, etc.) without changing its size or position. It scrolls with the card content and is not sticky to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-horoscope CTA is a secondary navigation link positioned on the same horizontal row as the save button, aligned towards the bottom-right of the preferences card. Within the card’s inner width, it sits flush to the right padding, directly opposite the save button, creating a balanced action bar. The link’s text is smaller and lighter-weight than the button, potentially paired with a small right-pointing arrow icon to suggest navigation. Vertically, it is aligned to the center or baseline of the save button so that both actions feel part of a single horizontal band. There is equal bottom padding beneath this link and the save button, reinforcing the rounded, pill-like silhouette of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline within the action row, right-aligned inside the form column. It does not affect vertical spacing of the form above but provides a clear endpoint for the user’s reading path. It scrolls with the card and does not become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation guard is mostly invisible but conceptually lives around the main preferences card, monitoring the form’s dirty state and user navigation. In its passive state, it has no visual footprint and does not affect spacing. When triggered (for example, if a user attempts to leave with unsaved changes), it invokes a modal overlay or a small confirmation dialog centered over the entire content column, dimming the watercolor header, nav pills, and preferences card beneath with a soft, semi-transparent lavender layer. This dialog is sized modestly (occupying about 30–40% of the viewport height and 40–50% of the width) and is centered both horizontally and vertically, keeping the user’s focus in the middle of the column. The underlying layout remains static while the guard is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no visible presence in the normal layout; when engaged, it behaves as a full-column modal overlay, fixed relative to the viewport, sitting above all other components (including loader and card) until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback banner appears as a rounded, pill-shaped message bar in the main content column, positioned between the breadcrumb pill and the top of the preferences card. It spans nearly the full width of the content column (about 90–100% of the card’s width), with matching left and right alignment to the card so they visually stack as related elements. The banner has a slightly higher-saturation background (green or purple for success, red for error) while still feeling soft and playful, and it contains a single line or two of text centered vertically within a height of roughly 48–56px. There is about 16–24px of lavender background above it (separating it from the breadcrumb) and another 16–24px below it (before the preferences card), making it feel like a distinct ribbon of feedback that sits just above the content it refers to. Optional small icons (checkmark, warning star, etc.) can appear on the left within the same pill strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in the normal vertical flow of the page. When not needed, it is completely absent, allowing the breadcrumb and preferences card to move closer together. It does not overlap any other components and scrolls naturally with the column content.

name: About
title: About
description: About page that loads and displays the site's philosophy, contact method, and disclaimers using getAboutPageContent, with optional deep-linking to specific sections.

core_features: Fetch and display About page content (title, main body text, contact email, and disclaimer) via getAboutPageContent.
Segment the fetched content into functional sections: general information, contact information, and disclaimers.
Provide a contact interaction that uses the dynamically returned contact_email (e.g., as a mailto link).
Provide a clear disclaimer section based on the disclaimer field returned from getAboutPageContent.
Support deep-link navigation to specific sections of the About page via URL parameters (e.g., about.html?section=disclaimer).
Provide navigation back to the homepage (index.html) via a 'Back to Home' link.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User opens about.html directly or via the footer 'About' link.
2. On initial page load, the About Page Data Loader component calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
3. When the response resolves, the About Page State Manager stores the returned object in page state (title, body, contact_email, disclaimer).
4. The Page Title component updates the document title using the returned title field.
5. The About Main Content Section parses the body field and renders the general information/philosophy content area.
6. The Disclaimer Section and Contact Section components receive the relevant parts of the fetched data and render them.

Use deep-link to a specific section:
1. User opens a link such as about.html?section=disclaimer.
2. On initial page load, the URL State Parser reads the section URL parameter.
3. The About Page Data Loader calls getAboutPageContent.
4. After data is loaded, the About Page State Manager sets the activeSection state from the section parameter.
5. The Section Navigation Highlighter marks the corresponding section (e.g., disclaimer) as active.
6. The Section Auto-Focus behavior scrolls or jumps to the active section.
7. If the user changes the section via internal section navigation links (e.g., clicking a link to the contact section), the URL State Manager updates the section query parameter and the activeSection state accordingly.

Contact the site owner via email:
1. After the About page loads content, the Contact Section component receives the contact_email value from getAboutPageContent.
2. The Contact Section renders an email interaction control (e.g., a mailto link) bound to the contact_email value.
3. The user clicks the email control.
4. The browser opens the user's default mail client using the dynamic email address; no data is submitted back to the site.
5. If contact_email is missing or empty in the response, the Contact Section shows a generic, non-email-specific fallback message based on state (without hardcoded contact details).

Review disclaimers for spiritual and predictive content:
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with ?section=disclaimer).
2. getAboutPageContent is called and returns a disclaimer field.
3. The Disclaimer Section component binds directly to the disclaimer field and renders it.
4. If the user navigates via an internal link or an external link specifying ?section=disclaimer, the URL State Parser and Section Auto-Focus scroll to or highlight the disclaimer area.
5. If disclaimer is missing from the response, the Disclaimer Section displays a controlled, generic fallback state indicating that no disclaimer text is currently available, without substituting any hardcoded disclaimer text.

Return to homepage:
1. User scrolls to the bottom of the About page.
2. User clicks the 'Back to Home' navigation link rendered by the Back to Home Link component.
3. Browser navigates to index.html without additional parameters (or with any parameters defined by the broader navigation strategy, if applicable).
interactions:
Initial data loading:
- Trigger: Page load event on about.html.
- Behavior: About Page Data Loader invokes getAboutPageContent with no arguments.
- Result: Page state updated with the returned title, body, contact_email, and disclaimer. Loading and error indicators update accordingly.

Deep-link section selection via URL:
- Trigger: Page load or URL change (e.g., navigation to about.html?section=contact or history manipulation within the page).
- Behavior: URL State Parser reads the section query parameter and updates activeSection state in the About Page State Manager.
- Result: Section Navigation Highlighter and Section Auto-Focus components adjust which section is considered active and ensure it is visible.

Internal section navigation clicks:
- Trigger: User clicks on internal section navigation links (e.g., 'Philosophy', 'Contact', 'Disclaimer') inside the About page.
- Behavior: Section Navigation component:
  - Calls the URL State Manager to set the section query parameter to the corresponding section key.
  - Updates activeSection in the page state.
- Result: The page scrolls/jumps to the selected section and visually marks it as active.

Contact email interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks on the contact email control in the Contact Section.
- Behavior: The contact email control constructs a mailto: URL using the contact_email value from getAboutPageContent and delegates to the browser to open the user's mail client.
- Result: User can compose an email to the dynamically provided contact_email address. If no contact_email is available, the control is disabled or hidden, and a non-interactive fallback message is rendered instead.

Retry after failed content load:
- Trigger: getAboutPageContent fails (e.g., network error), and the user clicks a 'Retry loading' control in the Error State component.
- Behavior: Error State component calls back into the About Page Data Loader to invoke getAboutPageContent again.
- Result: Page returns to a loading state and either recovers with valid data or remains in error if problems persist.

Back to Home navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Back to Home' link.
- Behavior: Navigation Link component requests navigation to index.html.
- Result: Browser navigates to the homepage, ending the About page session.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page state is derived from the getAboutPageContent response and the current URL parameters. On every load of about.html, the About Page Data Loader calls getAboutPageContent; there are no request parameters. The response object is stored in page state as aboutContent, with fields: title, body, contact_email, disclaimer. A separate view state object tracks: loadingStatus (one of 'idle', 'loading', 'loaded', 'error'), errorDetails (nullable), and activeSection (string or null). The activeSection property is controlled via the URL query parameter section, read and written through the URL State Parser/Manager. When the page loads, if section is present in the URL (e.g., about.html?section=disclaimer), the About Page State Manager sets activeSection to that value and triggers the Section Auto-Focus behavior. If section is absent or does not match a known section key, activeSection defaults to a predefined section identifier (e.g., the general information section), but this default is not hardcoded in content; it is structural to the UI only. Any internal section navigation updates the URL's section parameter via history.pushState or a full navigation (implementation-dependent) and keeps activeSection in sync, so reloading the page or sharing the URL preserves the currently focused section. No state is persisted in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies; the URL is the only persistent state mechanism. Error states do not alter URL parameters; they are purely in-memory. The document title is updated dynamically using aboutContent.title after successful loading, ensuring that the browser tab reflects the fetched About title rather than any static text.

components.functionality: On page initialization and on explicit retries, this component calls getAboutPageContent, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting aboutContent object (title, body, contact_email, disclaimer) to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: invokes getAboutPageContent, sets loadingStatus to 'loading', then to 'loaded' or 'error' based on the outcome.
onRetryClick: re-invokes getAboutPageContent after an error, resetting loadingStatus and errorDetails.

components.functionality: Reads and writes URL query parameters relevant to the About page, specifically the section parameter used to indicate the currently active section (e.g., general, contact, disclaimer). Keeps the activeSection state in sync with the URL.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: parses window.location.search for section and initializes viewState.activeSection.
onSectionChange: updates window.location.search (or history state) to reflect the new section value.
onPopState: listens for browser navigation events and re-parses the URL to update viewState.activeSection.

components.functionality: Centralizes view-related state for the About page, including loadingStatus, errorDetails, activeSection, and the aboutContent object returned by getAboutPageContent. Exposes derived state flags for child components (e.g., isLoading, hasError, isSectionActive).
data_binding: aboutContent: { title, body, contact_email, disclaimer } from getAboutPageContent
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: receives the response from about-page-data-loader and stores it in aboutContent; sets loadingStatus to 'loaded'.
onContentLoadError: stores error details, sets loadingStatus to 'error'.
onSectionParamParsed: updates activeSection based on url-state-parser.
onSectionRequested: updates activeSection when user clicks internal section navigation links and notifies url-state-parser.

components.functionality: Updates the browser's document title using the title field provided by getAboutPageContent so that the tab title reflects the dynamically loaded About page title.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: sets document.title to aboutContent.title when loadingStatus becomes 'loaded'.
onContentLoadError: optionally sets document.title to a generic error-related label without using any content-specific hardcoded text other than a structural indicator (e.g., 'Error').

components.functionality: Displays the main informational body of the About page (site purpose and philosophy) based on the body field from getAboutPageContent. This is treated as the general information section and is associated with a section key used for URL-based navigation.
event_handlers: onRender: shows a loading indicator when loadingStatus is 'loading', hides content until data is ready.
onActiveSectionChange: if the main section corresponds to the current activeSection, applies active styling and triggers scroll-into-view.
onContentLoaded: renders aboutContent.body once loadingStatus is 'loaded' and aboutContent.body is available.

components.functionality: Displays the disclaimer content explaining limitations and policies around spiritual and predictive content, using the disclaimer field from getAboutPageContent. It behaves as a distinct section controllable via the section URL parameter.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: renders aboutContent.disclaimer when data is available; if disclaimer is missing, renders a controlled, data-absent fallback state without inserting any hardcoded disclaimer text.
onActiveSectionChange: if activeSection matches this section's identifier, applies active styling and scrolls/focuses this block.
onSectionLinkClick: when a user selects this section via internal navigation, notifies about-page-state-manager to set activeSection accordingly.

components.functionality: Presents the site's contact method using the contact_email value from getAboutPageContent. Renders an email interaction control (e.g., mailto link) only when contact_email is present.
event_handlers: onContentLoaded: checks aboutContent.contact_email; if it exists, renders an interactive control bound to it; otherwise shows a non-interactive fallback message without any hardcoded email.
onEmailClick: constructs and opens a mailto URL using aboutContent.contact_email; if not available, prevents default interaction.
onActiveSectionChange: if activeSection corresponds to the contact section, ensures it is visible and marked active.
onSectionLinkClick: when the contact section is selected from an internal navigation element, informs about-page-state-manager to update activeSection.

components.functionality: Provides internal navigation controls to jump between sections of the About page (e.g., general information, contact, disclaimers). It synchronizes section selection with URL parameters.
event_handlers: onNavItemClick: when a user clicks a section link, calls about-page-state-manager.onSectionRequested with the selected section key, which in turn updates activeSection and notifies url-state-parser to update the section query parameter.
onActiveSectionChange: updates the visual state of navigation items to indicate which section is active.

components.functionality: Displays loading indicators while getAboutPageContent is in progress and shows an error message with a retry control if loading fails.
event_handlers: onLoadingStatusChange: when loadingStatus is 'loading', displays a non-specific loading indicator; when 'loaded', hides itself; when 'error', shows an error UI.
onRetryClick: emits a retry event to about-page-data-loader to call getAboutPageContent again.

components.functionality: Provides a navigation control that returns the user to the homepage (index.html) when clicked, as defined in the outgoing navigation for this page.
event_handlers: onClick: navigates the browser to index.html according to the site's routing strategy (e.g., standard hyperlink navigation).

layout.grid_system: Single centered column with a loose underlying grid approximating a 12-column layout, constrained to a fixed max-width content area within full-width background sections.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a single centered column with stacked, full-width bands of content against a lavender page background, mirroring the blog mockup. At the very top (outside these components) sits the watercolor illustration header. Directly beneath it, the About page introduces a rounded, pill-shaped section navigation bar (about-section-navigation), followed by a thinner breadcrumb-style back link (back-to-home-link). Below these navigation bands, a single large article card (about-main-content-section) fills the remaining vertical space. Inside that card, the About content is arranged in a classic vertical reading order: introductory philosophy at the top, then contact information, then disclaimers, each as clearly separated subsections. Loading and error states occupy this same article-card zone when data is unavailable, replacing the card content while preserving the macro layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a single centered column with stacked, full-width bands of content against a lavender page background, mirroring the blog mockup. At the very top (outside these components) sits the watercolor illustration header. Directly beneath it, the About page introduces a rounded, pill-shaped section navigation bar (about-section-navigation), followed by a thinner breadcrumb-style back link (back-to-home-link). Below these navigation bands, a single large article card (about-main-content-section) fills the remaining vertical space. Inside that card, the About content is arranged in a classic vertical reading order: introductory philosophy at the top, then contact information, then disclaimers, each as clearly separated subsections. Loading and error states occupy this same article-card zone when data is unavailable, replacing the card content while preserving the macro layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The watercolor header at the top occupies roughly the upper 25–30% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the rounded section navigation pill and the back-to-home breadcrumb pill each occupy around 8–10% of the viewport height, with 16–24px vertical gaps between them and the header. The main article card begins roughly one-third of the way down the viewport and extends downward, taking up the remaining 50–60% of the viewport and then scrolling as needed. The article card spans the full width of the central content column (approx. 80–90% of the page width on desktop, constrained by a fixed max-width), with generous inner padding of 16–24px on all sides. Within the article card, the philosophy section takes the upper portion (around 40–50% of the card content), the contact section is a shorter middle block, and the disclaimer section is a denser, lower block that may extend the card height further due to text length.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout balances airy macro spacing with moderate internal density. Large gaps (16–24px) separate the header, navigation pill, breadcrumb/back link, and the main article card, allowing plenty of lavender background to show through. Inside the navigation pill, section links are spaced evenly with comfortable padding so they feel tappable and not crowded. Within the article card, body text uses standard line height and paragraph spacing (roughly 1.4–1.6 line-height with 8–16px between paragraphs), giving a readable but not sparse feel. Section headings (e.g., Philosophy, Contact, Disclaimer) have extra top and bottom spacing (16–24px) to stand out, while subtext and disclaimers are slightly more compact but still readable. Feedback states reuse the article card’s padding and spacing rules to stay consistent.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is first drawn to the colorful watercolor header and site logo at the top center-right. From there, it moves downward to the bold, horizontally centered section navigation pill (about-section-navigation), which visually anchors the About page and offers quick links to its sections. Just below, the eye catches the smaller, left-weighted back-to-home breadcrumb pill (back-to-home-link), reinforcing context. The gaze then drops into the large, rounded article card where the purple About title and the Philosophy content appear first, followed by the Contact details and finally the disclaimer text. If deep-linked, the page scrolls so the relevant subsection heading is near the top of the card while the navigation pill remains visible (via sticky behavior), keeping orientation. Loading or error states briefly interrupt the flow by occupying the article-card area, but maintain the same centered card placement and rounded styling so the overall visual rhythm remains intact.

layout.overall_description: The About page sits within a whimsical, astrology-themed shell: a full-width lavender background and a watercolor illustration header across the top. Centered beneath this header, a rounded, mint or pale-cream navigation pill with playful typography spans the main content column, offering anchor links to Philosophy, Contact, and Disclaimer. A short distance below, a slimmer pill acts as a breadcrumb-like back-to-home link, aligned toward the left edge of the column but still inside the same rounded, card-like language. Beneath these two navigational bands, a single, large, softly rounded article card floats in the center of the page. It has a light background, substantial padding, and a slightly retro, handcrafted feel. Inside this card, the About story unfolds from top to bottom: a warm philosophical introduction at the top, followed by a clearly separated contact block, and finally a distinct disclaimer block styled to feel important but not harsh. Between each of these internal sections there is enough space to breathe, but not so much that the card feels fragmented. As the user scrolls, the navigation pill gently sticks near the top of the viewport so the user can jump between sections, while the card content glides underneath. Invisible data and state managers sit behind the scenes, orchestrating which section is in view and how deep-links behave, but the visible experience is a friendly, vertically flowing, single-column reading journey framed by soft shapes and pastel tones.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and lives at the very top of the About page’s component tree, conceptually positioned just beneath the global page chrome (header and illustration). It occupies no visible space and does not affect the flow of the layout; instead, it controls when the main article card can render based on data from getAboutPageContent. In terms of the visual layout, you can imagine it as an invisible layer sitting above the article card area: when data is loading or unavailable, it signals the about-loading-and-error-state component to occupy the central article-card slot, leaving the header and navigation pills intact above it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height component mounted near the top of the About page’s DOM tree; it does not influence spatial arrangement but gates whether the main article content can appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state parser is also invisible and does not render any UI. Conceptually, it sits alongside the data loader at the top of the page structure, listening for deep-link parameters or hash fragments that reference specific About sections (e.g., #philosophy, #contact, #disclaimer). It coordinates with the section-navigation and the internal anchors within the main article card to ensure that, when a deep-link is present, the page scrolls so that the corresponding subsection heading is positioned near the top of the visible article card, just beneath the sticky area reserved for the header and navigation pill.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-width/height component; it influences scroll behavior and which section is brought into view, but does not occupy any spatial region itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state manager is an invisible structural component that coordinates which section is active, which navigation item is highlighted, and whether the page is in loading, error, or ready state. It can be thought of as a hidden controller layered behind the navigation pill and the article card. As the user scrolls down the vertical flow from Philosophy to Contact to Disclaimer, this manager updates the active state, allowing the about-section-navigation pill to visually indicate the current section without altering the physical layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height component; it has no direct spatial footprint and simply orchestrates state changes that affect styling and highlighting in visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page title updater modifies the browser tab title and metadata but does not render on the screen. From a layout perspective, it is effectively a background process that runs when the About page mounts and when its content changes. It does not occupy any width or height, nor does it change the flow of the header, navigation pills, or article card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height/headless behavior; it exists purely for document title updates and has no spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main content section is the primary article card for the About page. It is centered within the single-column layout and sits below the watercolor header, the section-navigation pill, and the back-to-home breadcrumb pill. It starts roughly one-third down the initial viewport, with a vertical gap of about 24px beneath the breadcrumb pill. The card spans the full width of the content column (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop, capped at a comfortable max-width around 720–800px), leaving ample lavender background visible to the left and right. The card has generous 16–24px padding on all sides, a soft, rounded corner radius (16–24px), and a light interior background (e.g., off-white or very pale mint) that contrasts against the lavender page background. At the top inside this card, the About page title and the Philosophy content appear first, with the title in a prominent purple or green accent color. Below that, there is a clear vertical sequence: Philosophy content near the top, followed by a moderate gap (16–24px) leading into the about-contact-section block, and then another gap leading into the about-disclaimer-section block. The overall height of this card grows with content and usually fills the remainder of the viewport and beyond, making the page scrollable while keeping the card visually cohesive as a single, large container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable block-level element centered within the main column; it does not stick or float, but its top edge is positioned just below the navigation and breadcrumb bands.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The disclaimer section is a distinct, lower subsection within the main article card rather than a separate outer card. It is positioned toward the bottom of the about-main-content-section, appearing after the Philosophy and Contact blocks. There is a noticeable vertical gap (about 16–24px) above it to separate it from the contact information. Within the card’s inner width, the disclaimer section runs nearly full width (minus the card padding), forming a text block that may be slightly denser in content. Its heading (e.g., "Disclaimer") is set in the same whimsical but legible type as other headings, often in a strong accent color (perhaps red or deep purple) to convey importance, with extra spacing above (16–24px) to visually anchor it. The disclaimer copy is arranged in paragraphs or short bullet points with standard paragraph spacing (8–16px), ensuring readability while accepting slightly higher text density than the philosophy content. This section occupies the bottom part of the card, so as the user scrolls to the end of the About page, this text is the last thing they read.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block-level subsection inside the main article card; it scrolls with the card and does not have independent sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact section sits inside the about-main-content-section card, visually positioned between the Philosophy content and the Disclaimer section. It begins after a comfortable vertical gap (about 16–24px) below the philosophy text. Within the card, it uses the same inner width, aligned to the left text edge with a heading like "Contact" or "Reach Out" in a friendly accent color (e.g., mint green or bright purple) to stand out. Below the heading, one or more lines describe how to get in touch (e.g., an email address or form link), arranged in a compact but readable block, possibly with subtle iconography (like a small envelope) aligned left. The overall height of the contact section is shorter than the philosophy and disclaimer sections, making it feel like a concise middle segment. Extra space (16–24px) at its bottom separates it from the disclaimer, keeping the sections clearly distinguishable within the single card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level subsection inside the main article card; it scrolls with the card content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation appears as a prominent, rounded pill-shaped bar positioned directly beneath the watercolor header and any global site header elements. It is centered within the single-column layout and spans almost the full width of the content column (about 80–90% of page width up to the column’s max-width), with equal lavender background showing on both sides. The pill has a pastel mint or light cream background with a slight shadow or outline, and 16–24px of vertical padding inside. Navigation items (e.g., Philosophy, Contact, Disclaimer) are arranged horizontally on desktop, evenly spaced across the width, with each item styled as a pill-like clickable label, leaving at least 16px of horizontal padding around the text so it feels open and tappable. On smaller screens, the items can wrap or stack while preserving the centered, pill aesthetic. There is a vertical gap of about 24px between the bottom of the watercolor header and the top of this navigation pill, and another 16–24px gap below it before the back-to-home breadcrumb pill. When the user scrolls past the initial header region, this navigation pill becomes sticky and pins itself near the top of the viewport (just below any global browser/header chrome), maintaining a small offset (8–16px) from the top so it doesn’t feel cramped. This allows users to quickly jump between About sections at any point in their reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially positioned statically beneath the header; once scrolled past, it switches to a sticky behavior, remaining fixed at the top of the viewport within the column while the article card content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error state component occupies the same spatial region as the main article card, ensuring that the macro layout—header, navigation pill, breadcrumb pill above and lavender background around—remains unchanged. When active, it appears centered within the content column, starting at roughly the same vertical position as the about-main-content-section would: about 16–24px below the back-to-home breadcrumb pill. It presents as a rounded card with identical width, padding, and corner radius to the article card (spanning 80–90% of the page width within the column). In a loading state, this card might show playful skeleton lines or a short, friendly "peeking stars" animation aligned left within the card, with generous padding above and below. In an error state, it displays a concise, clearly readable message centered or left-aligned within the card, perhaps with a small illustrated icon above the text. The height of this card can be shorter than the fully loaded article card, but it should still feel substantial enough (at least 30–40% of the viewport height) to anchor the user’s attention in the same central area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the main article content: it appears in place of the article card as a standard centered block-level element, scrolling normally with the page and not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home link functions as a breadcrumb-like element directly beneath the section navigation pill, echoing the second horizontal band seen in the mockup. It is aligned within the same centered content column but visually anchored toward the left, providing a subtle directional cue. Spatially, it sits about 16–24px below the navigation pill and about 16–24px above the top edge of the main article card. In terms of width, it spans most of the column (roughly 70–90% of the column width) as a slim, rounded pill or strip with a soft pastel background slightly lighter or darker than the navigation pill to distinguish it. Inside, a left-pointing arrow icon and the "Back to Home" text are aligned to the left with 12–16px of horizontal padding, leaving generous empty space to the right so it feels light and unobtrusive. Vertical padding inside the pill is modest (8–12px), making it noticeably slimmer than the section-navigation pill. This band reinforces the page’s context while keeping the eye moving downward toward the large article card below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element within the column; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page and does not have sticky or fixed behavior.
